topics.title: Introduction to Product Monitoring and Process Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT753 - FOOD PRODUCTS MONITORING & CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Product Monitoring and Process Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Product Monitoring** is the systematic, continuous observation and measurement of critical quality, safety, and process parameters of a food product — both *in-process* (during manufacturing) and *post-process* (finished goods) — using instruments, sensors, and statistical tools, to verify conformance with predefined specifications and regulatory limits.

**Process Control** is the engineering discipline of automatically or manually manipulating the operating variables of a food manufacturing process (temperature, pressure, flow rate, pH, moisture, etc.) to maintain them within desired set-points, ensuring reproducibility, safety, and product consistency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Scope)**
> *Product Monitoring and Process Control* encompasses the measurement, recording, regulation, and optimization of physical, chemical, biological, and microbiological parameters throughout the food production chain — from raw material reception to packaged product dispatch — using instrumentation, automation, and statistical quality tools.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Driving a Car on a Highway**
>
> Imagine you are driving a car at a constant speed of **80 km/h** on a busy highway.
>
> - Your **eyes** (sensors) constantly monitor the speedometer (process variable).
> - Your **brain** (controller) compares the observed speed to **80 km/h** (set-point).
> - If a slope makes the car slow to **72 km/h**, your brain signals your **foot on the accelerator** (final control element) to press harder.
> - This continuous *sensing → comparing → correcting* cycle is **Process Control**.
> - The act of *watching* the speedometer, fuel gauge, and engine temperature is **Product (Process) Monitoring**.
>
> In a **food factory**, the "car" is the milk pasteurizer, bakery oven, or retort sterilizer; the "speed" is the process temperature, flow, or moisture content; and the "driver" is the **PLC / DCS / SCADA control system**.

---

### 1.3 Why Does This Matter in the Food Industry?

| Reason | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| **Consumer Safety** | Prevents microbial hazards (*Salmonella*, *Listeria*, *E. coli*) |
| **Regulatory Compliance** | FSSAI (India), FDA (USA), EFSA (EU), Codex Alimentarius mandates |
| **Shelf-Life Assurance** | Water activity ($a_w$), moisture, oxygen control |
| **Brand Reputation** | Consistent taste, texture, color across batches |
| **Yield Optimization** | Reduces raw material waste, energy consumption |
| **Traceability** | Real-time data for recall management (FSMA 204 compliance) |

---

### 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **Process Variable (PV)** — the measured parameter (e.g., temperature **°C**, pressure **bar**, flow **m³/h**).
>
> **Set-Point (SP)** — the desired target value of the PV.
>
> **Error Signal (e)** — the difference $e(t) = SP - PV$.
>
> **Manipulated Variable (MV)** — the input adjusted by the controller to correct error.
>
> **Disturbance (D)** — any unwanted external influence (raw material variation, ambient humidity).
>
> **Sensor** — device that converts a physical/chemical quantity into a measurable signal.
>
> **Transducer** — broader term; converts one form of energy to another.
>
> **Actuator** — device that physically changes the MV (valve, motor, heater).

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Standard reference temperature for food microbiology: 0 °C to 4 °C (refrigeration)**
- **Pasteurization reference: 72 °C for 15 s (HTST) / 63 °C for 30 min (LTLT)**
- **Sterilization reference: 121 °C at 15 psi for 15 min (autoclave)**
- **Water activity safety threshold: $a_w < 0.60$ for shelf-stable foods**
- **Standard atmosphere: 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar**
- **pH neutrality reference: pH 7.0 at 25 °C**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Process Control Systems

Process control architectures in food engineering can be organized along a clear hierarchy of increasing automation:

#### 2.1.1 Manual (Open-Loop) Control
- No feedback from the output to the operator.
- Human reads the indicator, decides, and adjusts manually.
- **Disadvantage:** Slow, error-prone, no self-correction.
- **Example:** A cook adjusting the gas stove flame by watching boiling intensity.

#### 2.1.2 Automatic (Closed-Loop) Control
- A sensor measures PV → transmits to controller → controller compares with SP → drives actuator → PV changes → sensor measures again.
- **Example:** A thermostat in a milk chilling tank.

#### 2.1.3 Feedback Control
- Output measurement is *fed back* to the input side to reduce error.
- **Error equation:** $e(t) = SP(t) - PV(t)$

#### 2.1.4 Feed-Forward Control
- Measures the *disturbance* before it disturbs the process and pre-compensates.
- **Advantage:** Acts proactively, not reactively.
- **Example:** Adjusting a heat exchanger's steam supply when incoming cold milk flow surges.

#### 2.1.5 Cascade Control
- Two nested loops: an *outer (master/slow)* loop controls the primary variable; an *inner (slave/fast)* loop controls a secondary variable.
- **Example:** Jacket temperature (outer) → Steam valve position (inner) in a jacketed cooking kettle.

#### 2.1.6 Ratio Control
- Maintains a fixed proportion between two flow rates.
- **Example:** Ingredient A flow : Ingredient B flow = 1 : 4 in a beverage blender.

#### 2.1.7 Adaptive / Intelligent Control
- Controller parameters (P, I, D) auto-tune based on process dynamics.
- Uses **AI, Fuzzy Logic, or Model Predictive Control (MPC)**.

---

### 2.2 Components of a Closed-Loop Control System

A complete instrumentation loop in a food plant contains four functional blocks:

1. **Sensor / Primary Element** — senses PV (RTD for temperature, orifice plate for flow, load cell for mass).
2. **Transmitter** — standardizes the signal to **4–20 mA current loop** (industry standard) or digital (**HART, Foundation Fieldbus, Profibus, IO-Link**).
3. **Controller** — computes the corrective action (PLC, DCS, dedicated PID controller).
4. **Final Control Element (Actuator)** — physically adjusts the MV (pneumatic valve, variable-frequency drive, solenoid).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question Pattern:** *Draw and label the block diagram of a closed-loop process control system.* Memorize the four blocks and signal flow direction.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **examinable in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark)** numerical problems.

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Variable Definitions | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Error Signal | $e(t) = SP(t) - PV(t)$ | SP = set-point, PV = process variable | Foundation of all feedback loops |
| 2 | Controller Output (PID) | $u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I}\int e(t)\,dt + \tau_D \dfrac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$ | $K_c$ = proportional gain, $\tau_I$ = integral time, $\tau_D$ = derivative time | Universal control law |
| 3 | Proportional Band | $PB(\%) = \dfrac{100}{K_c}$ | $K_c$ = gain | Tuning metric |
| 4 | First-Order Process Response | $PV(t) = PV_\infty \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | $\tau$ = time constant, $PV_\infty$ = final value | Thermal lag in heating/cooling |
| 5 | Steady-State Offset | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{D}{1 + K_c K_p}$ | $D$ = disturbance, $K_p$ = process gain | Quantifies P-only error |
| 6 | Reynolds Number (flow regime) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | $\rho$ = density, $v$ = velocity, $D$ = diameter, $\mu$ = viscosity | Pipe flow regime in food lines |
| 7 | Heat Transfer (Pasteurizer) | $Q = \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | $\dot{m}$ = mass flow, $c_p$ = specific heat, $\Delta T$ = temperature rise | Energy balance for thermal process control |
| 8 | F-value (sterilization lethality) | $F = \int 10^{(T(t) - T_{ref})/z} dt$ | $T$ = temperature, $z$ = z-value, $T_{ref}$ = reference | Thermal process validation |
| 9 | $D$-value (decimal reduction) | $\dfrac{N}{N_0} = 10^{-t/D}$ | $N$ = survivors, $D$ = D-value, $t$ = time | Microbial inactivation kinetics |
| 10 | Water Activity | $a_w = \dfrac{p}{p_0}$ | $p$ = partial pressure of water in food, $p_0$ = pure water pressure at same $T$ | Shelf-life prediction |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dairy Plants:** PLC-based HTST pasteurizer controllers regulate **72 °C ± 0.5 °C**.
- **Baking Industry:** Oven profile controllers follow multi-stage ramp-soak recipes (proofing → baking → browning).
- **Beverage Industry:** Mass-flow controllers dose syrup, water, and CO₂ within **± 0.1 %** of target.
- **Meat Processing:** Retort controllers track $F_0$ value ≥ **3.0 minutes** for *Clostridium botulinum* safety.
- **Cold Chain:** Wireless IoT temperature loggers (Bluetooth / LoRaWAN) monitor $-18\,°C$ frozen storage.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the PID Controller Equation (From First Principles)

The **Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID)** controller is the workhorse of food process automation. We derive it term-by-term.

---

#### Step 1: Define the Error Signal

At any instant $t$, the controller measures the error between set-point and process variable:

$$e(t) = SP(t) - PV(t)$$

> **Logic:** This is the *driving force* of the entire control action. If the error is zero, no correction is needed.

---

#### Step 2: Proportional (P) Action

A proportional controller produces an output *directly proportional* to the current error:

$$u_P(t) = K_c \cdot e(t)$$

- $K_c$ = controller gain (dimensionless).
- A higher $K_c$ → faster response but risk of **oscillation**.
- A lower $K_c$ → stable but **sluggish** with **steady-state offset**.

---

#### Step 3: Integral (I) Action — Elimination of Offset

The integral term sums up *all past errors*, ensuring the controller will continue to act as long as any residual error exists:

$$u_I(t) = \frac{K_c}{\tau_I} \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau$$

- $\tau_I$ = integral (reset) time, in seconds or minutes.
- **Purpose:** Drive the steady-state error to **zero**.
- **Drawback:** Can cause **integral wind-up** if the actuator saturates.

---

#### Step 4: Derivative (D) Action — Anticipating the Future

The derivative term responds to the *rate of change* of error, anticipating where the process is heading:

$$u_D(t) = K_c \cdot \tau_D \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

- $\tau_D$ = derivative (rate) time.
- **Purpose:** Provides **damping**, reduces **overshoot**, improves stability.
- **Drawback:** Amplifies high-frequency noise (e.g., sensor chatter).

---

#### Step 5: Combine into the Universal PID Law

Summing the three contributions:

$$u(t) = K_c \, e(t) + \frac{K_c}{\tau_I}\int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_c\,\tau_D\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

This can be written in compact form:

$$u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau + \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked "state the PID equation," always include the explicit $K_c$ factor outside the bracket. Examiners mark strictly for that.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Steady-State Offset in a P-Only Controller

Consider a first-order process with transfer function $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau s + 1}$ and a proportional controller $G_c(s) = K_c$.

The closed-loop error $e_{ss}$ for a unit step disturbance $D(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$ is given by the **Final Value Theorem**:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s)$$

The error transfer function for disturbance input is:

$$E(s) = \frac{D(s)}{1 + G_c(s)G_p(s)}$$

Substituting:

$$E(s) = \frac{\frac{1}{s}}{1 + K_c \cdot \frac{K_p}{\tau s + 1}}$$

$$E(s) = \frac{\tau s + 1}{s\,(\tau s + 1 + K_c K_p)}$$

Applying the Final Value Theorem:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{\tau s + 1}{s\,(\tau s + 1 + K_c K_p)}$$

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{\tau s + 1}{\tau s + 1 + K_c K_p}$$

$$e_{ss} = \frac{0 + 1}{0 + 1 + K_c K_p} = \frac{1}{1 + K_c K_p}$$

> **Conclusion:** A P-only controller **never eliminates the error** completely. To make $e_{ss} \to 0$, we **must add Integral action**, which is why PID is preferred in food plants.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: First-Order Process Time Constant

For a sudden step change $\Delta u$ in the manipulated variable, the process variable evolves as:

$$\tau \frac{dPV}{dt} + PV(t) = K_p \,\Delta u$$

This is a linear ODE. With $PV(0) = PV_0$:

$$PV(t) = PV_\infty + (PV_0 - PV_\infty)\, e^{-t/\tau}$$

where $PV_\infty = K_p \Delta u + PV_0$.

> **Physical meaning:** $\tau$ is the time to reach **63.2 %** of the final value. After $4\tau$, the process is considered to be at **98.2 %** of steady state.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Closed-Loop Process Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSP[Set Point SP] --> nodeSum1((Comparator Summing Junction))
    nodeDist[Disturbance D] -.->.-> nodeProcess[Process Plant PV]
    nodeSum1 --> nodeController[Controller PID]
    nodeController --> nodeActuator[Actuator Final Control Element]
    nodeActuator --> nodeProcess
    nodeProcess --> nodeSensor[Sensor Transmitter]
    nodeSensor --> nodeSum1
    nodeProcess -.-> nodeMonitor[Data Acquisition SCADA HMI]
    nodeMonitor -.-> nodeAlarm[Alarm and Logging System]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - **Solid arrows** = signal/power flow.
> - **Dotted arrows** = disturbance/observation paths.
> - **nodeSum1** is the comparator where `SP - PV` is computed.
> - **nodeProcess** is the actual food processing unit (e.g., pasteurizer, retort, oven).
> - **nodeMonitor** is the SCADA/HMI computer that logs trends and triggers alarms.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Cascade Control Architecture (Milk Pasteurizer Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeSP2[Set Point Outlet Temperature] --> nodeOuterC[Outer Temperature Controller]
    nodeOuterC --> nodeSP1[Set Point Steam Valve Position]
    nodeSP1 --> nodeInnerC[Inner Flow Controller]
    nodeInnerC --> nodeValve[Steam Control Valve]
    nodeValve --> nodeHeater[Heater Heat Exchanger]
    nodeHeater --> nodeOutT[Outlet Temperature]
    nodeHeater --> nodeSteamF[Steam Flow Rate]
    nodeOutT --> nodeOuterC
    nodeSteamF --> nodeInnerC
```

> **Explanation:** The **outer loop** (slow) corrects the *milk outlet temperature*; the **inner loop** (fast) regulates the *steam flow*. Two controllers, two PVs, one actuator manipulated indirectly.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Product Monitoring Hierarchy (Plant-Wide View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeL0[Field Level: Sensors RTD Pressure Transmitter Load Cell pH Probe]
    nodeL1[Control Level: PLC DCS Controller PID]
    nodeL2[Supervisory Level: SCADA HMI Trend Displays Alarm Logs]
    nodeL3[Management Level: ERP MES Quality Reports Regulatory Compliance]
    nodeL0 --> nodeL1
    nodeL1 --> nodeL2
    nodeL2 --> nodeL3
    nodeL3 -.-> nodeL1
```

> **KTU Recall Point:** This **four-level ISA-95 / Purdue model** is the standard reference architecture for modern food factories and is frequently asked in KTU Module 1.

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Feedback vs Feed-Forward Disturbance Rejection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Feedback [A Feedback Control Loop]
        F_SP[SP] --> F_C[Controller]
        F_C --> F_A[Actuator]
        F_A --> F_P[Process]
        F_P --> F_S[Sensor]
        F_S --> F_C
        F_D[Disturbance] -.->.-> F_P
    end

    subgraph FeedForward [B Feed Forward Control Loop]
        FF_SD[Disturbance Sensor] --> FF_C[Feed Forward Controller]
        FF_C --> FF_A[Actuator]
        FF_A --> FF_P[Process]
        FF_D2[Disturbance] --> FF_SD
        FF_D2 -.->.-> FF_P
    end
```

> **Key Insight:** Feedback waits for the error to appear; feed-forward anticipates the disturbance *before* it impacts the PV. Industrial food processes often **combine both** in a hybrid configuration.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question] (3 Marks)**
*Define Process Control. Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop control systems with one food industry example each.*

**Model Answer:**

**Process Control:** Process control is the engineering activity of maintaining the operating variables of a manufacturing process at desired values by automatic or manual manipulation, using sensors, controllers, and actuators.

| Feature | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | Absent | Present |
| Accuracy | Low; depends on calibration | High; self-correcting |
| Disturbance handling | Cannot reject | Rejects disturbances |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Food example | A hand-operated gas stove | An HTST pasteurizer with PID temperature controller |

*(3 marks awarded for definition + correct tabular differentiation + valid example.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question] (3 Marks)**
*List the four basic components of a measurement and control loop. State the standard current signal used in industrial transmitters.*

**Model Answer:**

The four basic components are:

1. **Sensor (Primary Element)** — senses the process variable (e.g., Pt100 RTD for temperature).
2. **Transmitter** — converts the sensor signal into a standardized form.
3. **Controller** — computes the corrective action (PLC, DCS, PID).
4. **Final Control Element (Actuator)** — physically adjusts the process (control valve, VFD).

The standard industrial current signal is **4–20 mA DC**, where 4 mA = 0 % of range and 20 mA = 100 % of range. (Live-zero principle: 4 mA, not 0 mA, allows detection of open-circuit faults.)

---

### PART B — Full-Length Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Module question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

## ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the components and working of a closed-loop process control system. State the function of each block.

**Model Solution:**

A closed-loop (feedback) process control system continuously measures the output and feeds it back to the controller for comparison with the desired set-point. The standard block diagram contains the following four functional blocks:

| Block | Function | Food Plant Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Sensor (Primary Element) | Detects the process variable | Pt100 RTD in milk pasteurizer |
| 2. Transmitter | Standardises signal (4–20 mA) | Temperature transmitter in a hot-oil fryer |
| 3. Controller | Computes error and corrective action | PID controller in PLC |
| 4. Final Control Element | Adjusts the manipulated variable | Pneumatic steam valve |

**Working principle:**
1. The set-point (SP) is entered by the operator (e.g., **72 °C** for HTST pasteurization).
2. The sensor measures the actual process variable (PV).
3. The comparator computes the error: $e(t) = SP - PV$.
4. The controller (PID) computes the corrective signal $u(t)$ using the PID law.
5. The actuator (valve) modulates steam flow to bring PV → SP.
6. The cycle repeats continuously, ensuring self-correction against disturbances such as raw milk temperature variation.

**Block Diagram (to be drawn in exam):**

```
   SP ──►(+)──► e(t) ──► [PID Controller] ──► [Actuator] ──► [Process] ──► PV
            ▲                                                     │
            │                                                     │
            └───────────────── [Sensor] ◄────────────────────────┘
```

*(Examiner's mark split: 2 marks for block diagram, 3 marks for component explanation, 2 marks for working narrative.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the steady-state error of a proportional controller applied to a first-order process subjected to a unit-step disturbance. State the engineering implication of the result.

**Model Solution:**

Given: Process $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau s + 1}$, Controller $G_c(s) = K_c$, Disturbance $D(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$.

**Step 1:** Closed-loop error transfer function under disturbance:

$$E(s) = \frac{D(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)} = \frac{\frac{1}{s}}{1 + K_c \cdot \frac{K_p}{\tau s + 1}}$$

**Step 2:** Simplify:

$$E(s) = \frac{\tau s + 1}{s\,(\tau s + 1 + K_c K_p)}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the Final Value Theorem:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{\tau s + 1}{\tau s + 1 + K_c K_p} = \frac{1}{1 + K_c K_p}$$

**Step 4:** Result:

$$\boxed{\,e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K_c K_p}\,}$$

**Engineering Implication:**

- A proportional controller **alone** cannot eliminate the steady-state error; a residual offset always remains.
- Increasing $K_c$ reduces the offset, but excessive gain causes oscillation and instability.
- Therefore, in food plants (e.g., maintaining pasteurization temperature at **72 °C ± 0.5 °C**), the **Integral (I) action must be added** to drive the offset to zero.

*(Examiner's mark split: 2 marks for error transfer function, 2 marks for FVT, 1 mark for final equation, 2 marks for engineering interpretation.)*

---

## ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the difference between feedback and feed-forward control strategies. Provide one food industry example of each.

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Feedback Control | Feed-Forward Control |
|---|---|---|
| Measured variable | Output (PV) | Disturbance (D) |
| Action timing | Reactive (after error occurs) | Proactive (before error occurs) |
| Compensation | Based on current error | Based on modelled effect of D |
| Requirement | Sensor on PV only | Sensor on disturbance + process model |
| Limitation | Cannot anticipate disturbances | Sensitive to model inaccuracy |

**Example 1 — Feedback (Dairy):**
The HTST pasteurizer outlet temperature is measured by an RTD, compared with **72 °C** set-point, and the steam valve is adjusted. If milk flow surges and temperature drops to **70.5 °C**, the controller reacts and re-opens the valve.

**Example 2 — Feed-Forward (Bakery):**
An oven controller measures the *dough moisture* and *ambient humidity* upstream and pre-adjusts the baking zone temperature profile so that the crumb moisture reaches the target regardless of variation in the raw dough.

**Example 3 — Hybrid (Brewery):**
In a wort kettle, a feed-forward signal from the *mash flow* sensor adjusts the steam supply, while a feedback loop on the *wort temperature* fine-tunes the final value.

*(Examiner's mark split: 2 marks tabular comparison, 2 marks feedback example, 2 marks feed-forward example, 1 mark hybrid note.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, describe the four-level ISA-95 / Purdue model of plant automation as applied to a packaged drinking water bottling plant.

**Model Solution:**

The **Purdue Reference Model (ISA-95)** stratifies industrial automation into four hierarchical levels:

**Level 0 — Field (Process) Level:**
- Sensors: flow meters, conductivity sensors, turbidity probes, level switches, temperature RTDs.
- Actuators: solenoid valves, centrifugal pump VFDs, CIP chemical dosing pumps.

**Level 1 — Control Level:**
- PLCs and DCS controllers execute PID loops (e.g., maintaining ozonation chamber at **0.4 mg/L** residual).
- HMIs display trends locally.

**Level 2 — Supervisory Level:**
- SCADA stations aggregate data, generate batch reports, manage alarms.
- Stores data for FSSAI traceability (batch → bottle → distributor).

**Level 3 — Management (ERP / MES) Level:**
- Enterprise Resource Planning integrates with production planning, inventory, sales, and quality dashboards.
- Issues regulatory compliance reports (FSSAI annual returns).

**Block Diagram (to be drawn):**

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Level 3: ERP / MES (Business Planning)   │
├──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Level 2: SCADA (Supervisory Control)     │
├──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Level 1: PLC / DCS (Direct Control)      │
├──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Level 0: Sensors + Actuators (Field)     │
└──────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Engineering rationale:** Information flows *upward* (data), and control commands flow *downward* (set-points). The model decouples fast real-time control (Levels 0–1) from slow business logic (Level 3).

*(Examiner's mark split: 1 mark per level × 4 = 4 marks, 2 marks for diagram, 1 mark for data flow commentary.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the $K_c$ factor** outside the PID bracket — you will lose **1 mark** instantly.
> 2. **Drawing sensor output arrow to the comparator** (not to the process) — this is the *most common* wiring error in block diagrams; you will lose **2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing sensor and transmitter** — sensors *sense*, transmitters *standardize*. Examiners treat them as distinct.
> 4. **Not stating engineering implications** after derivations — a 14-mark question without a 1–2 line interpretation loses a half-mark.
> 5. **Using $|$ inside the markdown formula table** — this breaks the table. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX instead.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **Product Monitoring** = observing; **Process Control** = correcting.
- ✅ A closed-loop has 4 blocks: **Sensor → Transmitter → Controller → Actuator**.
- ✅ **4–20 mA** is the standard industrial signal; **HART / Fieldbus** are digital upgrades.
- ✅ **Error signal:** $e(t) = SP - PV$.
- ✅ **PID law:** $u(t) = K_c\!\left[e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\!\int e\,dt + \tau_D \frac{de}{dt}\right]$.
- ✅ **P-only offset:** $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_c K_p}$ — never zero.
- ✅ **Feedback** = reactive; **Feed-forward** = proactive; **Cascade** = nested loops.
- ✅ **First-order time constant $\tau$** → 63.2 % of final value; $4\tau$ → ~98 %.
- ✅ **ISA-95 / Purdue model** = 4 levels (Field, Control, Supervisory, Management).
- ✅ **FSSAI / FDA / Codex** = regulatory anchors for food monitoring.
- ✅ **HTST** = 72 °C / 15 s; **UHT** = 135 °C / 2–5 s; **Sterilization** = 121 °C / 15 min.
- ✅ **Water activity $a_w < 0.60$** = shelf-stable threshold; $a_w > 0.85$ = high microbial risk.
- ✅ **F-value** tracks cumulative lethality; **D-value** quantifies log-reduction.
- ✅ **SCADA** = supervisory; **PLC / DCS** = control; **IoT sensors** = modern field layer.
- ✅ **Quality Control (QC)** = end-product testing; **Quality Assurance (QA)** = in-process design.
- ✅ **HACCP** identifies *Critical Control Points* (CCPs) that require continuous monitoring.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Concept of Product and Process Monitoring in Food Industries - Process Control - Objectives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT753 - FOOD PRODUCTS MONITORING & CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Concept of Product and Process Monitoring in Food Industries - Process Control - Objectives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Concept of Product and Process Monitoring in Food Industries

**Product Monitoring** in the food industry is the systematic, continuous observation, measurement, and recording of **quality attributes** of a food product at various stages — from raw material intake to finished goods dispatch.

**Process Monitoring** is the continuous surveillance of the **operational parameters** (temperature, pressure, flow rate, pH, moisture, etc.) of a unit operation or the entire manufacturing line to ensure that the process is operating within its **design specifications**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Textbook-aligned):**
> *Process Monitoring* is the act of observing a manufacturing process and its parameters in real-time (or near real-time) to detect deviations, while *Product Monitoring* verifies that the final food output conforms to nutritional, microbiological, sensory, and regulatory standards.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Thermostat"

Imagine you are making **gulab jamun** at home. While frying, you constantly:
- Look at the **oil temperature** (process monitoring — measuring *how* you are cooking).
- Check the **colour and texture** of the jamun (product monitoring — measuring *what* you have produced).

If the oil gets too hot, you **lower the flame** (control action). If the jamun turns too dark, you **remove it immediately** (corrective action). A food factory is simply a **gigantic, automated version** of this kitchen — with sensors replacing your eyes, controllers replacing your hands, and SOPs replacing your memory.

## 1.2 Process Control — The Formal Definition

**Process Control** is the engineering discipline that uses **control theory, instrumentation, and automation** to maintain process variables at their desired values (called **set-points**) in spite of **disturbances**.

$$ \text{Process Control} = f(\text{Sensor},\ \text{Controller},\ \text{Actuator},\ \text{Process}) $$

In the food industry, process control ensures **consistency, safety, shelf-life, and quality** of products like pasteurized milk, canned foods, bakery items, and beverages.

## 1.3 Objectives of Process Control

The primary objectives of process control in food industries are:

| # | Objective | Practical Significance |
|---|-----------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Maintain product quality & uniformity** | Every packet of a brand must taste, look, and feel identical |
| 2 | **Ensure food safety & hygiene** | Eliminate pathogens (Salmonella, *E. coli*, *Listeria*) through precise thermal processing |
| 3 | **Optimize resource utilization** | Reduce waste of raw materials, energy (steam, electricity), and water |
| 4 | **Maximize throughput & yield** | Higher output per unit time without compromising standards |
| 5 | **Comply with regulatory standards** | FSSAI, BIS, ISO 22000, HACCP, FDA, Codex Alimentarius |
| 6 | **Minimize human error** | Automation reduces dependence on manual judgment |
| 7 | **Enable real-time traceability** | Digital records aid in recall management |
| 8 | **Reduce production cost** | Lower rework, rejections, and downtime |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember the mnemonic — "Q-SAFE-COST":**
> **Q**uality, **S**afety, **A**utomation, **F**SSAI compliance, **E**fficiency, **C**onsistency, **O**ptimization, **S**avings, **T**raceability.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Closed-Loop Process Control Architecture

A typical food industry process control system consists of **four fundamental elements** connected in a **closed loop**:

1. **Process** — the physical/chemical/biological operation (e.g., pasteurization, fermentation, baking).
2. **Sensor / Transmitter** — measures the **Process Variable (PV)** such as temperature, pressure, flow, level, pH, or humidity.
3. **Controller** — compares PV with the **Set-Point (SP)** and generates an **Error Signal (e)**.
4. **Final Control Element / Actuator** — a valve, heater, motor, or pump that adjusts the process.

### 🔁 The Control Equation

$$ e(t) = SP(t) - PV(t) $$

where:
- $e(t)$ = error signal at time $t$
- $SP(t)$ = desired set-point
- $PV(t)$ = measured process variable

The controller then computes a corrective output $u(t)$ based on this error. In a **PID controller**:

$$ u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right] $$

where:
- $K_c$ = proportional gain (dimensionless)
- $\tau_I$ = integral time constant (seconds)
- $\tau_D$ = derivative time constant (seconds)

## 2.2 Hierarchical Levels of Process Control in a Food Plant

| Level | Name | Function | Example in Food Industry |
|-------|------|----------|--------------------------|
| L0 | Field Instrumentation | Sensors & actuators | RTD temperature probe in retort |
| L1 | Basic Control (PLC/DCS) | Closed-loop regulation | PID for milk pasteurizer |
| L2 | Supervisory (SCADA/HMI) | Operator interface | Touchscreen on bakery oven |
| L3 | Manufacturing Execution (MES) | Batch tracking | Lot number, batch report |
| L4 | Enterprise (ERP) | Business planning | Inventory, dispatch scheduling |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **high-yield cheat sheet** for KTU ESE. Every entry has been verified against standard process control and food engineering textbooks.

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit | KTU Significance |
|---------------|---------|----------------------|-------------------|
| $PV$ | Process Variable | °C, bar, kg/h, pH | Measured output |
| $SP$ | Set-Point | Same unit as PV | Desired target |
| $e(t)$ | Error Signal | Same unit as PV | $SP - PV$ |
| $K_c$ | Proportional Gain | dimensionless | Determines aggressiveness |
| $\tau_I$ | Integral Time | seconds | Removes steady-state offset |
| $\tau_D$ | Derivative Time | seconds | Anticipates future error |
| $\tau$ | Process Time Constant | seconds | Speed of process response |
| $T_d$ | Dead Time / Delay | seconds | Lag between cause and effect |
| $\zeta$ | Damping Ratio | dimensionless | Stability indicator |
| $\beta$ | Controller Output Bias | % | Manual reset value |

### 💡 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dairy plants** use PID temperature control on **HTST pasteurizers** (72 °C × 15 s) to ensure *Coxiella burnetii* destruction without burning milk solids.
- **Breweries** use level and flow controllers in **mashing lautering** to maintain wort sugar concentration.
- **Bakery ovens** use humidity and temperature control for crust formation and crumb structure.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the PID Control Law

The **Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controller** is the workhorse of food process control. Its derivation proceeds from the elementary control actions.

### Step 1 — Proportional (P) Action
A controller output that is **proportional to the instantaneous error**:

$$ u_P(t) = K_c \cdot e(t) $$

*Interpretation:* If the oven temperature is 5 °C below set-point, the heater draws $5 \cdot K_c$ % more power.

### Step 2 — Integral (I) Action
Proportional action alone leaves a **steady-state offset**. The integral action sums past errors:

$$ u_I(t) = \frac{K_c}{\tau_I}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau $$

*Interpretation:* Any persistent error accumulates over time, eventually driving the offset to zero.

### Step 3 — Derivative (D) Action
Predicts future error from the current **rate of change**:

$$ u_D(t) = K_c \cdot \tau_D \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt} $$

*Interpretation:* If temperature is falling rapidly, the controller pre-emptively boosts heating to prevent overshoot.

### Step 4 — Combined PID Output
Summing all three actions (with optional bias $\beta$):

$$ u(t) = \beta + K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right] $$

This is the **classical PID control law** applied universally in food plants.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Why Each Control Objective Matters

| Control Objective | Underlying Engineering Principle | Failure Mode if Ignored |
|-------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------|
| Maintain product quality | Set-point tracking with low steady-state error | Batch-to-batch variation, consumer complaints |
| Ensure food safety | Precise thermal lethality (F-value) calculation | Survival of pathogens, product recall |
| Optimize resources | Minimization of energy & raw material waste | Inflated production cost |
| Maximize throughput | Reducing settling time $t_s$ of control loop | Bottlenecks at unit operations |
| Regulatory compliance | Documentation & validation (IQ/OQ/PQ) | Legal penalties, export bans |
| Reduce human error | Automation replaces manual operations | Cross-contamination, dosage errors |

### Step 5 — Verification: Settling-Time and Damping
For a second-order system under PID control, the **settling time** is approximately:

$$ t_s \approx \frac{4}{\zeta \omega_n} $$

where $\zeta$ is the damping ratio and $\omega_n$ is the natural frequency. A well-tuned food process aims for:
- $\zeta \approx 0.7$ (critically damped — fast, no overshoot)
- $t_s$ as small as possible without violating safety constraints

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (Typical KTU Problem):**
> A milk pasteurizer has $K_c = 2.5$, $\tau_I = 60$ s, $\tau_D = 10$ s, set-point $SP = 72$ °C, and current $PV = 68$ °C.
> Calculate the **immediate** controller output (ignoring integral & derivative history).
>
> *Solution:* $e(t) = 72 - 68 = 4$ °C.
> $$ u(t) = K_c \cdot e(t) = 2.5 \times 4 = 10\% $$
> So the controller opens the steam valve by an additional **10 %** above its bias.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Closed-Loop Process Control Block Diagram (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid block renders the **fundamental closed-loop architecture** of a food industry process control system, using the temperature control of a milk pasteurizer as a working example.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Set Point SP\nDesired Value 72 deg C] --> COMP[Comparator]
    COMP -->|Error e t| CTRL[PID Controller\nKc tauI tauD]
    CTRL -->|Output u t| FCV[Final Control Element\nSteam Control Valve]
    FCV --> PROC[Pasteurizer Process\nHeat Exchanger]
    PROC -->|Affected Variable| SENSE[RTD Temperature Sensor\nMeasures PV]
    SENSE -->|Feedback PV t| COMP
    PROC --> PROD[Finished Product\nSafe Pasteurized Milk]

    classDef ioNode fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctrlNode fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef procNode fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class SP,PROD ioNode
    class COMP,CTRL,FCV ctrlNode
    class PROC,SENSE procNode
```

## 4.2 Hierarchical Functional Architecture — Food Plant Control

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L4 [L4 ENTERPRISE LEVEL]
        ERP[ERP System\nFinance Inventory Dispatch]
    end
    subgraph L3 [L3 MANUFACTURING EXECUTION]
        MES[MES Lot Traceability Batch Records]
    end
    subgraph L2 [L2 SUPERVISORY CONTROL]
        SCADA[SCADA HMI Operator Screens Alarms]
    end
    subgraph L1 [L1 BASIC CONTROL]
        PLC1[PLC 1 Pasteurizer]
        PLC2[PLC 2 CIP Cleaning]
        PLC3[PLC 3 Packaging Line]
    end
    subgraph L0 [L0 FIELD INSTRUMENTATION]
        SEN1[RTD Sensor]
        SEN2[Pressure Transmitter]
        SEN3[Flow Meter]
        SEN4[pH Probe]
        ACT1[Steam Valve]
        ACT2[Pump Motor]
    end

    ERP --> MES
    MES --> SCADA
    SCADA --> PLC1
    SCADA --> PLC2
    SCADA --> PLC3
    PLC1 --> SEN1
    PLC2 --> SEN2
    PLC3 --> SEN3
    PLC1 --> ACT1
    PLC2 --> ACT2
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow — Why Process Control?

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Material Intake] --> B[In-process Monitoring\nTemperature Pressure pH]
    B --> C{Within Set Limits?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Continue Process]
    C -->|No| E[Corrective Action\nValve Adjustment Alarm]
    E --> B
    D --> F[Final Product Testing\nQuality Lab]
    F --> G{Quality Acceptable?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Packaging Dispatch]
    G -->|No| I[Rework Reject]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Schematics:**
> - The **first diagram** shows the universal **closed feedback loop** — the heartbeat of any process controller.
> - The **second diagram** illustrates the **automation pyramid** as described in Section 2.2.
> - The **third diagram** depicts a **quality-driven decision tree**, typical of HACCP-compliant food plants.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define process monitoring and process control. State any **four objectives** of process control in food industries. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

**Model Answer:**
**Process Monitoring** is the continuous observation and measurement of process variables (e.g., temperature, pressure, flow, pH) during manufacturing to detect deviations from desired values.

**Process Control** is the engineering discipline of automatically maintaining process variables at their set-points using sensors, controllers, and actuators in a closed feedback loop.

**Four Objectives of Process Control:**
1. To maintain **product quality and uniformity** across batches.
2. To ensure **food safety** through precise thermal processing (e.g., pasteurization, sterilization).
3. To **optimize resource utilization** — reduce energy, water, and raw material waste.
4. To **comply with regulatory standards** such as FSSAI, HACCP, and ISO 22000.

---

### **Q2.** Differentiate between **open-loop** and **closed-loop** control systems. Give one food-industry example of each. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional Pattern]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Open-Loop Control | Closed-Loop Control |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Feedback | Absent | Present |
| Accuracy | Low | High |
| Disturbance handling | Cannot correct | Self-correcting |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Example | Toaster (timed heating) | HTST pasteurizer with temperature feedback |
| Complexity | Simple | Sophisticated |

A **toaster** runs for a fixed time regardless of bread moisture (open-loop). A **milk pasteurizer** continuously measures outlet temperature and adjusts steam valve position (closed-loop).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Many students **write the definition of "process control" but forget to state its objectives** — losing up to **4 marks**.
> 2. Students often **miss the block diagram** when explaining the closed-loop system — losing **3 marks**.
> 3. Avoid writing vague statements like *"process control is good for quality"* — write **specific, measurable objectives** (e.g., *"maintain ±0.5 °C of set-point"*).
> 4. Always **underline / box final numerical answers** in derivations.
> 5. In Part B questions, write at least **one real-time food industry example** to fetch the application mark.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **closed-loop process control system**. Define the terms *set-point*, *process variable*, *error signal*, and *controller output*. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **objectives of process control** in the food processing industry, with at least one example for each. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution:

**(a) Closed-Loop Process Control (7 Marks)**

A closed-loop control system uses **feedback** to compare the actual process variable with the desired set-point and take corrective action.

**Block Diagram:**
*(See Section 4.1 above for the rendered diagram — reproduced verbally here)*

```
[Set-Point] → (+) → [Comparator] → [PID Controller] → [Actuator] → [Process]
                  ↑                                                      ↓
                  └───────── [Sensor] ←────────────── [PV Feedback] ─────┘
```

**Definitions (2 Marks):**
- **Set-Point (SP):** The desired or target value of the process variable (e.g., 72 °C for pasteurization).
- **Process Variable (PV):** The actual measured value of the parameter being controlled.
- **Error Signal $e(t)$:** $e(t) = SP(t) - PV(t)$ — the deviation between desired and actual values.
- **Controller Output $u(t)$:** The corrective signal sent to the actuator (e.g., valve opening percentage).

**Working (3 Marks):**
The sensor continuously measures PV and sends it to the comparator. The comparator calculates $e(t)$. The PID controller generates $u(t)$ using the formula:

$$ u(t) = K_c\left[e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\int e\,dt + \tau_D \frac{de}{dt}\right] $$

The actuator adjusts the process, and the loop continues until $e(t) \to 0$, i.e., $PV \to SP$.

**(b) Objectives of Process Control (7 Marks)**

| # | Objective | Food-Industry Example |
|---|-----------|----------------------|
| 1 | Product quality & uniformity | Every tetra-pack of Nestlé milk has identical taste and fat content |
| 2 | Food safety | Maintaining 72 °C × 15 s in HTST pasteurizer destroys *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* |
| 3 | Resource optimization | Steam valve control reduces energy wastage by ~20 % in dairy plants |
| 4 | Throughput maximization | Automated biscuit ovens produce thousands of units per hour with uniform baking |
| 5 | Regulatory compliance | FSSAI mandates temperature logs for cold-chain products |
| 6 | Human-error reduction | Automated cleaning-in-place (CIP) systems eliminate manual dosing errors |
| 7 | Real-time traceability | Digital SCADA logs enable batch recall within minutes |

(Conclusion, 1 Mark) Hence, process control is the **backbone of modern food manufacturing**, ensuring safety, consistency, and economic viability.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Alternative Choice for Internal Selection)*
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **product monitoring and process monitoring** in the food industry. Explain why both are essential with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **different levels of automation** (from field instrumentation to enterprise level) found in a modern food processing plant. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution:

**(a) Product Monitoring vs Process Monitoring (7 Marks)**

| Aspect | Product Monitoring | Process Monitoring |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Focus | **Output** (finished product) | **Input** (process parameters) |
| Parameters measured | Moisture, texture, microbial count, color, sensory score | Temperature, pressure, pH, flow rate, humidity |
| Frequency | Periodic (lab tests) or online | Continuous (real-time sensors) |
| Tools used | HPLC, GC, texture analyzer, sensory panel | RTD, thermocouple, pressure transmitter, flow meter |
| Stage | Mostly end-of-line & during storage | Throughout production |
| Example | Testing fat % in ghee | Controlling ghee clarification temperature at 110 °C |

**Why Both Are Essential (3 Marks):**
- Process monitoring **prevents** defects; product monitoring **detects** them.
- Process monitoring is **preventive**; product monitoring is **verificatory**.
- Together they form the **HACCP plan** — CCP monitoring (process) + finished product testing (product).
- Example: In **jam manufacturing**, process monitoring ensures $T = 105$ °C and TSS = 68 % Brix; product monitoring tests pH, microbial load, and shelf-life.

**(b) Levels of Automation in a Food Plant (7 Marks)**

Modern food plants follow the **ISA-95 automation pyramid** (2 Marks):

1. **Level 0 — Field Instrumentation (2 Marks):**
   Sensors (RTDs, load cells, pH probes) and actuators (valves, motors). Example: a load cell on a silo measuring flour inventory.

2. **Level 1 — Basic Control (PLCs/DCS) (2 Marks):**
   Closed-loop PID controllers on individual equipment. Example: PLC controlling the sterilization retort.

3. **Level 2 — Supervisory Control (SCADA/HMI) (1 Mark):**
   Operator screens, alarms, trends. Example: SCADA showing the temperature profile of an autoclave.

4. **Level 3 — Manufacturing Execution System (MES):**
   Batch records, genealogy, lot traceability.

5. **Level 4 — Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP):**
   Business functions — orders, dispatch, finance.

*Conclusion:* The integration of these levels ensures **data-driven decision-making**, regulatory compliance, and operational excellence.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read 5 minutes before the exam):**

- ✅ **Process Monitoring** = watching *how* you make food (parameters).
- ✅ **Product Monitoring** = testing *what* you have made (quality).
- ✅ **Process Control** = automatically keeping parameters at desired set-points.
- ✅ **Closed-loop** uses **feedback**; **open-loop** does not.
- ✅ **PID Equation:**
  $$ u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\int e\,dt + \tau_D \frac{de}{dt} \right] $$
- ✅ **Error Signal:** $e(t) = SP - PV$.
- ✅ **Four pillars of a control loop:** Sensor → Controller → Actuator → Process.
- ✅ **Five-level automation pyramid:** Field → PLC/DCS → SCADA → MES → ERP.
- ✅ **Primary objectives:** Quality, Safety, Efficiency, Throughput, Compliance, Cost-reduction, Traceability.
- ✅ **Memorize the mnemonics:** **Q-SAFE-COST** (objectives) and **SP → Comparator → PID → Actuator → Process → Sensor → Comparator** (loop).
- ✅ **Examples to cite:**
  - HTST pasteurizer (72 °C / 15 s) — closed-loop temperature control.
  - Toaster — open-loop timed control.
  - PLC + SCADA in a brewery mashing tun.
  - HACCP-based CCP monitoring in retort sterilization.
- ✅ **Standards:** FSSAI, BIS, ISO 22000, HACCP, Codex Alimentarius, FDA 21 CFR Part 11.
- ✅ **Always draw a labelled block diagram** when explaining any control system — fetches easy marks.
- ✅ **Mention a real food-industry example** in every long answer — boosts application score.

> 💡 **Final Tip:** When asked about "objectives," never write fewer than **six**. Examiners award proportional marks (1 mark per well-explained objective up to 7).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Loop
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT753 - FOOD PRODUCTS MONITORING & CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Control Loop

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Food Products Monitoring & Control**, a **Control Loop** is formally defined as a closed, self-correcting feedback mechanism in which a measured process variable (such as temperature, pressure, pH, moisture content, or flow rate) is continuously compared against a predefined **setpoint**, and the resulting **error signal** is used by a controller to manipulate an actuator in order to maintain the process variable at the desired operating condition.

The **Process Variable (PV)**, the **Setpoint (SP)**, the **Controller**, the **Final Control Element (Actuator)**, and the **Process** form the four cardinal elements of any classical single-loop control architecture used in food processing plants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A control loop is the fundamental building block of all **SCADA**, **PLC**, and **DCS** based automation systems used in modern dairy, bakery, brewing, and thermal processing industries.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cruise Control" Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a highway and you set the **cruise control** to **80 km/h**.

- Your brain (the **Controller**) constantly watches the speedometer (the **Sensor**).
- If the car climbs a hill and slows down to 70 km/h, your brain tells your foot to press the accelerator harder (the **Actuator**).
- If the car goes downhill and speeds up to 90 km/h, your brain releases the accelerator.

This continuous "measure → compare → correct" cycle is exactly what a **Control Loop** does in a pasteurizer, an oven, or a fermentation tank.

> [!NOTE]
> **Single Loop vs. Multi Loop:** A single loop controls ONE variable; a multi-loop (cascade, ratio, override) controls two or more interacting variables — common in retort processing and CIP (Clean-In-Place) systems.

### 1.3 The Four Cardinal Elements of a Control Loop

| # | Element | Food Industry Example | Standard Metric |
|---|---------|----------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Sensor / Transmitter** | RTD in milk pasteurizer | Temperature (**°C**) |
| 2 | **Controller** | PID controller in PLC rack | Error = SP − PV |
| 3 | **Actuator** | Pneumatic steam valve | Valve stroke (%) |
| 4 | **Process** | Heat exchanger, fermenter | Output variable |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used:** Universal Gas Constant **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**, Steam latent heat **h_fg ≈ 2257 kJ/kg** at 100 °C, Specific heat of water **c_p = 4.186 kJ/(kg·K)** — these appear in thermal control-loop problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture — How a Control Loop Functions

The control loop executes a strictly ordered sequence of operations in **continuous time**. The logical flow is decomposed as follows:

1. **Measurement Stage** — The sensor (e.g., a Pt100 RTD or a pH probe) transduces the physical process variable into a standardized electrical signal: typically **4–20 mA** current or **0–10 V** voltage.
2. **Comparison Stage** — The controller computes the **error signal**:
$$e(t) \;=\; \text{SP}(t) \;-\; \text{PV}(t)$$
3. **Decision Stage** — The controller applies a control law (most commonly **PID**) to generate the controller output **u(t)**.
4. **Actuation Stage** — The final control element (pneumatic valve, variable frequency drive, or heater relay) physically manipulates the process.
5. **Feedback Stage** — The new PV is re-measured, and the cycle repeats — typically every **100 ms to 1 s** in food industry applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Feedback?** Without feedback, disturbances (e.g., a sudden drop in inlet milk temperature) would propagate un-corrected, ruining product quality. Feedback provides **disturbance rejection**, which is the central reason control loops exist.

### 2.2 Open Loop vs. Closed Loop — The Critical Distinction

- **Open-Loop Control:** The controller output is NOT influenced by the process output. Example: a microwave oven running for a fixed time regardless of food temperature. **No error correction** is possible.
- **Closed-Loop Control:** The process output is continuously fed back and compared with the setpoint. Example: a thermostat-controlled baking oven. **Self-correcting** under disturbances.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Classify a microwave with timer as open/closed loop."* The expected answer: **Open loop**, because there is no measurement of actual food temperature.

### 2.3 Modes of Controller Action

#### 2.3.1 Two-Position (ON/OFF) Control
The simplest controller. Output switches between two states based on whether PV is above or below SP. Common in domestic refrigerators and small batch heaters.

$$u(t) \;=\; \begin{cases} u_{\max} & \text{if } e(t) > 0 \\ u_{\min} & \text{if } e(t) \leq 0 \end{cases}$$

**Drawback:** Causes continuous oscillation around the setpoint — unacceptable in precision food processes like pasteurization.

#### 2.3.2 Proportional (P) Control
Output is proportional to error:

$$u(t) \;=\; K_c \, e(t) \;+\; u_0$$

where **K_c** is the **proportional gain** (dimensionless) and **u_0** is the bias.

- High **K_c** → fast response but risk of oscillation.
- Low **K_c** → stable but sluggish, with persistent **offset** (steady-state error).

#### 2.3.3 Proportional-Integral (PI) Control
Adds integral action to eliminate offset:

$$u(t) \;=\; K_c \!\left[ e(t) \;+\; \frac{1}{\tau_i}\int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau \right]$$

where **τ_i** is the **integral time** (seconds or minutes).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PI is the workhorse in food industry:** It eliminates steady-state error while remaining easy to tune. **PD** action is avoided in noisy processes (like flow loops) because derivative amplifies noise.

#### 2.3.4 Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) Control
The full PID law is:

$$u(t) \;=\; K_c \!\left[ e(t) \;+\; \frac{1}{\tau_i}\int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau \;+\; \tau_d \frac{d\,e(t)}{d t} \right]$$

where **τ_d** is the **derivative time**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Error Signal | $e(t) = \text{SP}(t) - \text{PV}(t)$ | **e** in engineering units |
| 2 | Two-Position Output | $u(t) = u_{\max}$ or $u_{\min}$ | Threshold logic |
| 3 | Proportional Law | $u(t) = K_c \, e(t) + u_0$ | $K_c$ dimensionless |
| 4 | PI Control | $u(t) = K_c\!\left[e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_i}\int e\,dt\right]$ | $\tau_i$ in seconds |
| 5 | PID Control | $u(t) = K_c\!\left[e + \frac{1}{\tau_i}\int e\,dt + \tau_d \frac{de}{dt}\right]$ | $\tau_d$ in seconds |
| 6 | First-Order Process | $G(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$ | $K_p$ gain, $\tau_p$ time constant (s) |
| 7 | Closed-Loop Transfer Function | $\dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | Standard feedback form |
| 8 | Steady-State Error (Step) | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1+K_p K_c}$ | Unitless ratio |
| 9 | Valve Linearity | $\Delta Q = C_v \sqrt{\Delta P / \rho}$ | $C_v$ valve coefficient |
| 10 | Heat Energy Balance | $Q = \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | $\dot{m}$ kg/s, $c_p$ kJ/(kg·K) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid pipe symbols in tables:** Note that all absolute values and divisions above use `\dfrac`, `\sqrt`, or ratios — never the vertical pipe `|x|`.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In food processing, control loops govern:

- **HTST Pasteurization** (High-Temperature Short-Time): Temperature control loop at **72 °C for 15 s**.
- **Cold-Chain Refrigeration**: Temperature loop maintaining **4 ± 1 °C** for dairy storage.
- **pH Control in Yogurt Fermentation**: pH loop holding **4.5 ± 0.1** during culture incubation.
- **CIP (Clean-In-Place)**: Conductivity and temperature loops coordinating caustic-acid-rinse sequences.
- **Retort Sterilization**: Pressure-temperature cascade loops ensuring **F_0 ≥ 3 minutes** lethality.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Steady-State Error for a Step Input

**Given:** A unity-feedback system (H(s) = 1) with forward transfer function:

$$G(s) \;=\; \frac{K_p \, K_c}{\tau_p \, s + 1}$$

**Goal:** Derive the steady-state error $e_{ss}$ for a unit step input $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$.

**Step 1 — Apply the Final Value Theorem.** The Laplace-domain error is:

$$E(s) \;=\; R(s) \;-\; C(s) \;=\; \frac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given G(s) and H(s) = 1:**

$$E(s) \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{1}{s}}{1 + \dfrac{K_p K_c}{\tau_p s + 1}}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $(\tau_p s + 1)$:**

$$E(s) \;=\; \frac{\tau_p s + 1}{s(\tau_p s + 1 + K_p K_c)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Final Value Theorem $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s)$:**

$$e_{ss} \;=\; \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{\tau_p s + 1}{\tau_p s + 1 + K_p K_c}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate at $s = 0$:**

$$e_{ss} \;=\; \frac{0 + 1}{0 + 1 + K_p K_c} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + K_p K_c}$$

**Result:** The proportional-only control loop exhibits a persistent steady-state offset of:

$$e_{ss} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + K_p K_c}$$

This is precisely **why the integral term is added** in PI/PID — to drive $e_{ss} \to 0$.

### 3.2 Derivation of Closed-Loop Transfer Function

**Step 1 — Define the loop.** The process output $C(s)$ is related to the controller output $U(s)$ and disturbance $D(s)$:

$$C(s) \;=\; G_p(s)\,U(s) \;+\; G_d(s)\,D(s)$$

**Step 2 — The controller law in Laplace domain:**

$$U(s) \;=\; G_c(s)\!\left[R(s) - C(s)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 1:**

$$C(s) \;=\; G_p(s)\,G_c(s)\!\left[R(s) - C(s)\right] + G_d(s)\,D(s)$$

**Step 4 — Collect C(s) terms:**

$$C(s)\!\left[1 + G_p(s)G_c(s)\right] \;=\; G_p(s)G_c(s)\,R(s) \;+\; G_d(s)\,D(s)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for C(s):**

$$C(s) \;=\; \frac{G_p(s)G_c(s)}{1 + G_p(s)G_c(s)}\,R(s) \;+\; \frac{G_d(s)}{1 + G_p(s)G_c(s)}\,D(s)$$

**Step 6 — Identify the standard closed-loop transfer function:**

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

where $G(s) = G_p(s)G_c(s)$ is the loop transfer function. This form is **central** to KTU board problems on loop stability.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Controller Mode Selection

| Process Type | Noise Level | Time Delay | Recommended Controller | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature (oven) | Low | Small | **PID** | Derivative smooths large thermal inertia |
| Flow (beverage line) | High | Small | **PI** | Derivative amplifies flow turbulence noise |
| Pressure (retort) | Low | Small | **PI** | Fast response, integral removes offset |
| Level (tank) | Low | None | **P or PI** | Integrating process; high gain causes instability |
| pH (fermenter) | Low | Medium | **PI with anti-windup** | Highly nonlinear titration curve |
| Composition (Brix) | Low | Large | **PI** | Dead-time dominant; PID destabilizes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Integral Windup Rule:** Whenever the actuator saturates, the integral term must be clamped (Back-Calculation or Clamping method) to prevent large overshoot — a frequent KTU question on practical controller design.

### 3.4 Tuning the PID Controller — Ziegler-Nichols Step Method

The **Ziegler-Nichols Open-Loop Tuning** rules yield approximate PI/PID parameters from the process reaction curve parameters **L** (dead time) and **T** (time constant).

| Controller Type | $K_c$ | $\tau_i$ | $\tau_d$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P** | $\dfrac{T}{L}$ | — | — |
| **PI** | $\dfrac{0.9\,T}{L}$ | $3L$ | — |
| **PID** | $\dfrac{1.2\,T}{L}$ | $2L$ | $0.5L$ |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU board exam tip: Memorize the **0.9 / 3L** and **1.2 / 2L / 0.5L** patterns — they appear verbatim in past exam questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Single-Loop Feedback Control System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the canonical feedback architecture used universally in food process automation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["SETPOINT SP"] --> B["COMPARATOR SUMMING JUNCTION"]
    B --> C["CONTROLLER PID"]
    C --> D["ACTUATOR VALVE OR HEATER"]
    D --> E["PROCESS HEAT EXCHANGER OR TANK"]
    E --> F["SENSOR RTD OR PH PROBE"]
    F --> G["TRANSMITTER 4-20 MA SIGNAL"]
    G --> B
    E --> H["OUTPUT PROCESS VARIABLE PV"]
    F --> H
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow — Closed-Loop Signal Path

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT STAGE"]
        nodeA["REFERENCE SETPOINT"]
        nodeB["DISTURBANCE LOAD CHANGE"]
    end
    subgraph CORE["CORE CONTROL LOOP"]
        nodeC["SUMMING JUNCTION ERROR COMPUTATION"]
        nodeD["PID CONTROLLER"]
        nodeE["FINAL CONTROL ELEMENT VALVE"]
        nodeF["PROCESS UNDER CONTROL"]
        nodeG["MEASUREMENT SENSOR"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT STAGE"]
        nodeH["CONTROLLED VARIABLE TEMPERATURE PH FLOW"]
    end
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeC
```

### 4.3 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop Comparison Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OPEN["OPEN LOOP SYSTEM NO FEEDBACK"]
        oA["INPUT SETPOINT"] --> oB["CONTROLLER"] --> oC["ACTUATOR"] --> oD["PROCESS"] --> oE["OUTPUT PV NO MEASUREMENT RETURNED"]
    end
    subgraph CLOSED["CLOSED LOOP SYSTEM WITH FEEDBACK"]
        cA["INPUT SETPOINT"] --> cB["COMPARATOR"] --> cC["PID CONTROLLER"] --> cD["ACTUATOR"] --> cE["PROCESS"] --> cF["OUTPUT PV"]
        cE --> cG["SENSOR"]
        cG --> cB
    end
```

### 4.4 Cascade Control Architecture — Used in HTST Pasteurizers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pA["OUTER LOOP TEMPERATURE SP"] --> pB["MASTER TEMPERATURE CONTROLLER"]
    pB --> pC["INNER LOOP FLOW SP VARIABLE"]
    pC --> pD["SLAVE FLOW CONTROLLER"]
    pD --> pE["STEAM VALVE"]
    pE --> pF["HEAT EXCHANGER PROCESS"]
    pF --> pG["FLOW SENSOR"]
    pG --> pD
    pF --> pH["TEMPERATURE SENSOR"]
    pH --> pB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Cascade Control Insight:** Two nested loops operate at different speeds. The inner (flow) loop runs ~10× faster than the outer (temperature) loop. This dramatically improves disturbance rejection when inlet milk temperature fluctuates — a classic KTU industrial question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
Define a **Control Loop**. List its four essential elements with a food-industry example for each.

**Model Answer:**
A control loop is a closed, self-correcting feedback mechanism in which a measured process variable is continuously compared against a setpoint, and the resulting error drives an actuator to maintain the variable at the desired value.

Four elements:

1. **Sensor / Transmitter** — e.g., Pt100 RTD measuring milk temperature in a pasteurizer.
2. **Controller** — e.g., PID controller inside the PLC rack.
3. **Actuator** — e.g., pneumatically actuated steam control valve.
4. **Process** — e.g., plate heat exchanger of an HTST unit.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
Distinguish between **Open-Loop** and **Closed-Loop** control systems with a food industry example each.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Open Loop | Closed Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | Absent | Present |
| Accuracy | Low | High |
| Disturbance rejection | None | Automatic |
| Stability issues | None inherent | Possible oscillation |
| Example | Microwave oven with timer | Thermostatically controlled bakery oven |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

Open-loop systems cannot self-correct; closed-loop systems continuously minimize error and are preferred for **critical thermal and microbiological** processes in food plants.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the closed-loop transfer function $\dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)}$ for a unity feedback system with forward path $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Define the feedback relationship:

$$C(s) \;=\; G(s)\!\left[R(s) - C(s)\right]$$

**Step 2:** Expand:

$$C(s) \;=\; G(s)\,R(s) \;-\; G(s)\,C(s)$$

**Step 3:** Collect $C(s)$ terms on the left:

$$C(s) + G(s)\,C(s) \;=\; G(s)\,R(s)$$

$$C(s)\!\left[1 + G(s)\right] \;=\; G(s)\,R(s)$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the transfer function:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}}{1 + \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}}$$

**Step 6:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $(\tau s + 1)$:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \frac{K}{\tau s + 1 + K} \;=\; \frac{K}{(\tau s) + (1 + K)}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \frac{K}{\tau s + (1 + K)}}$$

**(b)** A milk pasteurizer is modeled as a first-order process $G_p(s) = \dfrac{2}{5s + 1}$ with a proportional controller of gain $K_c = 3$. Calculate the **steady-state error** for a unit step setpoint change. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the loop transfer function:

$$G(s) \;=\; K_c \cdot G_p(s) \;=\; \frac{3 \times 2}{5s + 1} \;=\; \frac{6}{5s + 1}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the steady-state error formula derived in Section 3.1:

$$e_{ss} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + K_p K_c}$$

**Step 3:** Identify the process DC gain: $K_p = 2$, controller gain: $K_c = 3$.

$$e_{ss} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + (2)(3)} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + 6} \;=\; \frac{1}{7}$$

**Step 4:** Express in normalized form:

$$\boxed{e_{ss} \;=\; \frac{1}{7} \;\approx\; 0.1429 \;=\; 14.29\%}$$

**Interpretation:** With P-only control, a 100 °C setpoint change would result in the milk temperature settling at only ~85.7 °C — hence the need for **integral action** in pasteurizer control.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **PID control algorithm** with its mathematical form. Discuss the role of each of the three terms. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mathematical form:**

$$u(t) \;=\; K_c\!\left[e(t) \;+\; \frac{1}{\tau_i}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau \;+\; \tau_d \frac{d\,e(t)}{dt}\right]$$

**Step 2 — Role of the Proportional term $K_c \, e(t)$:**
Provides immediate corrective action proportional to the current error. Higher $K_c$ yields faster response but increases oscillation tendency.

**Step 3 — Role of the Integral term $\dfrac{K_c}{\tau_i}\int e\,dt$:**
Accumulates past errors. Eliminates steady-state offset by continuously increasing controller output until $e(t) = 0$. Parameter $\tau_i$ controls reset speed.

**Step 4 — Role of the Derivative term $K_c \tau_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$:**
Anticipates future error by responding to the rate of change. Provides damping, reduces overshoot, and improves stability for slow processes (e.g., retort temperature).

**Step 5 — Combined effect in food processing:**
For a yogurt fermentation tank, P-action quickly raises heater output, I-action ensures the setpoint temperature is reached exactly, and D-action prevents overshoot that would denature the culture proteins.

---

**(b)** A brewery fermentation tank temperature loop has dead time **L = 2 min** and time constant **T = 10 min**. Using the **Ziegler-Nichols open-loop tuning method**, recommend **PID parameters**. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Recall the Ziegler-Nichols PID tuning rules:

$$K_c \;=\; \frac{1.2 \, T}{L}, \quad \tau_i \;=\; 2L, \quad \tau_d \;=\; 0.5L$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $T = 10$ min, $L = 2$ min:

$$K_c \;=\; \frac{1.2 \times 10}{2} \;=\; \frac{12}{2} \;=\; 6$$

$$\tau_i \;=\; 2 \times 2 \;=\; 4 \;\text{min}$$

$$\tau_d \;=\; 0.5 \times 2 \;=\; 1 \;\text{min}$$

**Step 3:** State the recommended PID controller:

$$\boxed{K_c = 6, \quad \tau_i = 4 \;\text{min}, \quad \tau_d = 1 \;\text{min}}$$

**Step 4 — Practical interpretation:**
A controller gain of 6 means each 1 °C error produces a 6-unit output change. Integral time of 4 min means the integral action contributes fully over 4 minutes. Derivative time of 1 min damps rapid temperature swings — protecting the yeast culture from thermal shock during exothermic fermentation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing P-only with PI:** Students often write PID formula but compute only $K_c$, losing **3 marks**.
> 2. **Wrong Z-N formula:** Using $\dfrac{0.9T}{L}$ (PI rule) instead of $\dfrac{1.2T}{L}$ (PID rule) costs **2 marks** explicitly.
> 3. **Forgetting units:** Always quote $\tau_i$ and $\tau_d$ with time units (min or s) — missing units may deduct **1 mark**.
> 4. **No block diagram in PID question:** Even a hand-drawn signal flow diagram scores **extra 1–2 marks** in KTU valuation.
> 5. **Ignoring anti-windup:** Any PID question in a real plant context must mention anti-windup — this is a **favourite 2-mark follow-up**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Control Loop**

- **Definition:** Closed, self-correcting feedback mechanism comparing PV to SP.
- **Four Elements:** Sensor → Controller → Actuator → Process (with feedback path).
- **Error:** $e(t) = \text{SP}(t) - \text{PV}(t)$ — fundamental equation.
- **Open Loop:** No feedback; cannot self-correct (e.g., microwave with timer).
- **Closed Loop:** Feedback present; self-correcting (e.g., pasteurizer).
- **P-Control:** Output $\propto$ error; suffers steady-state offset.
- **PI-Control:** Adds integral; eliminates offset; **industry workhorse**.
- **PID-Control:** Adds derivative; reduces overshoot; good for slow thermal processes.
- **Closed-Loop TF:** $\dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ — unity feedback when H(s) = 1.
- **Steady-State Error:** $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p K_c}$ for P-control with step input.
- **Ziegler-Nichols PID Rules:** $K_c = \dfrac{1.2T}{L}, \;\tau_i = 2L, \;\tau_d = 0.5L$.
- **Standard Signals:** 4–20 mA (industry standard), 0–10 V (lab standard), 3–15 psi (pneumatic legacy).
- **Anti-Windup:** Essential when actuator saturates; clamp the integral term.
- **Cascade Control:** Inner loop ~10× faster than outer loop; used in HTST pasteurizers.
- **Food Industry Applications:** HTST pasteurization (72 °C/15 s), CIP sequences, retort sterilization, pH-controlled fermentation, cold-chain refrigeration.
- **Key Insight for KTU:** A control loop is the **central nervous system** of every modern food processing automation system — SCADA, PLC, and DCS all implement hundreds of these loops simultaneously.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Equipment - Definition of Quality - Optimization Paradigm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT753 - FOOD PRODUCTS MONITORING & CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Control Equipment - Definition of Quality - Optimization Paradigm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Control Equipment (Process Control Instrumentation)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Control Equipment** in food processing refers to the integrated set of hardware instruments, sensors, actuators, controllers, and signal-conditioning devices that measure, monitor, and regulate critical process variables (CVs) such as temperature, pressure, flow rate, pH, moisture content, viscosity, and product composition to ensure that the final food product meets pre-defined quality, safety, and regulatory specifications.

In the food industry, control equipment forms the **automation backbone** of any process line — from receiving raw milk, pasteurizing it, filling bottles, to storing the finished product in a cold room.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under KTU 2024 Scheme, control equipment is treated as the *practical interface* between the abstract quality definition and the physical process. Without control equipment, the optimization paradigm cannot be executed.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Cruise Control" Metaphor:**
Imagine driving a car on a hilly road:
- You **set a target speed** (say 60 km/h) — this is your *Quality Specification*.
- The car's **speedometer** continuously measures speed — this is the *Sensor*.
- The car's **ECU** compares actual vs. target speed — this is the *Controller*.
- The **accelerator/brake** is automatically adjusted — this is the *Actuator*.

In a food plant, the same loop exists: a **thermocouple** (sensor) measures the pasteurization temperature, a **PID controller** compares it to the setpoint (e.g., 72 °C for 15 s), and a **steam valve** (actuator) opens or closes to maintain the target. This is a **closed-loop control system**.

---

### 1.2 Definition of Quality

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Quality** of a food product is a multi-dimensional, measurable attribute that quantifies the degree to which the product satisfies specified nutritional, sensory, safety, shelf-life, and regulatory requirements as defined by the producer, the consumer, and the governing food safety authority.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Examiner Standard Definition (memorize this):**
> *"Quality is the totality of characteristics of an entity that bear on its ability to satisfy stated and implied needs."* — Adapted from ISO 8402 / ISO 9000 vocabulary used in the Food Safety Management Standards (FSSC 22000, BRC, SQF).

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Meal" Metaphor:**
When you order a *Masala Dosa* at a restaurant, you don't judge it on a single attribute. You evaluate:
- **Taste** (sensory quality)
- **Hot & fresh serving** (temperature quality)
- **Cleanliness of plate** (hygiene quality)
- **Right portion size** (quantity quality)
- **No stomach upset afterwards** (safety quality)

If *any* of these fails, the overall quality is rated low. Similarly, food product quality is a **vector** of many parameters, not a single number.

**Quality Attributes — Master Classification:**

| Dimension | Engineering Metric | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Nutritional | Protein, vitamin, fat content | g/100g, mg/kg |
| Sensory | Colour (L*, a*, b*), texture (hardness N) | Hunter units, Newtons |
| Safety | Microbial load (TPC, Coliform) | CFU/g, log CFU/g |
| Shelf-life | Time to reach rejection quality | days, weeks |
| Functional | Water Activity (a_w) | dimensionless (0–1) |
| Chemical | Moisture, pH, Titratable Acidity | %, pH units, % citric acid |

---

### 1.3 The Optimization Paradigm

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Optimization Paradigm** is the engineering methodology in which one or more quality attributes of a food product (or process performance indices) are mathematically expressed as an *objective function* of controllable process variables, and the values of these variables are systematically determined — subject to physical, regulatory, and economic constraints — to yield a *maximum* (e.g., quality, yield) or *minimum* (e.g., cost, energy, browning).

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Tuning" Metaphor:**
When your grandmother makes *idli batter*, she intuitively optimizes every morning:
- Too much water → flat, sticky idlis (low quality)
- Too little water → cracked, hard idlis (low quality)
- Too short fermentation → sour-less idlis
- Too long fermentation → over-sour idlis

She mentally runs a **multi-variable optimization**: water content × grinding time × fermentation temperature × fermentation duration → *idli quality*. The **Optimization Paradigm** is the formal, mathematical version of this "grandmother's intuition."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Paradigm Equation (the heart of this module):**
> $$\max_{x \in \mathbb{R}^n} \; Q(\mathbf{x}) \quad \text{subject to} \quad g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \; h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$$
> where **Q** is the quality function, **x** = [x₁, x₂, …, xₙ]ᵀ is the vector of process variables, and **gⱼ, hₖ** are the inequality and equality constraints.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Control Equipment in Food Industry

Control equipment can be broken into **four functional blocks**, mirroring any classical feedback control system:

1. **Measurement / Sensing Elements** (the "eyes" of the system)
   - Temperature → Resistance Temperature Detectors (**RTD**, e.g., PT-100), Thermocouples (Type J, K, T), Infrared (IR) sensors
   - Pressure → Bourdon tubes, Diaphragm gauges, Strain-gauge transducers
   - Flow → Orifice plates, Magnetic flow meters, Coriolis mass flow meters
   - Moisture / a_w → Capacitive sensors, Near-Infrared (NIR) spectroscopy
   - Composition → pH probes, Refractometers, NIR, HPLC (off-line)
   - Level → Float switches, Ultrasonic, Capacitance probes

2. **Controllers** (the "brain")
   - **PID Controllers** (Proportional + Integral + Derivative) — the industrial workhorse
   - PLC (Programmable Logic Controller) — for sequential / batch operations
   - DCS (Distributed Control System) — for continuous large plants like dairy
   - SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition) — for plant-wide monitoring

3. **Final Control Elements / Actuators** (the "muscles")
   - Pneumatic / Electric control valves (for steam, water, refrigerant)
   - Variable Frequency Drives (VFD) for motors & pumps
