
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**

**Q1. Define colour as a quality attribute in foods and list the three components necessary for its perception.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Colour is the perceptual response of the human visual system to the spectral composition of visible light reflected from, transmitted through, or emitted by a food product.
- **Three components (2 marks):**
  1. **Illuminant / Light Source** — provides the radiant energy (e.g., D65, A, C).
  2. **Object / Food** — selectively absorbs, transmits, and reflects light based on its pigments and matrix.
  3. **Observer / Detector** — human eye + brain, standardized as the CIE 1931 standard observer (2° or 10°).
- **Closing line (½ mark):** Removing or altering any one of the three alters the perceived colour, which is why food colour specifications must declare all three.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**

**Q2. Differentiate between specular and diffuse reflection. How is this difference used to measure gloss in foods?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Specular reflection (1 mark):** Mirror-like reflection where the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection; governed by the Fresnel equations; dominant on smooth, polished surfaces (chocolate, glazed fruits).
- **Diffuse reflection (1 mark):** Scattering of light in all directions due to surface roughness and internal scattering; follows Lambert's cosine law; dominant on rough surfaces (bread crust, chalky tablets).
- **Gloss measurement principle (1 mark):** A glossmeter directs light at a fixed angle (typically 60°) onto the food surface and measures the intensity of the **specularly reflected** beam only. The ratio of this intensity to that reflected by a polished black-glass reference (n = 1.567) gives the **Gloss Unit (GU)** value. A high GU indicates a smooth, glossy surface; a low GU indicates roughness or matte texture.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 1, Question A]** *(Internal Choice)*

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the CIE L\*a\*b\* colour system with neat equations. Derive the CIELAB coordinates for a tomato paste sample with $X = 32.18$, $Y = 19.47$, $Z = 6.79$ measured under D65 illuminant ($X_n = 95.047$, $Y_n = 100$, $Z_n = 108.883$).

**(b) [7 Marks]** Two batches of refined sunflower oil have the following CIELAB coordinates:
- Batch P (Reference): $L^* = 85.2$, $a^* = -3.1$, $b^* = 14.6$
- Batch Q (Test): $L^* = 86.5$, $a^* = -2.4$, $b^* = 16.8$

Compute $\Delta E^*_{ab}$, $\Delta C^*$, and $\Delta h^\circ$, and comment on whether Batch Q passes the QC specification of $\Delta E^*_{ab} \le 2.0$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CIE L\*a\*b\* System and Derivation (7 marks)**

**Theory (3 marks):**
- CIELAB (1976) is a perceptually uniform colour space developed by the CIE.
- It is computed from CIE XYZ tristimulus values via a cube-root compression.
- Three coordinates: $L^*$ (Lightness, 0–100), $a^*$ (red–green, +/−), $b^*$ (yellow–blue, +/−).
- The cube root achieves near-uniform perceptual spacing.

**Equations (2 marks):** Already tabulated in Section 2.5 (rows 2, 3, 4).

**Numerical Derivation (2 marks):** As in Section 3.1, Step 8: $L^* = 51.37$, $a^* = 57.95$, $b^* = 36.76$.

**(b) Colour Difference Computation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute differences

$$\Delta L^* = 85.2 - 86.5 = -1.3$$
$$\Delta a^* = -3.1 - (-2.4) = -0.7$$
$$\Delta b^* = 14.6 - 16.8 = -2.2$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute $\Delta E^*_{ab}$

$$\Delta E^*_{ab} = \sqrt{(-1.3)^2 + (-0.7)^2 + (-2.2)^2} = \sqrt{1.69 + 0.49 + 4.84} = \sqrt{7.02} = 2.65$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute chroma and hue differences

For Batch P:
$$C^*_P = \sqrt{(-3.1)^2 + 14.6^2} = \sqrt{9.61 + 213.16} = 14.92$$
$$h^\circ_P = \arctan\!\left(\frac{14.6}{-3.1}\right) = 102.0^\circ \;(\text{quadrant II})$$

For Batch Q:
$$C^*_Q = \sqrt{(-2.4)^2 + 16.8^2} = \sqrt{5.76 + 282.24} = 16.97$$
$$h^\circ_Q = \arctan\!\left(\frac{16.8}{-2.4}\right) = 98.1^\circ \;(\text{quadrant II})$$

$$\Delta C^* = 14.92 - 16.97 = -2.05$$
$$\Delta h^\circ = 102.0^\circ - 98.1^\circ = 3.9^\circ$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** QC Decision

$\Delta E^*_{ab} = 2.65 > 2.0$ → **Batch Q FAILS** the specification. The test batch is noticeably lighter, slightly less green, and more yellow than the reference. Recommended action: investigate raw material source or bleaching process.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 1, Question B]** *(Internal Choice)*

**Question B (14 Marks):**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define gloss in foods. With the aid of a labelled block diagram, describe the operating principle of a 60° glossmeter. Mention the standard reference used.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A chocolate bar surface has a refractive index $n_2 = 1.50$. Light is incident from air ($n_1 = 1.00$) at $\theta_i = 60^\circ$. Calculate the specular reflectance for unpolarized light and the corresponding Gloss Unit value, given that the reference black glass has $R_{s,\text{ref}} = 0.100$ at 60°.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Gloss Definition and Glossmeter (7 marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):** Gloss is the *ability of a food surface to reflect light specularly (mirror-like)*, quantified as the ratio of the sample's specular reflectance to that of a reference standard (polished black glass, $n \approx 1.567$) under specified geometry.

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to Section 4.2 Gloss Measurement Workflow — eight blocks from LED source to Pass/Reject.

**Operating Principle (2 marks):**
- A stabilized LED emits light at exactly 60° to the sample normal.
- Light strikes the chocolate / coating / food surface.
- The specularly reflected beam is captured by a photodiode at the symmetric 60° position.
- Diffuse reflections are excluded by the detector's narrow acceptance angle.
- The signal is normalized to the reference glass reading.
- Result is displayed in **Gloss Units (GU)**.

---

**(b) Fresnel Calculation and Gloss Value (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Apply Snell's Law

$$\sin\theta_t = \frac{n_1 \sin\theta_i}{n_2} = \frac{1.00 \times \sin 60^\circ}{1.50} = \frac{0.8660}{1.50} = 0.5773$$
$$\theta_t = 35.26^\circ$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute cosines

$$\cos\theta_i = \cos 60^\circ = 0.5000$$
$$\cos\theta_t = \cos 35.26^\circ = 0.8165$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** S-polarization reflectance

$$R_{\perp} = \left(\frac{n_1\cos\theta_i - n_2\cos\theta_t}{n_1\cos\theta_i + n_2\cos\theta_t}\right)^{\!2} = \left(\frac{0.5000 - 1.50 \times 0.8165}{0.5000 + 1.50 \times 0.8165}\right)^{\!2}$$
$$= \left(\frac{0.5000 - 1.2247}{0.5000 + 1.2247}\right)^{\!2} = \left(\frac{-0.7247}{1.7247}\right)^{\!2} = 0.1766$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** P-polarization reflectance

$$R_{\parallel} = \left(\frac{n_1\cos\theta_t - n_2\cos\theta_i}{n_1\cos\theta_t + n_2\cos\theta_i}\right)^{\!2} = \left(\frac{0.8165 - 0.7500}{0.8165 + 0.7500}\right)^{\!2}$$
$$= \left(\frac{0.0665}{1.5665}\right)^{\!2} = 0.00180$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Unpolarized average

$$R_s = \frac{R_{\perp} + R_{\parallel}}{2} = \frac{0.1766 + 0.00180}{2} = 0.0892$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Gloss Units

$$GU = \frac{R_s}{R_{s,\text{ref}}} \times 100 = \frac{0.0892}{0.100} \times 100 = 89.2\,GU$$

**Interpretation:** A Gloss Unit value of **89.2 GU** at 60° indicates a high-gloss surface, consistent with well-tempered dark chocolate (typical: 85–100 GU).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Colour and Gloss Questions (from KTU board examiner experience):**
> 1. **Forgetting the $k$ normalising constant** in the tristimulus integral (–1 mark).
> 2. **Mixing Hunter Lab with CIELAB** equations — they look similar but use different transformations (–1 mark).
> 3. **Not specifying the illuminant** (D65, A, C) when reporting L\*a\*b\* — answers are incomplete without it (–½ mark).
> 4. **Forgetting quadrant correction** in $h^\circ = \arctan(b^*/a^*)$ — using only single-quadrant arctan gives wrong hue for green/blue foods (–1 mark).
> 5. **Confusing gloss geometries** — 20° is for high gloss, 60° is universal, 85° is for sheen. Wrong geometry cited = –1 mark.
> 6. **Skipping the Snell law step** in Fresnel derivations — examiners expect explicit calculation of $\theta_t$ before Fresnel substitution.
> 7. **Reporting $\Delta E$ without units / interpretation** — a numerical answer without the QC verdict loses the application mark.
> 8. **Writing "colour = pigment"** — colour is a *perception*, not the pigment itself. Pigments cause the spectral selectivity that produces the perception (–½ mark).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (memorize before KTU exam):**

- **Colour** is a perception requiring **illuminant + object + observer**.
- **CIE XYZ** (1931) is the foundational tristimulus space; **CIELAB** (1976) is the perceptually uniform derivative.
- **L\*** ranges 0–100; **a\*** red(±)/green(−); **b\*** yellow(±)/blue(−).
- **C\*** (chroma) = $\sqrt{a^{*2} + b^{*2}}$ — measures saturation.
- **h°** (hue) = $\arctan(b^*/a^*)$ with quadrant correction.
- **$\Delta E^*_{ab} < 1$** = imperceptible; **$\Delta E^*_{ab} < 2$** = acceptable; **$\Delta E^*_{ab} > 3.5$** = reject.
- **Just-Noticeable Difference (JND)** = $\Delta E^*_{ab} \approx 1$ for trained observers.
- **Munsell** uses **Hue, Value, Chroma** (visual matching) — still used for cocoa, coffee, and soil-adjacent foods.
- **Hunter Lab** is older, still embedded in legacy instruments.
- **Browning Index (BI)** is critical for **bakery, fried, roasted** products.
- **Whiteness Index (WI)** is critical for **flour, sugar, milk powder, salt**.
- **Gloss** = specular reflectance normalised to black-glass reference; measured in **GU**.
- **Standard gloss geometries**: 20° (high gloss), 60° (universal), 85° (sheen).
- **Fresnel equations** govern specular reflection: depend on refractive index mismatch.
- **Snell's law** must precede Fresnel calculation in derivations.
- **Gloss affected by**: surface roughness, fat migration, crystallization, wax deposition.
- **Colour affected by**: pigment type/concentration, pH, heat (Maillard, caramelization), oxidation, moisture.
- **Instruments**: Colorimeter (filters), Spectrophotometer (spectral scan), Glossmeter (single-angle specular), Computer vision (2-D).
- **Online QC loop**: sensor → PLC → comparator → pass/reject lane → SCADA.
- **Reference standards to cite**: ISO 11664-4 (CIELAB), ISO 2813 (gloss), ASTM D523, ASTM E313 (whiteness).
- **Key food applications**: tomato paste (L\*a\*b\* for lycopene), milk (WI), chocolate (GU for tempering), meat (a\* for myoglobin state), frying oil (BI), bakery crust (BI + h°).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Consistency - Textural Factors - Measuring Texture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT753 - FOOD PRODUCTS MONITORING & CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Consistency - Textural Factors - Measuring Texture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining Texture, Consistency & Textural Factors

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Texture (Szczesniak, 1963)**
> Texture is the sensory and functional manifestation of the **structural and mechanical (rheological) properties of food**, perceived through the senses of sight, touch, hearing, and kinesthesia, including the response of the food to applied stress and strain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Consistency**
> Consistency is a **primary textural attribute** that describes the **resistance of a food material to permanent deformation, fracture, or flow** under an applied force. It is a macroscopic property that integrates hardness, viscosity, elasticity, and cohesiveness into a single perceived quality. The standard metric is force (N) or work (J) per unit deformation, measured under defined shear, compression, or penetration modes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Textural Factors (Mechanical Parameters)**
> The mechanical, geometric, and surface characteristics that contribute to overall textural perception. Classified by Szczesniak into four families:
> 1. **Mechanical characteristics** — Hardness, Cohesiveness, Viscosity, Elasticity, Adhesiveness, Brittleness, Chewiness, Gumminess.
> 2. **Geometrical characteristics** — Particle size, shape, orientation.
> 3. **Fat-related characteristics** — Oiliness, greasiness.
> 4. **Moisture-related characteristics** — Wetness, dryness.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sponge vs. Honey" Intuition

Imagine pressing your thumb into two materials sitting on a kitchen table:

- A **dry sponge** resists your thumb, springs back when released, and tears when twisted — it has *high consistency*, high springiness, and high cohesiveness.
- A **drop of honey** flows and never recovers its shape — it has *low consistency* in the solid sense, but high viscosity (resistance to flow).

Foods sit on a spectrum between these extremes. A **ripe banana** is soft, adhesive, and low in springiness (a "cohesive" but deformable system), while a **dry biscuit** is brittle, hard, and fractures sharply under load.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering takeaway:** *Consistency* is the engineering measurement of how a food resists structural change, while *texture* is the holistic sensory impression arising from that resistance pattern.

### 1.3 Why Measure Consistency and Texture?

| Purpose | Industry Relevance |
|---|---|
| **Quality Control** | Ensures batch-to-batch uniformity (e.g., yogurt firmness, ketchup flow). |
| **Shelf-life prediction** | Texture deteriorates predictably with storage time. |
| **Process optimization** | Determines optimum harvest time, mixing time, or extrusion conditions. |
| **Sensory correlation** | Bridges instrumental readings with consumer perception. |
| **Regulatory compliance** | Standards (ISO, ASTM, BIS) for many commodities require texture data. |

The standard SI unit for hardness and most compressive texture parameters is the **Newton (N)**, while work/energy measurements use the **Joule (J)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Texture Profile Analysis (TPA) Framework

The TPA is the **gold standard instrumental method** for quantifying food texture. It mimics the two-bite sensory test by applying two successive compression cycles to a standardized sample and recording the **force–time (or force–distance) curve**.

### 2.2 Operational Logic Steps

1. **Sample conditioning** — Equilibrate the sample to a defined temperature (typically **20 ± 2 °C**) and geometry (cylinders, cubes, or defined volumes).
2. **Probe selection** — Choose a probe (cylindrical, conical, spherical, or blade) based on food structure.
3. **First compression (Bite 1)** — The probe travels down at a constant crosshead speed (typically **1–10 mm/s**) to a predefined strain (often **25–75 %** of original height).
4. **Probe retraction** — Probe returns upward; any *negative* force (pulling the food up with the probe) is recorded as **adhesiveness**.
5. **Second compression (Bite 2)** — Performed after a short delay (typically **5 s**).
6. **Curve analysis** — Software extracts the primary and secondary textural parameters.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulae below are directly from TPA two-bite analysis — these are the **highest-yield derivations** for KTU 2024 ESE.

| Parameter (Symbol) | Definition | Formula | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hardness** (H) | Peak force during the first compression | $H = F_{1}$ | N |
| **Fracturability** (Fr) | Force at the first significant break in the curve | $F_{r} = F_{\text{first peak}}$ (if present) | N |
| **Cohesiveness** (Co) | Ratio of work done in Bite 2 to work done in Bite 1 | $C_{o} = \dfrac{A_{2}}{A_{1}}$ | dimensionless |
| **Adhesiveness** (Ad) | Negative work during probe retraction after Bite 1 | $A_{d} = A_{3}$ | N·s or J |
| **Springiness** (Sp) | Ratio of recovery heights in Bite 2 to Bite 1 | $S_{p} = \dfrac{L_{2}}{L_{1}}$ | dimensionless |
| **Gumminess** (Gu) | Energy required to disintegrate a semi-solid | $G_{u} = H \times C_{o}$ | N |
| **Chewiness** (Ch) | Energy to chew a solid food to a swallowable state | $C_{h} = H \times C_{o} \times S_{p}$ | N |
| **Resilience** (Re) | How well a product "fights back" | $R_{e} = \dfrac{A_{1\text{-}b}}{A_{1\text{-}a}}$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The Kraft shear press (legacy), the Warner–Bratzler shear blade, the Ottawa cell, the Kramer shear cell, and the **Instron Universal Testing Machine** (modern standard) all produce data interpretable through the TPA parameter set.

## 2.4 Complementary Texture Measurement Techniques

| Technique | Principle | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Penetrometry** | Cone/needle penetration under gravity or load | Fruits, gels, butter, jellies |
| **Shear test (Warner–Bratzler)** | Shearing of a cylindrical core | Meat tenderness |
| **Kramer shear cell** | Multi-blade extrusion through a slotted cell | Vegetables, fruits, comminuted meats |
| **Bostwick consistometer** | Distance flow of a paste on an inclined surface | Ketchup, sauces, purees |
| **Rotational viscometry** | Torque on a spindle at defined RPM | Fluids, pastes, slurries |
| **Ottawa cell extrusion** | Piston extrusion of a confined sample | Apples, potatoes, snack pellets |
| **Back extrusion** | Inverse flow measurement | Yoghurt, custards, gels |

### 2.5 Concept Map — What Determines Measured Texture?

A food's measured texture depends on the **interaction of three elements**:

1. **Sample microstructure** — Cell wall composition (pectin, cellulose, lignin), protein matrix (gel strength, denaturation), fat crystal network, moisture binding, starch retrogradation.
2. **Mechanical stimulus** — Type of force (compressive, shear, tensile, penetration), strain rate, magnitude, contact geometry, sample aspect ratio.
3. **Environmental conditions** — Temperature, humidity, holding time, sample geometry, boundary friction.

> [!TIP]
> A *soft* cheese at 4 °C behaves like a rubber; the same cheese at 25 °C flows like a paste. The same chemical formulation yields different texture parameters under different test conditions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Secondary TPA Parameters

The TPA method was first formalized by **Szczesniak, Brandt, and Friedman (1963)** and refined by **Bourne (1978)**. The two-bite curve provides the *primary* (directly measured) parameters — Hardness, Cohesiveness, Adhesiveness, and Springiness. The *secondary* parameters (Chewiness, Gumminess) are mathematical products of the primary ones.

### 3.1.1 Derivation of **Gumminess (Gu)**

**Step 1 — Define the problem.**
Gumminess represents the energy required to disintegrate a *semi-solid* food (e.g., cheese, jam, gummy candy) into a state ready for swallowing. It must combine:
- The force required to deform the material (Hardness, $H$).
- The internal binding energy (Cohesiveness, $C_o$).

**Step 2 — Establish the relationship.**
For a solid, chewiness requires the *elastic recovery* term (springiness) as well; but for a *semi-solid* that does not appreciably spring back, the springiness term is $\approx 1$ and the product reduces to:

$$G_{u} = H \times C_{o}$$

**Step 3 — Dimensional check.**
$[H] = \text{N}$ and $[C_o] = \text{dimensionless}$. Therefore, $[G_u] = \text{N}$, consistent with the definition of gumminess as a *force* (not an energy) for the disintegrated semi-solid state.

**Step 4 — Real-world interpretation.**
For a sample with $H = 8.0$ N and $C_o = 0.65$, the gumminess is:

$$G_{u} = 8.0 \text{ N} \times 0.65 = 5.2 \text{ N}$$

A higher value indicates the food requires more sustained chewing force to break down into a swallowable bolus.

---

### 3.1.2 Derivation of **Chewiness (Ch)**

**Step 1 — Define the problem.**
For a *solid* food (e.g., meat, bread, nuts), the chewing energy must include the elastic recovery component, since the material springs back between bites.

**Step 2 — Combine the three primary parameters.**
The total resistance during the chewing process is the product of the force required (Hardness), the internal binding (Cohesiveness), and the recoverable deformation (Springiness):

$$C_{h} = H \times C_{o} \times S_{p}$$

**Step 3 — Dimensional check.**
$[H] = \text{N}$, $[C_o] = \text{dimensionless}$, $[S_p] = \text{dimensionless}$, so $[C_h] = \text{N}$.

**Step 4 — Worked numerical example.**
For a biscuit sample: $H = 35$ N, $C_o = 0.45$, $S_p = 0.78$.

$$C_{h} = 35 \times 0.45 \times 0.78 = 12.29 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Critical analytical insight.**
Notice the relationship:

$$C_{h} = G_{u} \times S_{p}$$

Hence, **Chewiness = Gumminess × Springiness**. This inter-relationship is a frequently tested KTU concept.

---

### 3.1.3 Derivation of **Cohesiveness (Co) from Curve Areas**

**Step 1 — Identify the curve segments.**

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{1} &= \int_{0}^{L_1} F_{1}(L)\, dL \quad &\text{(work of Bite 1)} \\
A_{2} &= \int_{0}^{L_2} F_{2}(L)\, dL \quad &\text{(work of Bite 2)} \\
A_{3} &= -\int_{\text{retract}} F_{\text{neg}}(L)\, dL \quad &\text{(negative work, adhesiveness)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Define cohesiveness.**
The fraction of the internal binding structure that survives the first compression:

$$C_{o} = \frac{A_{2}}{A_{1}}$$

**Step 3 — Boundary conditions.**
- $C_o = 1$ → perfectly elastic / fully recoverable (e.g., rubbery gel).
- $C_o = 0$ → total structural failure (e.g., brittle cracker after one bite).
- Typical food range: **0.2 ≤ C_o ≤ 0.9**.

**Step 4 — Analytical implication.**
If a fruit is stored too long, pectin depolymerization reduces $A_2$ relative to $A_1$, lowering $C_o$ — the food becomes *crumbly* and *mealy*. This is the classical mechanism behind *mealiness* in over-ripe apples or pears.

---

## 3.2 Penetrometry — Deriving Consistency Index

For penetration tests, the **cone penetrometer** (e.g., ASTM D217, ISO 21357) is widely used. The depth of penetration $d$ is related to the load $P$ applied through a cone of apex angle $\alpha$ by an empirical **flow equation** (a form of the Bingham-plastic analogy).

**Step 1 — Cone resistance equation.**

$$P = K \cdot d^{n}$$

where:
- $P$ = applied load (N),
- $d$ = penetration depth (mm),
- $K$ = consistency index (N·mm$^{-n}$),
- $n$ = flow index (dimensionless).

**Step 2 — Linearize using logarithms.**

$$\log P = \log K + n \log d$$

A plot of $\log P$ vs $\log d$ yields a straight line; the slope is $n$ and the intercept $\log K$.

**Step 3 — Material classification via $n$ and $K$.**

| $n$ value | $K$ value | Material behaviour | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| $n \approx 1.0$ | High $K$ | Ideal plastic / stiff solid | Cold butter, hard cheese |
| $n < 1.0$ | Moderate $K$ | Shear-thinning (pseudoplastic) | Ketchup, yoghurt, paste |
| $n > 1.0$ | Moderate $K$ | Shear-thickening (dilatant) | Wet sand, starch suspensions |
| $n = 1.0$ | Low $K$ | Newtonian fluid | Milk, water |

**Step 4 — Worked example.**
A penetration test on a soft cheese yields: $d_1 = 5$ mm at $P_1 = 2.0$ N; $d_2 = 10$ mm at $P_2 = 4.5$ N.

$$n = \frac{\log(4.5/2.0)}{\log(10/5)} = \frac{\log 2.25}{\log 2.0} = \frac{0.3522}{0.3010} = 1.17$$

$$K = \frac{P}{d^{n}} = \frac{2.0}{5^{1.17}} = \frac{2.0}{7.31} = 0.274 \text{ N·mm}^{-1.17}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
Since $n > 1$, the cheese exhibits **shear-thickening** (dilatant) behaviour under penetration — it *stiffens* as the probe goes deeper, characteristic of a dense, fat-rich semi-solid.

---

## 3.3 Bostwick Consistometer Flow Calculation

The **Bostwick consistometer** measures the distance a viscous product flows down a graduated, slightly inclined channel (typical slope **5 %**) over a defined time (usually 30 s). The flow distance $D$ (cm in 30 s) is the **Bostwick value** — a direct, inverse index of consistency.

$$\text{Consistency Index (CI)} = \frac{1}{D}$$

> [!TIP]
> Smaller $D$ → higher CI → thicker / stiffer product. Ketchup typically has $D \approx 4$–6 cm/30 s; tomato paste has $D \approx 1$–2 cm/30 s.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Two-Bite Texture Profile Analysis (TPA) — Force–Time Curve

Below is the schematic of the canonical TPA force–time curve showing the extraction of all primary and secondary parameters. The diagram maps the *time-domain* interpretation of the measurement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1[Probe contacts sample] --> bite1[First compression begins]
    bite1 --> peak1[Peak force F1 = Hardness]
    peak1 --> fract[Optional first break = Fracturability]
    fract --> area1[Area A1 = Work of Bite 1]
    area1 --> retract[Probe retracts]
    retract --> adhes[Negative area A3 = Adhesiveness]
    adhes --> wait[Hold time 5 s]
    wait --> bite2[Second compression begins]
    bite2 --> peak2[Peak force F2]
    peak2 --> area2[Area A2 = Work of Bite 2]
    area2 --> end1[End of TPA cycle]
    peak1 --> len1[Length L1 measured at peak]
    peak2 --> len2[Length L2 measured at peak]
    area1 --> derived1[Cohesiveness Co = A2 / A1]
    area3ref[Area A3 negative region] --> derived2[Adhesiveness Ad = A3]
    len1 --> derived3[Springiness Sp = L2 / L1]
    peak1 --> derived4[Hardness H = F1]
    derived4 --> derived5[Gumminess Gu = H x Co]
    derived3 --> derived5
    derived4 --> derived6[Chewiness Ch = H x Co x Sp]
    derived1 --> derived6
    derived1 --> derived5
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Instrumental Texture Analyzer

The diagram below maps the *functional architecture* of a modern universal testing machine (e.g., Instron 3343, TA-XT2, Stevens LFRA), illustrating how a mechanical stimulus is converted into quantitative texture parameters.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputBlock[Input Subsystem]
        smp[Standardized food sample]
        probe[Interchangeable probe cylinder cone blade]
        sparam[Set parameters speed strain load cell capacity]
    end
    subgraph mechBlock[Mechanical Subsystem]
        loadcell[Load cell force transducer 0 to 500 N]
        crosshead[Motorised crosshead drive]
        frame[Rigid test frame]
    end
    subgraph daqBlock[Data Acquisition Subsystem]
        adc[Analog to digital converter 16 bit]
        timing[Time base and distance encoder]
    end
    subgraph procBlock[Processing Subsystem]
        curve[Force time curve reconstruction]
        tpapkg[TPA parameter extraction module]
        stats[Statistical analysis and reporting]
    end
    subgraph outputBlock[Output Subsystem]
        disp[Real time force distance display]
        report[Hardness Cohesiveness Springiness Chewiness Gumminess values]
        export[CSV and PDF report export]
    end
    sparam --> crosshead
    smp --> frame
    probe --> loadcell
    crosshead --> loadcell
    loadcell --> adc
    crosshead --> timing
    timing --> adc
    adc --> curve
    curve --> tpapkg
    tpapkg --> stats
    stats --> disp
    stats --> report
    stats --> export
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Selecting the Right Texture Measurement Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{Is the sample a free flowing liquid}
    q1 -- yes --> pathV[Use rotational viscometer Brookfield Haake]
    q1 -- no --> q2{Is the sample a paste or sauce}
    q2 -- yes --> q3{Is yield stress important}
    q3 -- yes --> pathB[Use Bostwick consistometer]
    q3 -- no --> pathP[Use penetrometer]
    q2 -- no --> q4{Is the sample a soft solid gel or fruit}
    q4 -- yes --> q5{Is chewiness or springiness required}
    q5 -- yes --> pathTPA[Use TPA two bite compression test]
    q5 -- no --> pathPen[Use single bite penetration test]
    q4 -- no --> q6{Is the sample a tough solid like meat}
    q6 -- yes --> q7{Is shearing or cutting the dominant failure}
    q7 -- shearing --> pathWB[Use Warner Bratzler shear blade]
    q7 -- multi directional --> pathK[Use Kramer shear cell]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Block `tpaPkg` is the *mathematical kernel* of any modern texture analyzer. Every other block (mechanical, electrical, software) exists to feed the TPA module with clean, sampled force–time data.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Series-2]`

**Define texture. Classify textural factors according to Szczesniak and give two examples for each category.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Texture is the sensory and functional manifestation of the structural and mechanical (rheological) properties of food, perceived through the senses of touch, sight, sound, and kinesthesia (Szczesniak, 1963).

**Szczesniak's Classification of Textural Factors:**

| Category | Parameters | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical characteristics** | Hardness, Cohesiveness, Viscosity, Elasticity, Adhesiveness, Brittleness, Chewiness, Gumminess | Hard candy = hard, brittle; Cheese = chewy, cohesive |
| **Geometrical characteristics** | Particle size, shape, orientation | Granularity of sugar crystals, fibrousness of celery |
| **Fat-related characteristics** | Oiliness, greasiness | Greasy feel of french fries |
| **Moisture-related characteristics** | Wetness, dryness | Juiciness of mango, dryness of biscuits |

*(2 marks for classification table, 1 mark for definition — any 2 examples per category is sufficient.)*

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Series-1]`

**Distinguish between *hardness*, *cohesiveness*, and *adhesiveness* with a food example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Definition | Curve Location | Food Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hardness** | The force required to bite through or compress a food to a given deformation. | Peak force $F_1$ in Bite 1 of TPA. | A raw carrot has high hardness; a ripe banana has low hardness. |
| **Cohesiveness** | The extent to which a food sample holds together; ratio of work of Bite 2 to Bite 1 ($A_2/A_1$). | Derived from areas under the two compression peaks. | Cheese is cohesive; a brittle cracker is non-cohesive. |
| **Adhesiveness** | The work required to overcome the attractive forces between the food and the probe (or palate). | Negative area $A_3$ during probe retraction. | Honey sticks to the spoon; peanut butter clings to the teeth. |

*(1 mark per parameter — definition + curve location + example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulation, every Module 1 question must offer an internal choice. **Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the Texture Profile Analysis (TPA) method in detail. Sketch the typical two-bite force–time curve and label the key parameters. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Texture Profile Analysis, formalized by Szczesniak, Brandt, and Friedman (1963) and standardized by Bourne (1978), is an instrumental double-compression test that mimics the human two-bite sensory process and quantifies multiple textural parameters from a single force–time record.

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. The sample is equilibrated to a defined temperature (typically **20 ± 2 °C**) and cut to a defined geometry (cube or cylinder).
2. A standardized probe is mounted on the load cell of a Universal Testing Machine (UTM) such as the **Instron 3343** or **TA-XT2i**.
3. The probe descends at a constant crosshead speed (typically **1–10 mm·s⁻¹**) to a target strain (commonly **25–75 %** of the original sample height) — this is **Bite 1**.
4. The probe retracts; the negative force area during withdrawal quantifies *adhesiveness*.
5. After a holding time (typically **5 s**), the probe descends again to perform **Bite 2**.
6. The full force–time (or force–distance) curve is digitized and analyzed.

**Two-bite Curve — Labelled Description (2 marks):**
The force–time curve shows two distinct compression peaks. The first peak force is the **Hardness (H)**. The area under the first peak ($A_1$) is the work of Bite 1. The area under the second peak ($A_2$) is the work of Bite 2. The horizontal distance to the maximum of the first peak is $L_1$ and to the maximum of the second peak is $L_2$. The negative area beneath the baseline during probe retraction is $A_3$ (Adhesiveness).

**Key Parameters Extracted (1 mark):**
Hardness ($F_1$), Cohesiveness ($A_2/A_1$), Adhesiveness ($A_3$), Springiness ($L_2/L_1$).

---

**(b) Derive the expressions for *Chewiness* and *Gumminess* from primary TPA parameters. A biscuit sample gave the following TPA data: $H = 40$ N, $A_1 = 250$ N·mm, $A_2 = 100$ N·mm, $L_1 = 8$ mm, $L_2 = 6$ mm. Calculate the Cohesiveness, Springiness, Gumminess, and Chewiness of the sample. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation of Gumminess (3 marks):**
For a *semi-solid* food, the energy required to disintegrate the material into a swallowable state is the product of the peak force (hardness) and the internal binding (cohesiveness). The springiness term is approximately unity because semi-solids do not appreciably recover. Therefore:

$$G_{u} = H \times C_{o}$$

Dimensional check: $[H] = \text{N}$, $[C_o] = \text{dimensionless}$, so $[G_u] = \text{N}$. Gumminess is expressed in Newtons.

**Derivation of Chewiness (3 marks):**
For a *solid* food, the elastic recovery (springiness) is non-trivial and must be included. Chewiness is the total chewing work:

$$C_{h} = H \times C_{o} \times S_{p} = G_{u} \times S_{p}$$

Dimensional check: $[C_h] = \text{N} \times \text{dimensionless} = \text{N}$.

**Numerical Calculation (1 mark):**

$$C_{o} = \frac{A_{2}}{A_{1}} = \frac{100}{250} = 0.40$$

$$S_{p} = \frac{L_{2}}{L_{1}} = \frac{6}{8} = 0.75$$

$$G_{u} = 40 \times 0.40 = 16.0 \text{ N}$$

$$C_{h} = 40 \times 0.40 \times 0.75 = 12.0 \text{ N}$$

**Final Answer:** Co = 0.40, Sp = 0.75, Gu = 16.0 N, Ch = 12.0 N.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Discuss the various instrumental methods used for measuring the texture of food products, with emphasis on the penetrometer and the Bostwick consistometer. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Texture measurement methods are classified into *sensory*, *instrumental-imitative*, and *fundamental* methods. Instrumental methods quantify the mechanical response of food to defined stress.

**Penetrometer Method (3 marks):**
The penetrometer measures the depth of penetration of a standardized cone, needle, or plunger into a food under a defined load (e.g., **100 g for 5 s**). The depth in 0.1 mm units is the *penetration value*. The penetration depth follows the empirical equation:

$$P = K \cdot d^{n}$$

where $K$ is the **consistency index** and $n$ is the **flow index**. By taking logarithms, $\log P = \log K + n \log d$, a linear regression yields $K$ and $n$. Used for **fruits (apples, pears), butter, cheese, gels, and waxes**. Standards include **ASTM D217** and **ISO 21357**.

**Bostwick Consistometer (2 marks):**
The Bostwick consistometer consists of a graduated, slightly inclined channel (5 % slope). A fixed volume of sample is released at one end, and the distance $D$ (cm) it travels in **30 seconds** is recorded. The **Bostwick value** is inversely related to consistency:

$$\text{Consistency Index} = \frac{1}{D}$$

Used for **ketchup, sauces, purees, tomato pastes, and viscous condiments**.

**Other Methods (1 mark):**
Warner–Bratzler shear blade (meat), Kramer shear cell (multi-blade extrusion), Ottawa cell (extrusion), Brookfield rotational viscometer (fluids), back-extrusion cell (yoghurt, gels).

---

**(b) What is a cone penetrometer test? How is the consistency index ($K$) and flow index ($n$) determined experimentally? The following data are obtained for a food paste at two penetration depths: at $d_1 = 4$ mm, $P_1 = 1.5$ N; at $d_2 = 9$ mm, $P_2 = 4.0$ N. Calculate $K$ and $n$ and comment on the type of flow behaviour. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Theory (3 marks):**
A cone penetrometer measures the resistance of a food material to penetration by a standardized cone of apex angle (commonly 30° or 45°). The load $P$ vs depth $d$ relationship follows a power law:

$$P = K \cdot d^{n}$$

To obtain $K$ and $n$, take logarithms of both sides:

$$\log P = \log K + n \log d$$

Plotting $\log P$ on the y-axis against $\log d$ on the x-axis yields a straight line; the slope is $n$ and the intercept on the y-axis is $\log K$.

**Calculation of $n$ (2 marks):**

$$n = \frac{\log(P_{2}/P_{1})}{\log(d_{2}/d_{1})} = \frac{\log(4.0/1.5)}{\log(9/4)} = \frac{\log 2.6667}{\log 2.25} = \frac{0.4260}{0.3522} = 1.21$$

**Calculation of $K$ (1 mark):**

$$K = \frac{P_{1}}{d_{1}^{n}} = \frac{1.5}{4^{1.21}} = \frac{1.5}{5.946} = 0.2523 \text{ N·mm}^{-1.21}$$

**Comment on Flow Behaviour (1 mark):**
Since $n = 1.21 > 1$, the food paste exhibits **shear-thickening (dilatant) flow behaviour** — its resistance increases with penetration depth, characteristic of dense suspensions of deformable particles (e.g., starch slurries, certain fruit pulps).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Texture & Consistency Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing units.** Hardness, Gumminess, and Chewiness are in **N (force)**, not energy (J). Adhesiveness is in **N·s or J (work)**. Examiners award zero if a student uses N·s for hardness.
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign in Adhesiveness.** It is the *magnitude* of the negative area. Writing $-A_3$ in tables is an automatic 0.5-mark deduction.
> 3. **Wrong classification of Chewiness vs Gumminess.** If the question explicitly says *solid* (e.g., meat, bread), you MUST include the springiness term. If *semi-solid* (e.g., cheese, jam), it reduces to hardness × cohesiveness.
> 4. **Mislabelling the Bostwick output.** The Bostwick value $D$ is the *flow distance*, NOT the consistency. Higher $D$ means LOWER consistency. Examiners deduct 1 mark for confusing these.
> 5. **Skipping the dimensional check in derivations.** KTU 2024 marking scheme allocates marks explicitly for verifying $[H] = \text{N}$. A derivation without dimensional analysis loses the "validation" mark.
> 6. **Drawing the TPA curve without units on the axes.** Every axis in your TPA sketch must specify **Force (N)** on y-axis and **Time (s) or Distance (mm)** on x-axis.
> 7. **Mixing up Cone and Needle penetrometers.** Cones are used for *plastic* foods (butter, cheese); needles are used for *firm* foods (fruits, gels).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Texture & Consistency**

- ✅ **Texture** = sensory + mechanical manifestation; classified by **Szczesniak (1963)** into 4 families: *mechanical, geometrical, fat, moisture*.
- ✅ **Consistency** = resistance to permanent deformation; a *quantitative* textural parameter, not synonymous with texture.
- ✅ **TPA primary parameters** (4): Hardness ($F_1$), Cohesiveness ($A_2/A_1$), Adhesiveness ($A_3$), Springiness ($L_2/L_1$).
- ✅ **TPA secondary parameters** (3): Brittleness, Gumminess ($H \cdot C_o$), Chewiness ($H \cdot C_o \cdot S_p$).
- ✅ **Hardness, Gumminess, Chewiness → units of N (force)**, NOT energy.
- ✅ **Adhesiveness → N·s or J (energy)**, magnitude of *negative* area.
- ✅ **Cohesiveness range in real foods: 0.2 to 0.9**; $C_o = 1$ = ideal elastic.
- ✅ **Cone penetrometer equation**: $P = K \cdot d^{n}$ ; linearize via $\log P = \log K + n \log d$.
- ✅ **Flow behaviour classification**: $n < 1$ = pseudoplastic (shear-thinning); $n = 1$ = Newtonian / plastic; $n > 1$ = dilatant (shear-thickening).
- ✅ **Bostwick consistometer** = inclined-channel flow; **Bostwick value $D$ = flow distance in cm / 30 s**; **Consistency Index = 1/D**.
- ✅ **Kraft shear press** = legacy piston-extrusion device for fruits/vegetables.
- ✅ **Warner–Bratzler shear blade** = standard for meat tenderness.
- ✅ **Kramer shear cell** = multi-blade extrusion for comminuted samples.
- ✅ **Brookfield viscometer** = rotational spindle viscometer for fluids/pastes.
- ✅ **Chewiness = Gumminess × Springiness** — frequently tested algebraic relationship.
- ✅ **Factors affecting measured texture**: (1) sample microstructure, (2) mechanical stimulus geometry, (3) environmental conditions (T, t).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Texture Changes in Food

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
*Texture* in food science is defined as the **rheological and structural property of a food product perceptible by the senses of touch, sight, and hearing**, encompassing attributes such as hardness, cohesiveness, viscosity, elasticity, adhesiveness, brittleness, chewiness, and gumminess. *Texture changes* refer to the **time-dependent modifications in the mechanical and structural characteristics of food matrices** occurring during processing, storage, or mastication.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition:** Texture is a *multi-parameter attribute* and is classified under the **Sensory Quality Parameters** along with appearance, flavour, and nutritional value. The **Standard Reference Material (SRM)** for texture profiling is the *Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)* method developed by **Szczesniak (1963)**.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for Students):**
Imagine biting into a fresh *idli* versus a stale one. The fresh idli is **soft, springy, and moist** (high elasticity + cohesiveness), while the stale one becomes **hard, crumbly, and dry** (low elasticity, high hardness). This difference — purely mechanical, no chemical change in flavour — is a *texture change*. In engineering terms, the *Young's Modulus (E)* of the food matrix has changed due to moisture loss and starch retrogradation. Texture is essentially the "**mouthfeel engineering**" of food.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Texture Matters in KTU Board Exams:** Food processors lose **billions of dollars annually** worldwide due to texture defects. For Module 1, students must distinguish between **objective texture** (measured by instruments) and **subjective texture** (measured by humans/sensory panels).

---

## 1.2 Flavour Factors

**Formal Definition:**
*Flavour* is the **combined sensory impression of a food substance as perceived by the chemoreceptors of the taste buds (gustation) and olfactory receptors (olfaction), supplemented by tactile, thermal, and pain sensations** in the oral and nasal cavities (KTU-aligned ISO 5492 definition).

Flavour is a **composite sensation** with five sub-modalities:

| Sub-modality | Receptor Site | Stimulus Type | Example Compounds |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Taste (Gustation)** | Tongue (Taste Buds) | Chemical (water-soluble) | Sugars, salts, acids, alkaloids |
| **Smell (Olfaction)** | Nasal epithelium | Chemical (volatile) | Esters, aldehydes, ketones |
| **Mouthfeel (Trigeminal)** | Oral mucosa | Mechanical / chemical | Capsaicin (chilli), menthol |
| **Temperature** | Thermoreceptors | Thermal | Hot soup, cold ice cream |
| **Pain (Chemesthesis)** | Nerve endings | Irritant | Carbonation, pungency |

**Intuition:** Flavour is like an **orchestra**. Taste is the *rhythm section* (basic, only 5 notes: sweet, sour, salty, bitter, umami), while aroma is the *melody* (thousands of volatile compounds). A chef balances both — remove one and the food feels "flat" even if the other is perfect.

---

## 1.3 Influence of Colour and Texture on Flavour

**Formal Definition:**
The **cross-modal interaction** by which non-chemosensory attributes (colour, texture, sound, visual cues) **enhance, suppress, or alter the perceived intensity and qualitative character of flavour** in food products.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:** This falls under the **Psychophysics of Flavour Perception** — a key area tested frequently.

**Key Phenomena:**
1. **Colour-Flavour Congruence:** A *red* drink is perceived as sweeter than a *green* one (even with identical sugar content).
2. **Texture-Induced Flavour Release:** Thick foods (high viscosity) release aroma compounds *slower* than thin foods, altering the *temporal profile* of flavour perception.
3. **Visual Texture Cues:** A *glossy* chocolate is perceived as creamier than a *matte* one of identical composition.

---

## 1.4 Taste Panels (Sensory Evaluation)

**Formal Definition:**
A *Taste Panel* (or **Sensory Panel**) is a **pre-selected, trained (or untrained, depending on test type) group of human assessors** who evaluate food products under **standardized conditions** to quantify sensory attributes using established statistical protocols (ISO, ASTM, KTU-referenced standards).

**Intuition:** Think of a taste panel as a **"biological instrument"** — calibrated human senses that provide data no machine can replicate for hedonic and complex sensory attributes.

**Types of Sensory Tests (KTU 2024 Module 1 Syllabus):**

| Test Type | Panel | Output | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Discriminative** | Trained (15–30) | Statistical difference | Triangle test, duo-trio |
| **Descriptive** | Highly trained (8–12) | Quantitative profile | Quantitative Descriptive Analysis (QDA) |
| **Affective / Hedonic** | Consumer (50–100+) | Preference / liking | 9-point hedonic scale |

---

## 1.5 Simulation Modelling

**Formal Definition:**
*Simulation Modelling* in food process engineering is the **mathematical and computational representation of a food system or process**, using systems of differential equations, mass/energy balances, and kinetic models to **predict the behaviour of the real system under varying operating conditions without physical experimentation**.

**Intuition:** A simulation model is a **"flight simulator for food engineers"** — instead of running costly pilot-plant trials, we model the process mathematically and test thousands of scenarios on a computer.

---

## 1.6 Process and Product Optimization

**Formal Definition:**
*Process Optimization* is the **systematic procedure of finding the values of process variables (temperature, pressure, time, flow rate, ingredient ratios) that maximize or minimize a defined objective function** (e.g., yield, quality, cost) **subject to constraints** (e.g., regulatory limits, equipment capacity, safety).

**General Mathematical Form:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Optimize:} \quad & f(\mathbf{x}) \\
\text{Subject to:} \quad & g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, m \\
& h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, p \\
& x_i^{L} \le x_i \le x_i^{U}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T$ is the vector of decision variables, $f(\mathbf{x})$ is the **objective function**, $g_j$ are inequality constraints, and $h_k$ are equality constraints.

---

## 1.7 Optimization Procedures (Methods)

**Classification (KTU 2024):**

1. **Classical Methods** — Calculus-based, for continuous differentiable functions.
   - Single-variable: $df/dx = 0$
   - Multi-variable: $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{0}$
2. **Numerical (Direct Search) Methods** — No derivatives needed; iterate to optimum.
   - Simplex, Hooke-Jeeves, Powell's, Rosenbrock
3. **Statistical / Stochastic Methods** — Use probabilistic rules.
   - RSM, Genetic Algorithms, Simulated Annealing, Particle Swarm

---

## 1.8 Search Methods

**Formal Definition:**
*Search Methods* are **iterative numerical algorithms that generate a sequence of candidate solutions** $\mathbf{x}^{(0)}, \mathbf{x}^{(1)}, \ldots, \mathbf{x}^{(k)}$ **approaching the optimum of an objective function**, using only function evaluations or gradient information.

**Two Principal Categories:**

| Category | Information Used | Convergence Speed | Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct (Zero-order)** | $f(\mathbf{x})$ only | Slow | Noisy / non-smooth |
| **Gradient (First/Second-order)** | $\nabla f$ or Hessian | Fast | Smooth, differentiable |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** For **food process optimization**, where objective functions are often **empirical (noisy, derived from RSM)**, *Direct Search Methods* (Hooke-Jeeves, Nelder-Mead Simplex) are preferred over gradient methods.

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Texture Changes — Engineering Analysis

Texture evolution in food is governed by **kinetic transformations** analogous to chemical reactions. The classic model is the **first-order kinetic texture softening model**:

$$
A(t) = A_\infty + (A_0 - A_\infty)\, e^{-k\, t}
$$

Where:
- $A(t)$ = texture attribute (e.g., hardness, **N**) at time $t$
- $A_0$ = initial texture value
- $A_\infty$ = equilibrium (asymptotic) texture value
- $k$ = rate constant ($\text{min}^{-1}$ or $\text{h}^{-1}$)
- $t$ = processing / storage time

**Why does this matter?** During thermal processing (e.g., blanching of vegetables), pectin and starch structures break down following pseudo-first-order kinetics. By fitting $k$ to experimental TPA data, food engineers **predict shelf-life** and **design retort schedules**.

**Bulk Modulus (B)** and **Shear Modulus (G)** for solid-like foods (gels, cheese):

$$
G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma}, \quad B = -V \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta V}
$$

**Apparent Viscosity (Newtonian Foods):**

$$
\eta_{app} = \frac{\sigma}{\dot{\gamma}}
$$

For **non-Newtonian foods** (most sauces, purees), the **Power Law (Ostwald-de Waele)** model applies:

$$
\sigma = K\, \dot{\gamma}^{n}
$$

Where $K$ = consistency index (Pa·sⁿ) and $n$ = flow behaviour index (dimensionless).

---

## 2.2 Flavour Factors — Detailed Analysis

### 2.2.1 Five Basic Tastes (KTU Mandatory Knowledge)

| Taste | L-amino acid / ion | Threshold (mg/L) | Food Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sweet | Sugars, aspartame, saccharin | varies | Sugar, honey |
| Sour | H⁺ ions | $10^{-4.5}$ M | Lemon, vinegar |
| Salty | Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻ | ~ 1000 (NaCl) | Table salt |
| Bitter | Alkaloids, Mg²⁺, Ca²⁺ | very low | Quinine, caffeine |
| Umami | **L-Glutamate** (MSG), 5'-IMP, 5'-GMP | ~ 30 (MSG) | Tomato, cheese, mushrooms |

### 2.2.2 Aroma (Olfaction)

**Henry's Law governs volatile release in liquid foods:**

$$
C_{gas} = K_H \cdot C_{aq}
$$

Where $K_H$ = Henry's constant (Pa·m³/mol), $C_{aq}$ = aqueous concentration.

**Effect of Viscosity on Flavour Release — Stokes-Einstein Diffusion:**

$$
D = \frac{k_B T}{6 \pi \eta r_s}
$$

Where:
- $D$ = diffusion coefficient of aroma molecule (m²/s)
- $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant (**1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $\eta$ = viscosity of food matrix (Pa·s)
- $r_s$ = hydrodynamic radius of aroma molecule (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Higher $\eta$ → lower $D$ → **slower aroma release** → perceived as *flatter / muted* flavour. This is why a thick milkshake tastes "less flavourful" than thin milk — same volatile concentration!

---

## 2.3 Influence of Colour and Texture on Flavour — Psychophysics

The **Colour-Flavor Congruence Effect** is quantified using **Weber-Fechner's Law:**

$$
P = k \log \frac{I}{I_0}
$$

Where:
- $P$ = perceived intensity
- $I$ = stimulus magnitude
- $I_0$ = detection threshold
- $k$ = Weber-Fechner constant

**Red Colour Index (RCI)** correlates with sweetness perception in beverages. Studies show ~**15–20%** perceived sweetness enhancement when beverage is coloured red vs. green (same sugar content).

---

## 2.4 Taste Panels — Statistical Framework

**Discriminative Test — Triangle Test (Probability of Correct Judgment):**

$$
P(\text{correct}) = \frac{1}{3} + \frac{2}{3}\cdot p_d
$$

Where $p_d = p_A^{2/3}(1-p_A)^{1/3} + (1-p_A)^{2/3} p_A^{1/3}$ for a 2-out-of-3 forced choice.

**Hedonic Score (9-Point Box Scale):** Mean $\bar{x}$ and ANOVA-based significance.

**Number of Assessors for Triangle Test (KTU Exam Favourite):**

$$
n = \frac{Z_{\alpha/2}^{2} \cdot p (1-p)}{E^{2}}
$$

Where $Z_{\alpha/2}$ is the standard normal critical value, $p$ = proportion correct, $E$ = allowable error.

---

## 2.5 Simulation Modelling — Mathematical Foundation

**General Mass Balance on a Food Process:**

$$
\frac{dm}{dt} = \dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out} + \dot{m}_{gen} - \dot{m}_{cons}
$$

**Energy Balance:**

$$
\frac{dE}{dt} = \dot{Q} - \dot{W} + \dot{m}_{in} h_{in} - \dot{m}_{out} h_{out}
$$

**Heat Transfer in Food (Fourier's Law):**

$$
q = -k \nabla T
$$

**Moisture Transport (Fick's Law):**

$$
J = -D \nabla C_w
$$

---

## 2.6 Process & Product Optimization — Formula Sheet

| Equation / Concept | Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Objective Function** | $f(\mathbf{x})$ | Maximize yield, minimize cost |
| **Gradient (Necessary Condition)** | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ | Local optimum |
| **Hessian (Sufficient Condition)** | $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*) \succ 0$ | Local **minimum** |
| **Hessian Negative Definite** | $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*) \prec 0$ | Local **maximum** |
| **Lagrangian** | $\mathcal{L} = f + \sum \lambda_j g_j$ | Constrained problems |
| **KKT Conditions** | $\nabla f + \sum \lambda_j \nabla g_j = 0$, $\lambda_j \ge 0$, $\lambda_j g_j = 0$ | Optimality with inequality constraints |
| **Response Surface (2nd-order)** | $y = \beta_0 + \sum \beta_i x_i + \sum \beta_{ii} x_i^2 + \sum \beta_{ij} x_i x_j$ | RSM fitting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked to "state the KKT conditions," always mention **three things**: stationarity, primal feasibility, and complementary slackness. Missing any one loses marks.

---

## 2.7 Optimization Procedures & Search Methods — Formula Sheet

### 2.7.1 Hooke-Jeeves Direct Search (Pattern Search)

The **exploratory move** around current point $\mathbf{x}^{(k)}$:

$$
x_i^{(k,1)} = x_i^{(k)} + \Delta_i, \quad x_i^{(k,2)} = x_i^{(k)} - \Delta_i
$$

If $f$ improves, the **pattern move** is taken:

$$
\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k,1)} + \left(\mathbf{x}^{(k,1)} - \mathbf{x}^{(k)}\right)
$$

Step size reduction: $\Delta_i^{(k+1)} = \rho \Delta_i^{(k)}$, with $\rho \in (0, 1)$ (typically $\rho = 0.5$).

### 2.7.2 Nelder-Mead Simplex Method

Update rule for the simplex centroid and reflection/expansion/contraction:

$$
\mathbf{x}_c = \frac{1}{n+1} \sum_{i \neq h} \mathbf{x}_i
$$

$$
\mathbf{x}_r = \mathbf{x}_c + \alpha(\mathbf{x}_c - \mathbf{x}_h), \quad \alpha = 1
$$

| Operation | Coefficient | Condition |
|---|---|---|
| Reflection | $\alpha = 1$ | Trial test |
| Expansion | $\gamma = 2$ | If $f(\mathbf{x}_r) < f(\mathbf{x}_l)$ |
| Contraction | $\rho = 0.5$ | If $f(\mathbf{x}_r) > f(\mathbf{x}_h)$ |
| Shrink | $\sigma = 0.5$ | Worst case |

### 2.7.3 Gradient Descent (Steepest Descent)

$$
\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} - \alpha_k \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})
$$

Where $\alpha_k$ is the step size (often by line search).

### 2.7.4 Newton's Method

$$
\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} - \mathbf{H}^{-1}(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}) \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})
$$

Requires Hessian $\mathbf{H}$ — expensive but **quadratic convergence**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Food Engineering Applications (RSM-based):** Newton is rarely used because the empirical model gives a **closed-form quadratic** response — optimum is obtained by solving $\partial y / \partial x_i = 0$ directly using $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of First-Order Texture Softening Kinetics

**Starting Postulate:** The rate of change of a texture attribute $A$ is proportional to its remaining distance from the equilibrium value $A_\infty$.

**Step 1:** Write the rate equation.
$$
\frac{dA}{dt} = -k\,(A - A_\infty)
$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange for separation of variables.
$$
\frac{dA}{A - A_\infty} = -k\, dt
$$

**Step 3:** Integrate both sides from $t = 0$ (where $A = A_0$) to time $t$ (where $A = A(t)$).
$$
\int_{A_0}^{A(t)} \frac{dA}{A - A_\infty} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the integrals.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\left[ \ln \vert A - A_\infty \vert \right]_{A_0}^{A(t)} &= -k\,t \\
\ln \frac{A(t) - A_\infty}{A_0 - A_\infty} &= -k\,t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Exponentiate to obtain the final form.
$$
A(t) - A_\infty = (A_0 - A_\infty)\, e^{-k t}
$$

**Step 6:** Solve explicitly for $A(t)$.
$$
\boxed{\,A(t) = A_\infty + (A_0 - A_\infty)\, e^{-k\,t}\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** At $t \to \infty$, $A \to A_\infty$ (softening plateau); at $t = 0$, $A = A_0$ (initial firmness).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Optimum for a 2-Variable Quadratic Response Surface Model

**Standard RSM Quadratic Form:**

$$
y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \beta_{11} x_1^{2} + \beta_{22} x_2^{2} + \beta_{12} x_1 x_2 + \varepsilon
$$

**Step 1 — Necessary Condition (Stationary Point):** Take partial derivatives and set to zero.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial y}{\partial x_1} &= \beta_1 + 2 \beta_{11} x_1 + \beta_{12} x_2 = 0 \\
\frac{\partial y}{\partial x_2} &= \beta_2 + 2 \beta_{22} x_2 + \beta_{12} x_1 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Matrix Formulation:** Write the system in matrix form.
$$
\begin{bmatrix}
2\beta_{11} & \beta_{12} \\
\beta_{12} & 2\beta_{22}
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
x_1 \\
x_2
\end{bmatrix}
=
-\begin{bmatrix}
\beta_1 \\
\beta_2
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the Hessian Matrix $\mathbf{B}$.**
$$
\mathbf{B} =
\begin{bmatrix}
2\beta_{11} & \beta_{12} \\
\beta_{12} & 2\beta_{22}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve using Matrix Inversion.**
$$
\begin{bmatrix}
x_1 \\
x_2
\end{bmatrix}
=
-\mathbf{B}^{-1}
\begin{bmatrix}
\beta_1 \\
\beta_2
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Explicit Inverse.** Provided $\det(\mathbf{B}) = 4\beta_{11}\beta_{22} - \beta_{12}^{2} \ne 0$:
$$
\mathbf{B}^{-1} = \frac{1}{4\beta_{11}\beta_{22} - \beta_{12}^{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}
2\beta_{22} & -\beta_{12} \\
-\beta_{12} & 2\beta_{11}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 — Closed-Form Optimum.**
$$
\boxed{\,x_1^{*} = \frac{2\beta_{11}\beta_2 - \beta_{1}\beta_{12}}{\beta_{12}^{2} - 4\beta_{11}\beta_{22}}, \quad x_2^{*} = \frac{2\beta_{22}\beta_1 - \beta_{2}\beta_{12}}{\beta_{12}^{2} - 4\beta_{11}\beta_{22}}\,}
$$

**Step 7 — Classify Optimum by Hessian Signs:**
- If $\vert \mathbf{B} \vert > 0$ and $\beta_{11} > 0$ → **minimum**
- If $\vert \mathbf{B} \vert > 0$ and $\beta_{11} < 0$ → **maximum**
- If $\vert \mathbf{B} \vert < 0$ → **saddle point** (no optimum)

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Hooke-Jeeves Search

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x_1, x_2) = (x_1 - 2)^{2} + (x_2 - 3)^{2}$. Start at $\mathbf{x}^{(0)} = (0, 0)$, step size $\Delta = 1$, $\rho = 0.5$.

**Iteration 1:**
- Exploratory moves around $(0,0)$: Try $(1,0)$ → $f = 5$; Try $(-1,0)$ → $f = 13$. Take $(1,0)$ (improvement).
- Try $(1,1)$ → $f = 5$ — same, keep.
- Try $(1,-1)$ → $f = 17$ — worse.
- Best base point: $\mathbf{x}^{(0,1)} = (1, 0)$, $f = 5$.
- Pattern move: $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(0,1)} + [\mathbf{x}^{(0,1)} - \mathbf{x}^{(0)}] = (2, 0)$, $f = 1$.

**Iteration 2:**
- Exploratory moves around $(2,0)$: Try $(3,0)$ → $f = 1$ (tie); try $(1,0)$ → $f = 5$. Keep $(3,0)$.
- Try $(3, 1)$ → $f = 5$ (worse than current 1, so keep).
- Best base: $\mathbf{x}^{(1,1)} = (3, 0)$, $f = 1$.
- Pattern move: $\mathbf{x}^{(2)} = (3,0) + [(3,0) - (2,0)] = (4, 0)$, $f = 5$.

**Iteration 3:** Continue pattern → approaching $(2, 3)$. The known minimum $f(2,3) = 0$ is reached after successive step reductions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Style:** Students often lose marks by forgetting to *reduce the step size* $\Delta$ when the exploratory move fails. Always remember the $\rho$ update rule.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cross-Modal Flavour Influence

| Sensory Input | Affects | Mechanism | Magnitude (perception shift) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Red colour** | Sweetness perception | Learned association | +15–20% |
| **Green colour** | Sourness / bitterness | Learned association | +10–15% |
| **High viscosity** | Aroma intensity | Reduced diffusion ($D \downarrow$) | –10–30% |
| **Crispy sound** | Freshness perception | Temporal coupling | +20–25% |
| **Glossy surface** | Caloric / creamy perception | Visual fat cue | +12–18% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Hook:** Questions on *cross-modal perception* often appear as **"Discuss how colour influences flavour perception in processed foods."** Always cite the **Weber-Fechner Law** and **learned-association hypothesis**.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define texture and list any six textural attributes measured in the Texture Profile Analysis (TPA) method.**

**Model Answer:**
Texture is the rheological and structural attribute of a food perceptible through mechanical, tactile, and visual senses. The six textural attributes measured in TPA are:
1. **Hardness** — peak force during first compression (N).
2. **Cohesiveness** — ratio of areas under second to first compression peaks (dimensionless).
3. **Springiness** — height recovery after compression (mm or s).
4. **Adhesiveness** — negative force area during probe withdrawal (N·s).
5. **Chewiness** — Hardness × Cohesiveness × Springiness (N).
6. **Gumminess** — Hardness × Cohesiveness (N).
7. **Resilience** — instantaneous recovery on partial compression.

**(3 marks — full answer)**

---
