Together, the system tells you **WHAT** is in the food (identification by $m/z$) and **HOW MUCH** is present (quantification by ion intensity). > [!NOTE] > **Key Performance Metrics in Bold:** > - **Detection Limit (LOD):** typically in the **parts-per-trillion (ppt)** to **parts-per-billion (ppb)** range > - **Mass Accuracy:** modern instruments achieve **< 5 ppm** with high-resolution analyzers > - **Mass Range:** up to **m/z 60,000** (for biomolecules) on standard LC-MS systems ### 1.3 Why LC-MS Over GC-MS for Food Analysis? | Criterion | GC-MS | LC-MS | |---|---|---| | Analyte Polarity | Non-polar, volatile | Polar, non-volatile, thermolabile | | Derivatization | Often required | Not required | | Molecular Weight | < 500 Da | Up to 100,000+ Da | | Food Applications | Fatty acids, pesticides (volatile) | Mycotoxins, peptides, dyes, polar pesticides | ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Two Major Subsystems — Operational Logic The LC-MS workflow is a sequential cascade governed by the principle **"separate first, identify later."** **A. Liquid Chromatography (LC) Subsystem** 1. **Mobile Phase Delivery:** A high-pressure pump (typically operating at **4000–15,000 psi**) drives a binary or ternary solvent mixture (water + acetonitrile/methanol + modifiers like formic acid or ammonium acetate) through the column. 2. **Sample Injection:** The food extract (typically 1–50 $\mu$L) is injected via an autosampler into the mobile phase stream. 3. **Separation Column:** A C18 (octadecyl) reversed-phase column (length 50–250 mm, internal diameter 2.1–4.6 mm, particle size 1.7–5 $\mu$m) is the workhorse. Analytes partition between the hydrophobic stationary phase and the polar mobile phase. 4. **Elution:** Compounds elute at characteristic retention times ($t_R$), governed by their hydrophobicity. 5. **Why this matters:** Without chromatographic separation, isobaric compounds (same nominal mass) and matrix interferents would overwhelm the MS detector, causing **ion suppression**. **B. Mass Spectrometry (MS) Subsystem** 1. **Ionization Source:** Converts liquid-phase eluate into gas-phase ions. 2. **Mass Analyzer:** Separates ions by their $m/z$ ratio. 3. **Detector:** Converts ion current into an electrical signal proportional to abundance. ### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" of LC-MS Components **Ionization Sources (Atmospheric Pressure Ionization — API):** - **Electrospray Ionization (ESI):** The eluate is nebulized through a charged capillary, producing a fine aerosol of charged droplets. As solvent evaporates (via heated nitrogen), Coulombic repulsion overcomes surface tension, ejecting **gas-phase ions** via the **Taylor cone mechanism**. ESI is a **"soft" ionization** technique, preserving molecular ions with minimal fragmentation. Ideal for polar, thermally labile compounds (peptides, mycotoxins). - **Atmospheric Pressure Chemical Ionization (APCI):** The eluate is vaporized in a heated chamber, and a corona discharge creates reagent ions that transfer charge to analytes via gas-phase reactions. APCI handles less polar, smaller molecules better than ESI. - **Atmospheric Pressure Photoionization (APPI):** Uses a UV lamp (e.g., krypton lamp at 10.0 eV) to photoionize analytes. Excellent for non-polar analytes like PAHs and lipid-soluble vitamins. > [!NOTE] > **Electrospray Ionization (ESI) was awarded the 2002 Nobel Prize in Chemistry** to John Fenn (alongside Koichi Tanaka for MALDI), cementing LC-MS as the dominant technique in biological and food analysis. **Mass Analyzers:** - **Quadrupole (Q):** Four parallel rods with oscillating DC and RF voltages. Acts as a **mass filter** — only ions of a specific $m/z$ have stable trajectories and reach the detector. - **Time-of-Flight (TOF):** Ions are accelerated to a known kinetic energy and drift through a field-free flight tube. The **time of flight** is proportional to $\sqrt{m/z}$, allowing simultaneous detection of all masses. - **Ion Trap (IT):** Ions are trapped in a 3D RF field and ejected sequentially by $m/z$ for detection. Excellent for **MS/MS** experiments. - **Orbitrap:** Ions orbit a central spindle electrode; their axial frequency is proportional to $\sqrt{m/z}$. Provides **ultra-high resolution (R > 100,000)** and mass accuracy (< 1 ppm). **Common LC-MS Configurations:** - **Single Quadrupole (LC-Q-MS):** For screening and quantitation. - **Triple Quadrupole (LC-QQQ or LC-MS/MS):** For **Multiple Reaction Monitoring (MRM)** — the gold standard for trace-level quantification in food safety. - **Quadrupole-Time-of-Flight (LC-Q-TOF-MS):** For **non-target screening** and **high-resolution accurate mass (HRAM)** analysis. - **Quadrupole-Orbitrap (LC-Q-Orbitrap-MS):** For **proteomics, metabolomics, and untargeted foodomics**. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units / Description | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Retention Factor ($k$) | $k = \frac{t_R - t_M}{t_M}$ | Dimensionless; $t_R$ = retention time, $t_M$ = dead time | | 2 | Chromatographic Resolution ($R_s$) | $R_s = \frac{2(t_{R2} - t_{R1})}{w_1 + w_2}$ | Dimensionless; $w$ = peak width at base | | 3 | Van Deemter Equation (HETP) | $H = A + \frac{B}{u} + C \cdot u$ | $H$ in $\mu$m, $u$ = linear velocity (cm/s) | | 4 | TOF Time-of-Flight | $t = L \sqrt{\frac{m}{2zeV}}$ | $L$ = drift length, $V$ = accelerating voltage | | 5 | Quadrupole Mathieu Parameters | Stability region: $a = \frac{2zeU}{m\omega^2 r_0^2}$, $q = \frac{2zeV_{RF}}{m\omega^2 r_0^2}$ | Defines $m/z$ transmission window | | 6 | ESI Droplet Charge (limit) | $z_{Ray} = 0.71 \cdot \frac{4\pi}{e} \cdot (\gamma \cdot \varepsilon_0 \cdot r^2)^{1/2}$ | Rayleigh charge limit for droplets | | 7 | Mass Resolution (R) | $R = \frac{m}{\Delta m}$ | $\Delta m$ = peak width at FWHM | | 8 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (S/N) | $S/N = \frac{I_{signal}}{\sigma_{noise}}$ | Detection limit criterion: S/N $\geq 3$ for LOD, $\geq 10$ for LOQ | | 9 | Ion Suppression Factor | $\% IS = 100 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{A_{post-extraction}}{A_{neat \, standard}}\right)$ | Matrix effect metric | | 10 | Mass Accuracy (ppm) | $\Delta m \, (ppm) = \frac{m_{measured} - m_{theoretical}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 10^6$ | Unit: **ppm** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Engineering Tip:** Always check for **ion suppression/enhancement** in food matrix analysis using **post-extraction spike** or **standard addition** methods. The matrix effect in food LC-MS can cause **errors of 20–80%** if not corrected. ### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering LC-MS is the cornerstone technique for compliance with **food safety regulations** including: - **FSSAI (India)**, **Codex Alimentarius**, **EU Regulation 1881/2006** (contaminant limits), and **US FDA** tolerance levels. - Detection of **pesticide residues (e.g., glyphosate, neonicotinoids)**, **mycotoxins (aflatoxin B1, ochratoxin, deoxynivalenol)**, **veterinary drug residues (chloramphenicol, nitrofurans)**, **food dyes (Sudan I-IV)**, and **process contaminants (acrylamide, 3-MCPD, PAHs)**. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation I: Electrospray Ionization — Rayleigh Charge Limit The **Rayleigh limit** defines the maximum charge a liquid droplet can hold before Coulombic fission occurs. This is the foundational physics of the ESI mechanism. **Step 1: Define the surface charge density and outward electrostatic pressure.** Consider a spherical droplet of radius $r$ carrying total charge $Q$ uniformly on its surface. The outward electrostatic pressure is given by: $$P_{elec} = \frac{\sigma^2}{2\varepsilon_0}$$ where $\sigma$ is the surface charge density and $\varepsilon_0$ is the vacuum permittivity. **Step 2: Express surface charge density in terms of total charge.** Since charge is uniformly distributed on a sphere of radius $r$: $$\sigma = \frac{Q}{4\pi r^2}$$ Substituting: $$P_{elec} = \frac{Q^2}{32\pi^2 \varepsilon_0 r^4}$$ **Step 3: Define the inward Laplace pressure from surface tension.** For a spherical droplet with surface tension $\gamma$: $$P_{Laplace} = \frac{2\gamma}{r}$$ **Step 4: Apply the Rayleigh stability criterion.** The droplet becomes unstable (and undergoes Coulombic fission) when outward electrostatic pressure equals inward Laplace pressure: $$P_{elec} = P_{Laplace}$$ $$\frac{Q^2}{32\pi^2 \varepsilon_0 r^4} = \frac{2\gamma}{r}$$ **Step 5: Solve for the Rayleigh charge $Q_R$.** $$Q_R^2 = 64\pi^2 \varepsilon_0 \gamma r^3$$ $$Q_R = 8\pi r \sqrt{\varepsilon_0 \gamma r}$$ **Step 6: Convert to elementary charge units $z_R$.** $$z_R = \frac{Q_R}{e} = \frac{8\pi r}{e}\sqrt{\varepsilon_0 \gamma r}$$ **Step 7: Practical approximation formula (commonly used in MS literature).** $$z_R \approx 0.71 \cdot \frac{4\pi}{e}\sqrt{\gamma \varepsilon_0 r^2}$$ This derivation shows that as solvent evaporates, $r$ decreases, and the droplet reaches the Rayleigh limit and ejects a fine jet of much smaller charged droplets — the **"Taylor cone-jet"** mechanism. This is the physical basis for generating gas-phase ions in ESI. ### 3.2 Derivation II: Chromatographic Resolution and the Resolution Equation **Step 1: Definition of peak width and separation.** For two adjacent peaks with retention times $t_{R1}$ and $t_{R2}$ and baseline widths $w_1$ and $w_2$: $$R_s = \frac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{0.5(w_1 + w_2)}$$ **Step 2: Express separation in terms of selectivity factor $\alpha$.** The selectivity factor is: $$\alpha = \frac{k_2}{k_1}$$ **Step 3: Express efficiency in terms of plate number $N$.** For a peak with retention time $t_R$ and baseline width $w$: $$N = 16 \left(\frac{t_R}{w}\right)^2$$ **Step 4: Combine using the Purnell equation.** $$R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k_2}{1 + k_2}$$ **Step 5: Solve for required plate count $N$ for a target $R_s = 1.5$ (baseline separation).** $$N_{req} = 16 R_s^2 \left(\frac{\alpha}{\alpha - 1}\right)^2 \left(\frac{1 + k_2}{k_2}\right)^2$$ This shows that to **double resolution**, the plate count must **quadruple** (since $R_s \propto \sqrt{N}$), which directly impacts the column length and analysis time. ### 3.3 Derivation III: Time-of-Flight (TOF) Mass Analyzer Physics **Step 1: An ion of charge $ze$ accelerated through potential $V$ gains kinetic energy.** $$zeV = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$ **Step 2: Solve for ion velocity.** $$v = \sqrt{\frac{2zeV}{m}}$$ **Step 3: Time to traverse flight tube of length $L$.** $$t = \frac{L}{v} = L \sqrt{\frac{m}{2zeV}}$$ **Step 4: The $m/z$ ratio is then determined by:** $$\frac{m}{z} = \frac{2eVt^2}{L^2}$$ **Step 5: In terms of $m/z$:** $$t = \frac{L}{\sqrt{2eV}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{m}{z}}$$ This shows that TOF separates ions because **heavier ions arrive later** at the detector, with the arrival time scaling as $\sqrt{m/z}$. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Key Performance Comparison of LC-MS Ionization Sources | Parameter | ESI | APCI | APPI | |---|---|---|---| | Polarity Suitability | Polar to moderately polar | Less polar, medium polarity | Non-polar | | Molecular Weight Range | Up to 150,000 Da (proteins) | Up to ~1,500 Da | Up to ~1,500 Da | | Soft/Hard Ionization | Very soft (intact M+H$^+$) | Soft to medium | Soft | | Mobile Phase Flow | 1 $\mu$L/min – 1 mL/min | 0.2 – 2 mL/min | 0.2 – 2 mL/min | | Ion Suppression Susceptibility | **High** | Medium | Low | | Typical Food Applications | Mycotoxins, peptides, dyes | Vitamins, steroids, herbicides | PAHs, lipid vitamins | | Adduct Formation | $[\text{M+H}]^+$, $[\text{M+Na}]^+$, $[\text{M+NH}_4]^+$ | $[\text{M+H}]^+$, $[\text{M+NH}_4]^+$ | $[\text{M}]^{+\cdot}$ (radical cation) | ### 3.5 Quantitative Analysis Using LC-MS/MS (MRM Mode) In **Multiple Reaction Monitoring (MRM)**, a triple quadrupole MS performs: 1. **Q1:** Selects the precursor ion (parent ion) of the target analyte. 2. **Q2 (collision cell):** Fragments the precursor via **Collision-Induced Dissociation (CID)** using argon or nitrogen gas at energies of 5–50 eV. 3. **Q3:** Selects a specific **product ion (fragment ion)**. A **transition** (Q1 $\rightarrow$ Q3) is monitored. Two transitions are typically used for **EU SANTE/11813/2017** confirmation: $$Q1 \, (\text{precursor } m/z) \xrightarrow{\text{CID, collision energy}} Q3 \, (\text{product } m/z)$$ The **quantification ion** is used for concentration measurement, and the **qualification ion** confirms identity by **ion ratio** matching within $\pm$ 30% of a reference standard. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an LC-MS System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Sample Vial
Food Extract] --> B[Autosampler
1 to 50 microlitre] B --> C[Solvent Reservoirs
Water and ACN and MeOH] C --> D[Binary HPLC Pump
4000 to 15000 psi] D --> E[Injector Valve
Rheodyne Six Port] E --> F[HPLC Column
C18 Reversed Phase] F --> G[Column Oven
25 to 60 degree Celsius] G --> H[ESI Source
Taylor Cone Formation] H --> I[Mass Analyzer
Q or TOF or Orbitrap] I --> J[Detector
Electron Multiplier] J --> K[Data System
MassHunter or Xcalibur] H2[Nebulizer Gas N2] -.-> H H3[Heating Gas] -.-> H H4[Sheath Gas] -.-> H I2[Collision Gas Ar] -.-> I ``` ### 4.2 Detailed Mass Spectrometer Subsystem — Triple Quadrupole Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph IonSource[API Ionization Source] I1[Eluate Inlet] --> I2[Capillary High Voltage 3 to 5 kV] I2 --> I3[Electrospray Taylor Cone] I3 --> I4[Charged Droplets] I4 --> I5[Gas Phase Ions] end I5 --> Q1Stage subgraph Q1Stage[Q1 Mass Filter] Q1a[Four Rod Electrodes] --> Q1b[Selects Precursor Ions] end Q1b --> CollisionCell subgraph CollisionCell[Q2 Collision Cell] C1[Argon Gas at 5 to 50 eV] --> C2[CID Fragmentation] end C2 --> Q3Stage subgraph Q3Stage[Q3 Mass Filter] Q3a[Four Rod Electrodes] --> Q3b[Selects Product Ions] end Q3b --> DetectorStage subgraph DetectorStage[Detection and Data] D1[Electron Multiplier] --> D2[Signal Amplifier] D2 --> D3[ADC Converter] D3 --> D4[Chromatogram and Mass Spectrum] end ``` ### 4.3 LC-MS Analytical Workflow — Food Sample to Result ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Step 1: Food Sample Collection] --> S2[Step 2: Homogenization] S2 --> S3[Step 3: QuEChERS or SPE Extraction] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Centrifugation and Filtration] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Reconstitution in Mobile Phase] S5 --> S6[Step 6: LC-MS Injection] S6 --> S7[Step 7: Chromatographic Separation] S7 --> S8[Step 8: Ionization in ESI Source] S8 --> S9[Step 9: Mass Analysis] S9 --> S10[Step 10: Data Processing and Quantification] S10 --> S11[Step 11: Confirmation against MRL Limits] S11 --> S12[Step 12: Report Generation] S3 --> S3a[SPE: Solid Phase Extraction
Removes matrix interferents] S10 --> S10a[Internal Standard Calibration
Isotope Dilution Method] ``` ### 4.4 Decision Tree — Selection of LC-MS Configuration for Food Analytes ```mermaid flowchart TD Q[Analyte Properties] --> Q1{Polarity and MW} Q1 -->|Polar and MW greater than 1000 Da| P1[ESI with Ion Trap or Orbitrap] Q1 -->|Polar and MW less than 1000 Da| P2[ESI with Triple Quad MRM] Q1 -->|Non-polar| P3[APCI or APPI] Q1 --> Q2{Analysis Type} Q2 -->|Targeted Quantification| T1[LC-MS or LC-MS or LC-QTOF] Q2 -->|Untargeted Screening| T2[LC-QTOF or LC-QOrbitrap HRAM] Q2 -->|Structural Elucidation| T3[LC-MS or LC-Orbitrap with MS or LC-MS] Q1 --> Q3{Regulatory Requirement} Q3 -->|EU MRL Confirmation| R1[SANTE 11312 Two MRM Transitions] Q3 -->|FDA Tolerance| R2[FDA CVM Method Validation] Q3 -->|FSANZ| R3[FSANZ Method 1 to 14] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** Define Liquid Chromatography–Mass Spectrometry (LC-MS). List **three advantages** of LC-MS over GC-MS in food contaminant analysis. **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** LC-MS is a hyphenated analytical technique that combines the separation capability of High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) with the mass-selective detection capability of Mass Spectrometry (MS), enabling identification and quantification of analytes based on their mass-to-charge ratio ($m/z$). **Three Advantages (2 marks — 1 mark each, any three):** 1. **Analyte Versatility:** LC-MS can analyze **polar, non-volatile, and thermally labile** compounds (e.g., mycotoxins, peptides) that cannot be analyzed by GC-MS without derivatization. 2. **No Derivatization Required:** Eliminates time-consuming chemical derivatization steps, reducing sample preparation time and analytical error. 3. **Superior Selectivity and Sensitivity:** Provides detection limits in the **ppt (parts-per-trillion)** range through MRM, with high specificity via characteristic fragment ions. 4. **Structural Information:** Soft ionization (ESI) preserves molecular ions and enables MS/MS for fragmentation pattern analysis and confirmation. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]** What is **Electrospray Ionization (ESI)**? State the principle behind ion formation in ESI. **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Electrospray Ionization (ESI) is a "soft" atmospheric pressure ionization technique in which a liquid eluate is passed through a capillary held at high potential (typically **2.5–5.0 kV**), generating a fine aerosol of charged droplets that undergo solvent evaporation and Coulombic fission to produce gas-phase ions. **Principle (1.5 marks):** The principle is based on the **Taylor cone-jet mechanism** and the **Rayleigh charge limit**. As the charged droplets shrink due to solvent evaporation, the surface charge density increases. When Coulombic repulsion exceeds surface tension (Rayleigh limit), the droplet undergoes asymmetric fission, ejecting smaller charged progeny droplets. Ultimately, field desorption of ions from these microdroplets produces intact gas-phase molecular ions, predominantly as $[\text{M+H}]^+$ or $[\text{M+Na}]^+$. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Part B Module 3 questions carry 14 marks. Internal choice is provided — students must answer **one full question with sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks**. --- **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Question Paper Code: PEFTT753-M3-Q7A]** **(a)** With a **neat block diagram**, describe the working principle of an LC-MS system. Explain the role of **(i) HPLC column** and **(ii) Electrospray Ionization (ESI) source** in detail. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the principle and application of **Quadrupole Mass Analyzer** in LC-MS. Derive the relationship between the **ion trajectory stability and the Mathieu parameters $a$ and $q$**. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Question A:** **(a) Working Principle of LC-MS (7 marks):** **Block Diagram (3 marks):** ``` Sample → Autosampler → HPLC Pump → Injector → Column → ESI Source → MS Analyzer → Detector → Data System ``` **Working Principle (2 marks):** A liquid food extract is injected into a stream of mobile phase (water + organic solvent). The HPLC column separates the analytes based on differential partitioning. The eluate is then ionized in the ESI source at atmospheric pressure. The resulting gas-phase ions are introduced into the high-vacuum mass analyzer through a series of differentially pumped skimmers, separated by $m/z$, and detected. **HPLC Column Role (1 mark):** The C18 reversed-phase column separates analytes by hydrophobicity. Longer retention indicates more non-polar character, reducing matrix interference and ion suppression. **ESI Source Role (1 mark):** The ESI source converts the liquid eluate into gas-phase ions at atmospheric pressure without thermal degradation, preserving molecular ions and enabling analysis of thermolabile compounds. **(b) Quadrupole Mass Analyzer (7 marks):** **Principle (3 marks):** A quadrupole consists of four parallel cylindrical rods of radius $r_0$, with opposite pairs connected electrically. A DC voltage $U$ is superimposed on an RF voltage $V\cos(\omega t)$. Ions travel along the $z$-axis, and their trajectories in the $x$-$y$ plane are described by the **Mathieu differential equation**: $$\frac{d^2u}{d\xi^2} + (a - 2q\cos 2\xi)u = 0$$ where $u$ represents displacement ($x$ or $y$) and $\xi = \omega t/2$. **Mathieu Parameters (2 marks):** $$a = \frac{8zeU}{m\omega^2 r_0^2}, \quad q = \frac{4zeV}{m\omega^2 r_0^2}$$ For an ion to have a **stable trajectory** and pass through the quadrupole, the $(a, q)$ values must lie within the **first stability region** of the Mathieu stability diagram. By selecting specific $(U, V)$ values, only ions of a particular $m/z$ are transmitted, while others undergo unstable trajectories and are ejected. **Application in LC-MS (2 marks):** In **MRM (Multiple Reaction Monitoring)** mode, a triple quadrupole (Q1-Q2-Q3) performs: - Q1: Selects the precursor ion of the target analyte. - Q2: Fragments the precursor via CID. - Q3: Selects a specific product ion. This dual-stage mass filtering provides **exceptionally high selectivity** and **ppt-level sensitivity**, making it the gold standard for quantification of pesticide residues, veterinary drugs, and mycotoxins in food matrices. --- **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Question Paper Code: PEFTT753-M3-Q7B]** **(a)** Explain the principle of **Time-of-Flight (TOF) Mass Analyzer**. Derive the equation relating the time of flight to the mass-to-charge ratio. List **two advantages** and **one disadvantage** of TOF over quadrupole analyzers. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is **Matrix Effect** in LC-MS analysis of food samples? Discuss the strategies adopted to compensate for it. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Question B:** **(a) Time-of-Flight (TOF) Mass Analyzer (7 marks):** **Principle (2 marks):** In a TOF analyzer, ions produced in the ion source are accelerated through a fixed potential $V$ and allowed to drift through a field-free flight tube of length $L$. Since all ions of the same charge acquire the same kinetic energy, ions of different masses travel at different velocities and arrive at the detector at different times. **Lighter ions arrive first; heavier ions arrive later.** **Derivation (3 marks):** For an ion of mass $m$ and charge $ze$ accelerated through potential $V$: $$zeV = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = \sqrt{\frac{2zeV}{m}}$$ Time to traverse the flight tube: $$t = \frac{L}{v} = L\sqrt{\frac{m}{2zeV}}$$ Rearranging for $m/z$: $$\frac{m}{z} = \frac{2eVt^2}{L^2}$$ Thus, $m/z$ is determined by measuring the time-of-flight $t$, with the mass spectrum generated by recording ion abundance as a function of $t$. **Advantages (1 mark):** 1. **High mass range** (theoretically unlimited). 2. **Fast acquisition** — full mass spectrum in microseconds, ideal for coupling with fast LC separations (UPLC) and chromatography. 3. **Non-scanning** — high duty cycle and sensitivity. **Disadvantage (1 mark):** Lower mass resolution compared to Orbitrap for very high-resolution work, unless **reflectron** or **ion mobility** techniques are added. **(b) Matrix Effect in LC-MS (7 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** Matrix effect refers to the **suppression or enhancement** of analyte ion signal caused by **co-eluting matrix components** (phospholipids, proteins, sugars, salts) in the food extract. These components compete for charge, alter droplet formation in ESI, and deposit on the cone, leading to inaccurate quantification. **Types of Matrix Effect:** - **Ion Suppression:** Co-eluting components reduce analyte ion signal. - **Ion Enhancement:** Some matrices (e.g., salts) can enhance ionization. **Quantification (1 mark):** The matrix effect (ME) is measured by: $$ME(\%) = \left(\frac{A_{post-extraction} - A_{neat}}{A_{neat}}\right) \times 100$$ **Compensation Strategies (4 marks):** 1. **Sample Preparation Optimization:** Use **QuEChERS (Quick, Easy, Cheap, Effective, Rugged, Safe)** with dispersive SPE (d-SPE) cleanup using **PSA (primary secondary amine)** and **graphitized carbon black (GCB)** to remove sugars, fatty acids, and pigments. 2. **Isotope-Labeled Internal Standards (ISTD):** The **gold standard**. Use a stable isotope-labeled analog of the analyte (e.g., ${}^{13}\text{C}$-aflatoxin B1). Since the ISTD experiences the same matrix effect as the analyte, the analyte/ISTD ratio provides accurate quantification. **Cost is high.** 3. **Matrix-Matched Calibration:** Prepare calibration standards in **blank matrix extract** rather than neat solvent, mimicking the actual sample environment. **Limitation:** Requires blank matrix for each food type. 4. **Standard Addition Method:** Spiking the sample with known analyte concentrations and extrapolating to zero addition. **Accurate but labor-intensive.** 5. **Dilution Approach:** Diluting the sample extract reduces matrix concentration and thus matrix effects, though it also reduces analyte concentration. 6. **Chromatographic Separation Enhancement:** Improved LC separation to physically separate the analyte from matrix interferences (e.g., extended gradient, different column chemistry). --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in LC-MS Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > > 1. **Confusing LC-MS with GC-MS (–2 marks):** Students often describe GC components instead of LC. Always state the **mobile phase is liquid**, not gaseous. Pump pressure is **4000–15,000 psi**, not ambient. > > 2. **Forgetting "Atmospheric Pressure" in ESI (–1 mark):** ESI operates at **atmospheric pressure**, distinguishing it from older vacuum ionization techniques. Writing it correctly is critical. > > 3. **Missing Adduct Ions in ESI (–1.5 marks):** ESI does not always produce $[\text{M+H}]^+$. Students must mention $[\text{M+Na}]^+$, $[\text{M+NH}_4]^+$, or $[\text{M-H}]^-$ depending on the analyte and mobile phase additives. > > 4. **Confusing Quadrupole and Ion Trap (–1 mark):** Quadrupole is a **mass filter** (passes one $m/z$ at a time); Ion Trap is a **mass storage device** (traps and ejects ions sequentially). > > 5. **No Mention of Internal Standard in Quantitation (–2 marks):** Any LC-MS quantification answer must include internal standard correction, especially for matrix effects. > > 6. **Forgetting Soft vs Hard Ionization Classification (–1 mark):** ESI and APCI are **soft** (preserve molecular ion); EI is **hard** (extensive fragmentation). This distinction is repeatedly tested. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **LC-MS = HPLC + MS** — the gold standard for polar, non-volatile, thermally labile food contaminants. - **Key Components:** Autosampler → Pump → Injector → Column → ESI/APCI Source → Mass Analyzer (Q/TOF/Orbitrap) → Detector → Data System. - **Ionization Sources:** **ESI** (polar, high MW), **APCI** (less polar, medium MW), **APPI** (non-polar). - **Common Adducts in ESI:** $[\text{M+H}]^+$, $[\text{M+Na}]^+$, $[\text{M+NH}_4]^+$ (positive mode); $[\text{M-H}]^-$ (negative mode). - **Mass Analyzers:** Quadrupole (filter, unit resolution), TOF (parallel, high mass range), Ion Trap (storage, MS$^n$), Orbitrap (highest resolution). - **MRM Mode** (Triple Quadrupole) is the gold standard for **quantification** with **two ion transitions** for confirmation. - **Mass Resolution:** $R = m/\Delta m$; Mass Accuracy in **ppm**. - **Rayleigh Limit:** Defines the critical charge for Coulombic fission in ESI droplets; governs ion formation efficiency. - **Mathieu Parameters** $a$ and $q$ define the stability region for ion transmission in a quadrupole. - **Matrix Effect** is a critical concern; compensate using **isotope-labeled internal standards** or **matrix-matched calibration**. - **QuEChERS** is the standard sample preparation method for pesticide residue analysis in food. - **Detection Limits:** LC-MS/MS in **ppt (parts-per-trillion)** range for most food contaminants. - **TOF Equation:** $t = L\sqrt{m/(2zeV)}$ — time scales as $\sqrt{m/z}$. - **Regulatory References:** EU **SANTE 11312/2021**, **FDA CVM**, **Codex Alimentarius**, **FSSAI** for food residue analysis. - **Soft vs Hard Ionization:** ESI/APCI = soft (molecular ion preserved); EI = hard (extensive fragmentation). - **Always use internal standards** in quantitative LC-MS to correct for matrix effects and instrument variability. - **LOD criterion:** S/N $\geq$ **3**; **LOQ criterion:** S/N $\geq$ **10**.

topics.title: FTIR - Analysis of Chiral Compounds - Formation of Flavour Compounds in Milk and Milk Products During Heat Processing like UHT Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT753 - FOOD PRODUCTS MONITORING & CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** FTIR - Analysis of Chiral Compounds - Formation of Flavour Compounds in Milk and Milk Products During Heat Processing like UHT Processing ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) **Formal Definition:** Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) is an analytical technique used to identify organic, polymeric, and in some cases inorganic materials by measuring the absorption of infrared radiation by the sample as a function of wavelength (or wavenumber). It exploits the fact that molecules absorb specific frequencies characteristic of their structure (functional groups) when infrared light passes through them, producing an infrared absorption spectrum that acts as a molecular "fingerprint." > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** FTIR is classified as a **vibrational spectroscopy** technique in food analysis. The typical spectral range used is the **mid-IR region (4000–400 cm⁻¹)**. The key components are: **Source → Michelson Interferometer → Sample → Detector → Computer (Fourier Transform).** **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Think of FTIR as a **"molecular stethoscope."** When you tap a glass with a spoon, it rings at a particular pitch — its natural frequency of vibration. Every material has its own unique "ring." FTIR shines a broad band of infrared "light tones" onto a sample and listens for which tones are absorbed. Each tone (frequency) absorbed corresponds to a specific bond vibration (like C=O, O–H, N–H). The resulting spectrum is a unique audio "fingerprint" of the molecule, allowing rapid identification. **Standard Metrics in FTIR:** - **Wavenumber ($\bar{\nu}$):** **4000 cm⁻¹ to 400 cm⁻¹** (mid-IR range) - **Wavelength ($\lambda$):** **2.5 µm to 25 µm** - **Resolution:** Typically **0.5–4 cm⁻¹** - **Detector:** Deuterated Triglycine Sulfate (DTGS) or Mercury Cadmium Telluride (MCT) --- ### 1.2 Chiral Compounds **Formal Definition:** A **chiral compound** (from Greek *cheir* = hand) is a molecule that is non-superimposable on its mirror image. Such molecules possess one or more **chiral centers** (typically a carbon atom bonded to four different substituents). The two non-superimposable mirror images are called **enantiomers**, and they exhibit identical physical properties but differ in optical activity (rotation of plane-polarized light) and biological activity. > [!NOTE] > **Chirality in Food:** Many flavor and aroma compounds (e.g., limonene, carvone, menthol) are chiral. (+)-Limonene smells like oranges, while (–)-limonene smells like lemons. Hence, analyzing chirality is critical for authentic flavor profiling. **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Hold up your left hand to a mirror — the image looks like a right hand. They are mirror images, but you cannot superimpose one on the other (a left glove does not fit the right hand). Molecules that behave the same way are called **chiral**. The two mirror versions are called **enantiomers** — like a pair of molecular "left and right gloves" that interact differently with biological receptors, including our nose and tongue. --- ### 1.3 Formation of Flavour Compounds in Milk During Heat Processing (UHT) **Formal Definition:** **Ultra-High Temperature (UHT) processing** is a thermal treatment of milk at temperatures of **135–150 °C for 2–5 seconds**, followed by aseptic packaging. The intense heat induces a complex series of chemical reactions — primarily the **Maillard reaction, Strecker degradation, lipid oxidation, and casein denaturation** — that generate volatile flavor compounds such as aldehydes, ketones, lactones, furans, pyrazines, and sulfur-containing compounds, producing the characteristic **"cooked"** and **"stale/heated"** flavors in UHT milk. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Key Concept:** UHT milk has a longer shelf life (3–6 months at ambient temperature) but develops distinct **off-flavors** due to heat-induced chemistry, in contrast to pasteurized milk (72 °C / 15 s) which retains a fresh flavor profile. **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Imagine toasting a piece of bread. The browning and aroma you smell are the result of the **Maillard reaction** — proteins and sugars reacting under heat. UHT milk undergoes a similar but much milder version of this same chemistry: heat "toasts" the milk at a molecular level, generating new aroma compounds (like furfural and HMF) that you perceive as the **cooked** taste of UHT milk. The longer the storage, the more these compounds evolve, producing **stale** flavors. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 FTIR — Working Principle and Instrumentation #### 2.1.1 The Michelson Interferometer (Heart of FTIR) The Michelson interferometer consists of: - A **beam splitter** that divides the IR beam into two paths. - A **fixed mirror** and a **moving mirror**. - The two beams recombine at the beam splitter, producing an **interferogram** as a function of the moving mirror's position. The interferogram is a plot of **signal intensity vs. optical path difference (OPD)**. It is then **Fourier Transformed** by the computer to obtain the conventional **infrared spectrum (intensity vs. wavenumber)**. #### 2.1.2 The Fundamental Equation of FTIR The relationship between the interferogram and the spectrum is given by the **Fourier transform pair**: $$I(\bar{\nu}) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} B(\delta) \, \cos(2\pi \bar{\nu} \delta) \, d\delta$$ Where: - $I(\bar{\nu})$ = intensity at wavenumber $\bar{\nu}$ - $B(\delta)$ = interferogram intensity at optical path difference $\delta$ - $\bar{\nu}$ = wavenumber (cm⁻¹) The inverse transform recovers the interferogram from the spectrum: $$B(\delta) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} I(\bar{\nu}) \, \cos(2\pi \bar{\nu} \delta) \, d\bar{\nu}$$ #### 2.1.3 Why FTIR over Dispersive IR? | Feature | Dispersive IR | FTIR | |---|---|---| | Method | Monochromator scans | Interferometer + Fourier transform | | Speed | Slow (minutes) | Fast (seconds) | | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) | Low | **High (Fellgett advantage)** | | Throughput (Jaquinot) | Low | **High** | | Wavenumber precision (Connes) | Moderate | **High (laser reference)** | > [!NOTE] > **The Three Key FTIR Advantages (Examiner's favorite 3-mark question):** > 1. **Fellgett (Multiplex) Advantage** — measures all frequencies simultaneously. > 2. **Jaquinot (Throughput) Advantage** — no narrow slits, more energy reaches detector. > 3. **Connes Advantage** — internal He–Ne laser provides highly accurate wavenumber calibration. #### 2.1.4 Characteristic IR Absorption Bands in Food Chemistry | Wavenumber (cm⁻¹) | Bond / Vibration | Functional Group / Compound | |---|---|---| | 3500–3200 | O–H stretch | Water, alcohols, sugars | | 3400–3300 | N–H stretch | Proteins, amides | | 3000–2850 | C–H stretch (aliphatic) | Lipids, fatty acids | | 1740–1720 | C=O stretch | Carbonyls, esters, aldehydes | | 1700–1650 | C=O (Amide I) | Proteins | | 1600–1500 | N–H bend (Amide II) | Proteins | | 1200–950 | C–O, C–C stretch | Carbohydrates | | 900–650 | =C–H bend | Aromatics, cis-alkenes | --- ### 2.2 Analysis of Chiral Compounds #### 2.2.1 Why Chiral Analysis is Important in Foods Enantiomers of flavor compounds have **different odor thresholds and qualities**: - **(R)-(+)-Limonene** → orange odor - **(S)-(–)-Limonene** → lemon/turpentine odor - **(R)-(–)-Carvone** → spearmint odor - **(S)-(+)-Carvone** → caraway odor > [!IMPORTANT] > Chiral analysis is essential for **authentication of natural vs. synthetic flavors**, **detection of adulteration**, and **quality control** of essential oils and dairy flavor compounds. #### 2.2.2 Methods for Chiral Analysis 1. **Polarimetry** — measures optical rotation $[\alpha]$ at a specific wavelength (usually sodium D-line, 589 nm): $$[\alpha] = \frac{\alpha}{c \cdot l}$$ Where $\alpha$ = observed rotation, $c$ = concentration (g/mL), $l$ = path length (dm). 2. **Chiral Gas Chromatography (Chiral GC)** — uses cyclodextrin-based chiral stationary phases (e.g., **2,3-di-O-methyl-6-O-tert-butyl-dimethylsilyl-β-cyclodextrin**). 3. **Chiral High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC)** — uses chiral stationary phases like Pirkle-type, cellulose derivatives, or chiral crown ethers. 4. **Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) with Chiral Shift Reagents** — e.g., Eu(hfc)₃ (Europium tris[3-(heptafluoropropylhydroxymethylene)-(+)-camphorate]). 5. **Enzymatic Methods** — using stereospecific enzymes (e.g., lipases, oxidases) that react with only one enantiomer. 6. **FTIR coupled with Vibrational Circular Dichroism (VCD)** — measures differential absorption of left- and right-circularly polarized IR light. --- ### 2.3 Formation of Flavour Compounds in Heat-Processed Milk (UHT) #### 2.3.1 UHT Processing Parameters | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Temperature | **135–150 °C** | | Time | **2–5 seconds** (direct steam injection) or **4–15 s** (indirect heating) | | Microbial inactivation | Commercial sterility (no viable pathogens/spoilage microbes) | | Shelf life | 3–6 months at ambient (aseptic packaging) | | Equivalent $F_0$ | > 9 (sterilization value relative to *B. stearothermophilus*) | #### 2.3.2 Chemical Reactions Causing Flavor Changes **A. Maillard Reaction (Non-enzymatic Browning)** A reaction between the **carbonyl group of reducing sugars (lactose)** and the **amino group of proteins (casein, whey proteins)**, generating hundreds of flavor-active compounds. **B. Strecker Degradation** Amino acids react with α-dicarbonyls (Maillard intermediates) to form **Strecker aldehydes**, which have characteristic odors: - Methionine → **Methional** (boiled potato) - Phenylalanine → **Phenylacetaldehyde** (floral/honey) - Leucine → **3-Methylbutanal** (malty) **C. Lactose Degradation** - **Heat-induced hydrolysis** → glucose + galactose. - **Lactulose formation** (galactose + fructose) — a key UHT marker; concentration > 200 mg/L confirms UHT treatment. **D. Lipid Oxidation and Lactone Formation** - **Free fatty acids** released by lipolysis undergo **γ- and δ-lactone formation**, contributing to **coconut-like / fruity** notes. - **β-Ketoacids decarboxylate** to form methyl ketones (e.g., 2-nonanone). **E. Sulfur-Containing Compounds** - **Hydrogen sulfide (H₂S)**, **dimethyl sulfide (DMS)**, and **methanethiol** from sulfur amino acids (cysteine, methionine) — responsible for **"cooked"/"eggy"** off-flavor. - **Volatile fatty acids (VFAs)** — formic, acetic, propionic — generated via Maillard sugar fragmentation. **F. Furan Derivatives** - **Furfural** and **5-Hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF)** — markers of heat damage. #### 2.3.3 Key Flavour Compounds in UHT Milk | Compound | Origin | Odor Descriptor | |---|---|---| | 2-Methylpropanal | Strecker (Valine) | Malty | | 2-Methylbutanal | Strecker (Isoleucine) | Malty, chocolate | | 3-Methylbutanal | Strecker (Leucine) | Malty | | Methional | Strecker (Methionine) | Boiled potato | | Benzaldehyde | Strecker (Phenylalanine) | Bitter almond | | Phenylacetaldehyde | Strecker (Phenylalanine) | Floral, honey | | Furfural | Maillard / Lactose | Caramel, bread | | HMF | Maillard / Lactose | Caramel, musty | | δ-Decalactone | Lipid / Lactone | Coconut, peach | | γ-Dodecalactone | Lipid / Lactone | Peach, fatty | | Hydrogen sulfide (H₂S) | Sulfur amino acids | Cooked, sulfurous | | Dimethyl sulfide (DMS) | Methionine | Cooked cabbage | | Diacetyl (2,3-Butanedione) | Citrate metabolism | Buttery | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Note:** The distinction between **"cooked flavor"** (from H₂S, DMS — perceived within first 24 h) and **"stale flavor"** (from lactones, Strecker aldehydes — develops during storage) is a **frequently asked 7-mark question**. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Interferogram Equation in FTIR The Michelson interferometer produces a path difference $\delta$ between the two arms. For a monochromatic source of wavenumber $\bar{\nu}_0$, the recombined beam intensity as a function of $\delta$ is: **Step 1:** The electric field from the two arms is: $$E_1 = E_0 \cos(2\pi \bar{\nu}_0 x)$$ $$E_2 = E_0 \cos(2\pi \bar{\nu}_0 (x + \delta))$$ **Step 2:** The detector measures intensity proportional to the square of the sum of the two fields. Applying the cosine addition identity: $$\cos A + \cos B = 2 \cos\left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right) \cos\left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)$$ We obtain: $$I(\delta) = I_0 \left[1 + \cos(2\pi \bar{\nu}_0 \delta)\right]$$ **Step 3:** The constant term $I_0$ is the DC offset; the AC term $\cos(2\pi \bar{\nu}_0 \delta)$ is the **interferogram** of a single wavenumber source. **Step 4:** For a polychromatic source with continuous spectrum $B(\bar{\nu})$, integration over all wavenumbers gives: $$B(\delta) = \int_{0}^{\infty} S(\bar{\nu}) \cos(2\pi \bar{\nu} \delta) \, d\bar{\nu}$$ **Step 5:** The IR spectrum is recovered by inverse Fourier transform: $$S(\bar{\nu}) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} B(\delta) \cos(2\pi \bar{\nu} \delta) \, d\delta$$ This is the fundamental working equation of FTIR. The computer (using the **Cooley–Tukey Fast Fourier Transform algorithm**) computes the spectrum in real time. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Specific Rotation for Chiral Compounds **Step 1:** The observed optical rotation $\alpha$ is measured using a polarimeter at the sodium D-line (589.3 nm), temperature $T$, and path length $l$ (in decimeters). **Step 2:** Specific rotation is defined to normalize the measurement to standard conditions: $$[\alpha]_{\lambda}^{T} = \frac{\alpha}{c \cdot l}$$ Where: - $[\alpha]_{\lambda}^{T}$ = specific rotation (deg·mL·g⁻¹·dm⁻¹) - $\alpha$ = observed rotation (degrees) - $c$ = concentration (g/mL) - $l$ = path length (dm) **Step 3:** **Enantiomeric excess (ee)** quantifies the optical purity: $$ee = \frac{[R] - [S]}{[R] + [S]} \times 100\%$$ **Step 4:** **Optical purity (op)** is related to specific rotation: $$op = \frac{[\alpha]_{observed}}{[\alpha]_{pure}} \times 100\%$$ For an enantiomerically pure compound $op = ee$ when the rotation is linear with concentration (valid for dilute solutions). **Step 5 — Worked Example (KTU-style):** A sugar solution shows an observed rotation of +4.5° in a 1 dm tube at a concentration of 0.10 g/mL. The specific rotation of pure (+)-glucose is +52.7°. Calculate the enantiomeric excess. $$\begin{aligned} [\alpha]_{observed} &= \frac{+4.5°}{0.10 \times 1} = +45.0° \\ op &= \frac{45.0}{52.7} \times 100\% = 85.4\% \\ ee &= op = 85.4\% \end{aligned}$$ Therefore the sample contains 85.4% excess of the (+)-enantiomer, i.e., 92.7% (R) and 7.3% (S). --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Reaction Pathways in UHT Milk | Reaction | Substrate | Key Products | Temperature Trigger | Flavor Impact | |---|---|---|---|---| | Maillard Reaction | Lactose + Casein ε-amino group | Amadori products, Melanoidins, Pyrazines | > 100 °C | Cooked, caramel | | Strecker Degradation | Amino acids + α-dicarbonyls | Strecker aldehydes, CO₂ | > 110 °C | Malty, floral, sulfurous | | Lactose Isomerization | Lactose | **Lactulose** (> 200 mg/L = UHT marker) | > 100 °C | Indirect indicator | | Lipid Hydrolysis | Milk fat triglycerides | Free fatty acids | > 80 °C | Soapy, rancid | | Lactone Formation | γ-/δ-Hydroxy fatty acids | γ-/δ-Lactones (C8–C12) | > 100 °C | Coconut, peach, fruity | | Lipid β-Oxidation | Unsaturated fatty acids | Aldehydes (hexanal, 2-nonenal) | > 90 °C | Oxidized, cardboard | | Sulfur Release | Cystine, Methionine | H₂S, DMS, Methanethiol | > 120 °C | Cooked, sulfurous | | HMF Formation | Hexoses (in Maillard) | 5-HMF, Furfural | > 130 °C | Caramel, musty | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Analytical Question Hint:** "Explain the role of lactulose as a chemical marker of UHT processing" → Lactulose is **not present in raw milk**; it is exclusively formed by **alkaline isomerization of lactose** during UHT treatment. Concentrations: pasteurized milk < 50 mg/L; UHT milk > 200 mg/L; sterilized milk > 600 mg/L. --- ### 3.4 FTIR Identification of Functional Groups in Milk Components | Milk Component | Diagnostic IR Bands (cm⁻¹) | Functional Group | |---|---|---| | Casein (Protein) | 1650 (Amide I), 1540 (Amide II), 3300 (N–H) | Peptide backbone | | Lactose (Carbohydrate) | 1040, 1075 (C–O), 3400 (O–H) | Hydroxyl groups | | Milk Fat (Triglyceride) | 1745 (C=O ester), 2920, 2850 (C–H), 1160 (C–O) | Ester linkage | | Water | 3300 (O–H stretch), 1640 (H–O–H bend) | — | | Lactulose | 1080, 880 | Fructose moiety | This forms the basis of **FTIR-based adulteration detection** in milk (e.g., detection of urea, melamine, sucrose, and vegetable fat). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram of an FTIR Spectrometer ```mermaid flowchart LR src[IR Source
Glowbar SiC] --> bs[Beam Splitter
Ge coated KBr] bs --> fm[Fixed Mirror] bs --> mm[Moving Mirror
He-Ne laser reference] fm --> bs mm --> bs bs --> smp[Sample
Compartment] smp --> det[Detector
DTGS or MCT] det --> adc[Analog to Digital
Converter] adc --> pc[Computer with
FFT Algorithm] pc --> out[Spectrum Display
Intensity vs Wavenumber] ``` --- ### 4.2 Schematic of a Michelson Interferometer (FTIR Core) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[IR Source] -->|Incident Beam| B[Beam Splitter] B -->|Transmitted| C[Fixed Mirror] B -->|Reflected| D[Moving Mirror] C -->|Reflected Beam| B D -->|Reflected Beam| B B -->|Recombined Beam| E[Sample] E --> F[Detector] F --> G[Interferogram] G --> H[Fourier Transform] H --> I[IR Spectrum] ``` --- ### 4.3 Functional Architecture of Chiral Separation Methods ```mermaid flowchart TB ch[Chiral Compound
Enantiomeric Mixture] --> p1[Method 1
Polarimetry] ch --> p2[Method 2
Chiral GC
Cyclodextrin Column] ch --> p3[Method 3
Chiral HPLC
Cellulose CSP] ch --> p4[Method 4
NMR with
Chiral Shift Reagent] ch --> p5[Method 5
FTIR VCD
Vibrational CD] p1 --> r1[Specific Rotation] p2 --> r2[Enantiomer Separated Peaks] p3 --> r3[Enantiomer Separated Peaks] p4 --> r4[Split NMR Signals] p5 --> r5[Differential IR Absorption] ``` --- ### 4.4 Reaction Pathway Block Diagram: UHT Flavor Generation ```mermaid flowchart TB milk[Raw Milk Components] --> lp[Lactose] milk --> pr[Casein and Whey Proteins] milk --> ft[Milk Fat Triglycerides] milk --> aa[Free Amino Acids] lp -->|Maillard| mr[Maillard Reaction] pr -->|Maillard| mr mr --> am[Amadori Products] am --> sd[Strecker Degradation] aa -->|Substrate| sd sd --> sa[Strecker Aldehydes
Malty, Floral, Sulfurous] lp -->|Isomerization| lt[Lactulose Marker] ft -->|Lipolysis| ffa[Free Fatty Acids] ffa -->|Lactonization| lc[Gamma and Delta Lactones
Coconut, Peach] ft -->|Oxidation| lh[Lipid Hydroperoxides] lh -->|Cleavage| al[Aldehydes
Cardboard Off-flavor] pr -->|Sulfur Release| sf[H2S, DMS, Methanethiol
Cooked Flavor] sa --> uht[UHT Milk Flavor Profile] lc --> uht al --> uht sf --> uht lt --> uht uht --> cooked[Cooked Flavor
First 24 h] uht --> stale[Stale Flavor
During Storage] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A: 2-Mark / 3-Mark Short Answer Questions **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Define FTIR. List any two advantages of FTIR over dispersive IR spectroscopy. **Model Answer:** FTIR (Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy) is an analytical technique that measures the absorption of infrared radiation by a sample as a function of wavenumber, producing a spectrum that is the Fourier transform of an interferogram generated by a Michelson interferometer. **Advantages over dispersive IR:** 1. **Fellgett (Multiplex) Advantage** — all wavenumbers are measured simultaneously, improving speed and signal-to-noise ratio. 2. **Jaquinot (Throughput) Advantage** — absence of narrow slits allows more radiation to reach the detector, increasing sensitivity. 3. **Connes Advantage** — internal He–Ne laser provides highly precise wavenumber calibration. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** What is a chiral compound? Give one example of a chiral flavor compound and state how its enantiomers differ in odor. **Model Answer:** A chiral compound is a molecule that is **non-superimposable on its mirror image** and contains at least one chiral (asymmetric) carbon center bonded to four different groups. The two non-superimposable mirror images are called **enantiomers**. **Example:** Limonene (C₁₀H₁₆) - (R)-(+)-Limonene → **orange odor** - (S)-(–)-Limonene → **lemon / turpentine odor** Although enantiomers have identical physical properties (boiling point, density), they differ in their interaction with biological receptors, hence in odor and biological activity. --- ### Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model]** **(a)** Describe the working principle and instrumentation of FTIR with the help of a block diagram. Explain the three advantages of FTIR. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the application of FTIR in the analysis of food components, with specific reference to milk analysis. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Working Principle and Instrumentation of FTIR:** **Principle:** FTIR is based on the absorption of infrared radiation by molecular vibrations. When IR light of frequency matching the natural vibrational frequency of a bond is incident on a molecule, it is absorbed, and a peak appears in the IR spectrum. The sample is exposed to all IR frequencies simultaneously using a **Michelson interferometer**, and the resulting interferogram is mathematically transformed into the spectrum via **Fourier transformation**. **Instrumentation:** 1. **IR Source:** A silicon carbide (globar) or Nernst glower emitting broadband IR radiation (4000–400 cm⁻¹). 2. **Michelson Interferometer:** Consists of a beam splitter (Ge-coated KBr), fixed mirror, and moving mirror. The moving mirror introduces a path difference $\delta$, producing an **interferogram** $B(\delta)$. 3. **Sample Compartment:** The IR beam passes through (or is reflected from) the sample. Sample preparation methods include **KBr pellet, ATR (Attenuated Total Reflectance), and liquid film**. 4. **Detector:** DTGS (room temperature) or MCT (liquid nitrogen cooled, more sensitive). 5. **Computer & FFT Processor:** Converts the time-domain interferogram to the frequency-domain spectrum using the Cooley–Tukey Fast Fourier Transform algorithm. **Three Advantages of FTIR:** - **Fellgett (Multiplex) Advantage:** All frequencies are measured simultaneously, giving superior signal-to-noise ratio. - **Jaquinot (Throughput) Advantage:** High light throughput due to absence of slits. - **Connes Advantage:** Internal He–Ne laser reference provides wavenumber accuracy better than 0.01 cm⁻¹. **(b) Application of FTIR in Food / Milk Analysis:** FTIR is extensively used for **rapid, non-destructive quantification of milk components** in dairy laboratories: | Application | Functional Group / Method | |---|---| | **Fat quantification** | C=O ester stretch at **1745 cm⁻¹** (validated AOCS + IDF official methods) | | **Protein quantification** | **Amide I (1650 cm⁻¹)** and **Amide II (1540 cm⁻¹)** bands | | **Lactose quantification** | C–O stretch at **1040 cm⁻¹** | | **Adulteration detection** (urea, melamine, sucrose, starch) | Comparison of fingerprint region (1500–900 cm⁻¹) | | **Lactulose detection** (UHT marker) | Band at **1080 cm⁻¹** (fructose moiety) | | **Water content** | O–H stretching at 3300 cm⁻¹ | FTIR enables **simultaneous analysis of fat, protein, lactose, and moisture** in less than 30 seconds — making it the gold standard in modern dairy quality control (e.g., **MilkoScan FTIR** instruments). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Alternative Model]** **(a)** Explain the formation of flavour compounds in milk during UHT processing. Discuss the Maillard reaction and Strecker degradation with examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Differentiate between **cooked flavor** and **stale flavor** in UHT milk. List the key volatile compounds responsible for each. How can FTIR help monitor these changes? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Formation of Flavour Compounds in UHT Milk:** UHT processing (135–150 °C / 2–5 s) triggers several heat-induced chemical reactions that generate new volatile flavor compounds: **1. Maillard Reaction:** A non-enzymatic browning reaction between the **carbonyl group of lactose** (reducing sugar) and the **free ε-amino group of casein / whey proteins** (mainly lysine). It proceeds through three stages: - **Initial stage:** Formation of a Schiff base → Amadori / Heyns rearrangement products. - **Intermediate stage:** Formation of dicarbonyls (deoxyosones), furfurals (HMF), and reductones. - **Final stage:** Polymerization to **melanoidins** (brown pigments) and aroma compounds like **pyrazines, furans, thiophenes**. **2. Strecker Degradation:** Amino acids react with α-dicarbonyls (Maillard intermediates) to form **Strecker aldehydes**, CO₂, and amines. Examples: - Valine → **2-Methylpropanal** (malty) - Leucine → **3-Methylbutanal** (malty, chocolate) - Isoleucine → **2-Methylbutanal** (malty) - Methionine → **Methional** (boiled potato) - Phenylalanine → **Phenylacetaldehyde** (floral, honey) - Cystine → **Hydrogen sulfide, Acetaldehyde** (sulfur, cooked) **3. Other Reactions:** - **Lactose isomerization** → **Lactulose** (UHT marker). - **Lipolysis and lactonization** of milk fat → γ- and δ-lactones (coconut, peach notes). - **Lipid β-oxidation** → hexanal, 2-nonenal (oxidized/cardboard notes). - **Sulfur release** from cystine/methionine → H₂S, DMS, methanethiol (cooked/sulfurous). **(b) Cooked vs Stale Flavor in UHT Milk:** | Parameter | Cooked Flavor | Stale Flavor | |---|---|---| | Onset | Within 24 h of processing | Develops during storage (weeks–months) | | Origin | **Sulfur compounds** (H₂S, DMS, methanethiol) from heat-induced breakdown of sulfur amino acids | **Strecker aldehydes + Lactones + Lipid oxidation products** accumulated over time | | Key volatiles | H₂S, DMS, methanethiol, dimethyl disulfide | 2-Methylbutanal, 3-Methylbutanal, methional, δ-decalactone, γ-dodecalactone, hexanal | | Odor | Sulfurous, boiled-egg, cabbage | Malty, fruity, peach, oxidized, cardboard | | Reversibility | **Partially reversible** — dissipates after a few days (degassing / escape of H₂S) | **Progressive and irreversible** | **Role of FTIR in Monitoring:** - FTIR **cannot directly detect volatiles** (H₂S, aldehydes) because they are below detection limits in the liquid matrix, but it can detect: - **Lactulose** (band at 1080 cm⁻¹) — a chemical indicator of UHT treatment intensity. - **5-HMF** and **furfural** (Maillard markers) at specific bands. - Changes in **Amide I / Amide II** ratios indicating casein–lactose adduct formation. - **Lipid oxidation** via carbonyl band growth at **1740–1720 cm⁻¹**. - Therefore, FTIR is a **complementary technique** to **GC-MS / GC-O** for volatile profiling, but a primary tool for **quantifying heat-damage markers** (lactulose, HMF). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls (Where Students Lose Marks):** > 1. **Confusing "Maillard" with "Caramelization":** Caramelization involves only sugars, while Maillard involves sugars **+ amino acids**. UHT milk undergoes Maillard, not caramelization (lactose alone does not caramelize easily). > 2. **Writing "HMF = Hydroxymethyl Furfural"** incorrectly as "Hydroxy Methyl Furfuraldehyde" — lose 0.5 mark. > 3. **Mixing up cooked and stale flavors:** Cooked = H₂S, sulfur compounds (early); Stale = lactones, Strecker aldehydes (during storage). Do NOT interchange. > 4. **Forgetting units in FTIR:** Wavenumbers are in **cm⁻¹**, not nm or Hz. > 5. **Writing the FTIR equation without stating it's the inverse Fourier transform** — lose 1 mark in derivations. > 6. **Stating "Fellgett advantage = speed"** — incorrect; Fellgett = improved S/N ratio from simultaneous measurement. Speed is a consequence. > 7. **In chiral analysis, ignoring that enantiomers have IDENTICAL IR spectra in achiral environments** — chirality is detected by **VCD (Vibrational CD)** or **chiral chromatography**, not conventional FTIR alone. --- ### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** - **FTIR Principle:** Michelson interferometer + Fourier Transform = broadband IR spectrum. Three advantages: **Fellgett, Jaquinot, Connes**. - **Key FTIR spectral range:** **4000–400 cm⁻¹** (mid-IR). Diagnostic bands: 1745 (C=O fat), 1650 (Amide I), 1540 (Amide II), 1040 (lactose). - **Chiral compounds:** Non-superimposable mirror images (enantiomers) — same physical properties, different biological / odor activity. Analyzed by polarimetry, chiral GC/HPLC, NMR shift reagents, VCD. - **Specific rotation:** $[\alpha] = \frac{\alpha}{c \cdot l}$ at sodium D-line (589 nm). - **UHT Processing:** **135–150 °C / 2–5 s** → commercial sterility; shelf life 3–6 months. - **Maillard reaction** = lactose (carbonyl) + casein (amino) → Amadori products → melanoidins + aroma compounds. - **Strecker degradation** = amino acids + α-dicarbonyls → Strecker aldehydes (malty, floral, sulfurous). - **Cooked flavor** = sulfur compounds (H₂S, DMS) — early, partly reversible. - **Stale flavor** = Strecker aldehydes + lactones — late, irreversible. - **Lactulose** is the **key chemical marker** of UHT processing (> 200 mg/L = UHT confirmed). - **FTIR detects** lactulose, HMF, fat, protein, lactose — but **NOT trace volatiles** (use GC-MS for those). - **Exam-ready keywords:** Fellgett, Jaquinot, Connes, Michelson interferometer, enantiomer, ee (enantiomeric excess), Maillard, Strecker, lactulose, HMF, γ-lactone, δ-lactone, cooked flavor, stale flavor. - **Common pitfall to avoid:** Never confuse "wavenumber" with "wavelength" — wavenumber is **cm⁻¹** = 1/λ(cm).

topics.title: Caramelization and Extrusion Cooking - Fermentation and Ripening and Storage - Maillard Browning - Aroma Loss or Retention During the Drying Process - Industrial Processes for Extraction of Desirable and Undesirable Volatile Components from Fresh and/or Stored Products by Supercritical Fluid (SCF) Technique - Colour Characterization - Colour and Appearance - Gloss and Translucence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT753 - FOOD PRODUCTS MONITORING & CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Caramelization and Extrusion Cooking - Fermentation and Ripening and Storage - Maillard Browning - Aroma Loss or Retention During the Drying Process - Industrial Processes for Extraction of Desirable and Undesirable Volatile Components from Fresh and/or Stored Products by Supercritical Fluid (SCF) Technique - Colour Characterization - Colour and Appearance - Gloss and Translucence

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This module integrates the **physico-chemical transformations** occurring during food processing and storage, focusing on **thermal browning, biochemical ripening, aroma retention, and optical quality attributes**. These form the core of food product monitoring and control.

## 1.1 Caramelization

**Definition (KTU Standard):**
Caramelization is a non-enzymatic browning reaction involving the **thermal decomposition (pyrolysis) of sugars** in the absence of amino acids/proteins, producing characteristic brown colour, characteristic flavour compounds, and polymeric brown pigments called **caramelans**.

The reaction typically occurs at temperatures above **160 °C** for sucrose, and lower (≈ 100–120 °C) for more reactive sugars like fructose and glucose.

**Reaction Pathway (Intuitive Analogy):**
Imagine a sugar molecule as a **"house of cards"** built from C, H, and O atoms. When you heat it above its tolerance limit, the cards tumble. Some break apart into small aromatic fragments (giving smell), some reform into coloured rings (giving colour), and some polymerize into long dark chains. No "catalyst" (like amino acid) is needed — heat alone does the job.

**Key Caramelization Products:**
- **Caramelans** (C₂₄H₃₆O₁₈) — bitter
- **Caramelens** (C₃₆H₅₀O₂₅) — bitter
- **Caramelins** (C₁₂₅H₁₈₈O₈₀) — high molecular weight, dark colour
- Volatile compounds: **Diacetyl, Furan, Hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF)**

> [!NOTE]
> **HMF (5-Hydroxymethylfurfural)** is the **key quality indicator** for caramelization. KTU examiners frequently test this marker.

## 1.2 Extrusion Cooking

**Definition:**
Extrusion cooking is a **high-temperature short-time (HTST)** process in which moistened, expanding food material is forced through a die under controlled conditions of temperature, pressure, and shear, resulting in a shaped, cooked, and textured product.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Think of a **chapati maker machine** in a sweet shop. The dough is pushed (pushed = extruded) through a small opening under pressure, and the heat cooks it instantly while the shape is formed. The moment it exits, steam puffs it up, giving a porous, crunchy snack.

**Key Operating Variables:**
- **Barrel Temperature:** 100–200 °C
- **Screw Speed:** 100–500 rpm
- **Moisture Content:** 12–30 %
- **Residence Time:** 10–30 seconds

## 1.3 Fermentation, Ripening and Storage

**Fermentation:** A **biochemical process** where microorganisms (yeast, bacteria, moulds) enzymatically convert carbohydrates into alcohols, acids, CO₂, or other metabolites.

**Ripening:** A **post-harvest biochemical maturation** phase involving conversion of starch → sugars, acids → esters, chlorophyll → carotenoids, and softening of pectin.

**Storage:** Maintenance of the product under controlled **temperature, relative humidity (RH ≈ 85–95 % for fresh), and atmospheric composition** to minimize quality loss.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Fermentation is like a **"tiny brewery"** running inside the food. Ripening is the food's own **"growing up" process** — a mango turning from sour-starchy to sweet-aromatic. Storage is **"putting it to sleep"** so it does not spoil.

> [!NOTE]
> **Climacteric fruits** (banana, mango, apple) show a **respiratory burst** (rise in CO₂ and ethylene) during ripening — a high-yield KTU concept.

## 1.4 Maillard Browning

**Definition:**
The **Maillard reaction** is a non-enzymatic browning reaction between the **carbonyl group (C=O) of reducing sugars** and the **free amino group (–NH₂) of amino acids, peptides, or proteins**, producing a complex mixture of brown nitrogenous polymers called **melanoidins**.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Imagine roasting a **bread slice** on a tawa. The golden-brown crust, the toasted aroma, the nutty taste — all come from the Maillard reaction between sugars and proteins on the surface. It is essentially the **"marriage of sugar and protein under heat"** producing a brown wedding outfit (melanoidin).

**The Three Stages of Maillard Reaction:**
1. **Initial Stage:** Schiff base → Amadori rearrangement → Glycosylamine
2. **Intermediate Stage:** Dehydration, fragmentation → Strecker degradation
3. **Final Stage:** Aldol condensation, polymerization → **Melanoidins**

## 1.5 Aroma Loss / Retention during Drying

**Definition:**
Aroma loss during drying is the **volatilization and/or thermal degradation of low-molecular-weight volatile compounds** responsible for the characteristic odour/flavour of foods. Retention depends on drying method, temperature, moisture content, and whether aroma is bound or free.

**Mechanisms of Aroma Loss:**
- **Selective diffusion theory:** Water diffuses out faster than aroma; aroma concentrates and is lost at the surface
- **Selective sorption theory:** Water-selective membrane retains aroma
- **Hydrodistillation theory:** Aroma co-distills with water
- **Capillary flow theory:** Liquid migrates to surface carrying aroma

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key:** The **selective diffusion theory** (Thijssen & Coumans) is the most accepted and **frequently asked** mechanism.

## 1.6 Supercritical Fluid (SCF) Extraction

**Definition:**
**Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SFE)** is a separation technique in which a fluid above its **critical temperature (Tc)** and **critical pressure (Pc)** is used as the solvent to extract desirable (or strip undesirable) volatile components from fresh/stored food matrices.

**CO₂ as SCF Solvent:**
- Tc = 31.1 °C
- Pc = 73.8 bar
- Non-toxic, GRAS, cheap, recyclable

**Intuitive Analogy:**
A supercritical fluid is like a **"shape-shifting liquid"** — at high pressure it acts like a solvent (dissolves oil-like aroma), and when pressure is released, it evaporates as gas, leaving **pure extract with zero solvent residue**. This is why SCFE is used for **decaffeinated coffee and high-value spice oleoresins**.

## 1.7 Colour Characterization

**Definition:**
Colour characterization is the **objective measurement of food colour** using three standardized systems: **CIE (XYZ / L*a*b*), Munsell (Hue, Value, Chroma), and Hunter (L, a, b)**, translating human perception into numerical quality parameters.

**Colour and Appearance:**
Visual quality attributes — **colour, size, shape, surface defects, uniformity, freshness** — collectively form **appearance**, the first sensory attribute evaluated by consumers.

**Gloss and Translucence:**
- **Gloss:** Specular (mirror-like) reflectance from a surface, measured at angles of **20°, 60°, 85°**.
- **Translucence:** Partial transmission of light through a material, between opaque and transparent. Quantified by **transmittance (T%)** or **opacity** (contrast ratio).

**Intuitive Analogy for Gloss vs Translucence:**
A **shiny apple** reflects light (gloss), while a **jelly cube** lets light pass through (translucence). Both differ from a **matte chalk** (no gloss, opaque).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite:** The **L*a*b* (CIELAB)** colour space is the most commonly examined — know it cold.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Caramelization — Detailed Mechanism

Caramelization is **acid-catalyzed** and proceeds in three sequential stages:

| Stage | Temperature Range | Key Intermediates | Description |
|-------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Stage I (Melting & Inversion)** | 160 °C (sucrose) | Glucosyl cation, 1,2-enediol | Sugar melts, glycosidic bond breaks |
| **Stage II (Dehydration & Fragmentation)** | 170–190 °C | HMF, Diacetyl, Furan derivatives | Loss of water, formation of volatile aroma |
| **Stage III (Polymerization)** | > 200 °C | Caramelan, Caramelen, Caramelin | High-MW brown pigments |

**Catalysts:** Acid (lemon juice, vinegar) accelerates; alkali promotes fragmentation.

## 2.2 Extrusion Cooking — Process Engineering

The extruder comprises a **barrel** with rotating **screws** and a **die**. Starch undergoes the following changes:

1. **Feed zone:** Granular starch mixed with water (≈ 25 % moisture)
2. **Compression/Transition zone:** Compaction, friction heating (T ↑)
3. **Cooking/Metering zone:** Starch gelatinization (60–90 °C), protein denaturation (80–110 °C), Maillard browning (> 130 °C)
4. **Die zone:** Pressure drop → sudden flashing of steam → **puffing and expansion**
5. **Cooling:** Product sets into porous matrix

**Mathematical Model of Expansion:**
The expansion ratio is empirically related to Specific Mechanical Energy (SME):

$$E = \int_{0}^{t} \frac{T \cdot \omega \cdot dt}{M}$$

Where:
- $E$ = SME (kJ/kg)
- $T$ = torque (N·m)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s)
- $M$ = mass flow rate (kg/s)

## 2.3 Maillard Browning — Detailed Pathway

**Stage 1 (Condensation):** Reducing sugar + Amino acid → **Schiff base** + H₂O → **Amadori/Heyns compound**

**Stage 2 (Degradation):** Amadori → **Deoxyosones** → Strecker aldehydes (responsible for aroma!)

**Stage 3 (Polymerization):** Aldol condensation → **Melanoidins** (brown, antioxidant)

**Factors Affecting Maillard:**
- Temperature (Q₁₀ ≈ 2–3)
- pH (optimum 6–8)
- Water activity (a_w = 0.6–0.85 ideal)
- Type of sugar (pentoses > hexoses)
- Type of amino acid (lysine is most reactive)

## 2.4 Aroma Retention Theory

The **Aroma Retention Ratio (R)** is defined as:

$$R = \frac{C_{product}}{C_{feed}} \times 100 \;(\%)$$

The **selective diffusion** of water (high diffusivity) over aroma (low diffusivity) creates a **high local concentration of aroma at the drying surface**, leading to its loss.

**Key dimensionless number — Biot number for mass transfer:**

$$Bi_m = \frac{h_m \cdot L}{D_{AB}}$$

Where:
- $h_m$ = mass transfer coefficient (m/s)
- $L$ = characteristic length (m)
- $D_{AB}$ = diffusivity of aroma in water (m²/s)

For aroma retention, $Bi_m \ll 1$ is desired.

## 2.5 SCF Extraction — Engineering

A supercritical fluid exists when:

$$T > T_c \quad \text{and} \quad P > P_c$$

For CO₂: $T_c = 304.25 \;K$ (31.1 °C), $P_c = 73.8 \;bar$.

**Solubility parameter (δ) of SC-CO₂** can be tuned by pressure:

$$\delta \uparrow \quad \text{as} \quad P \uparrow \;(T \; \text{constant})$$

**Process Steps (Industrial SFE):**
1. Pressurize CO₂ to supercritical state
2. Pass through food matrix in extractor (selective dissolution)
3. Depressurize in separator → SCF becomes gas → pure extract precipitates
4. Recompress and recycle CO₂

**Applications:**
- Desirable extraction: **Spice oleoresins, essential oils, hops, caffeine (decaf)**
- Undesirable removal: **Cholesterol from butter, pesticides, off-flavours**

## 2.6 Colour — Formula Sheet (Critical for KTU)

### CIELAB Coordinates

$$L^* = 116 \cdot f\!\left(\frac{Y}{Y_n}\right) - 16$$

$$a^* = 500 \cdot \left[ f\!\left(\frac{X}{X_n}\right) - f\!\left(\frac{Y}{Y_n}\right) \right]$$

$$b^* = 200 \cdot \left[ f\!\left(\frac{Y}{Y_n}\right) - f\!\left(\frac{Z}{Z_n}\right) \right]$$

Where $X_n, Y_n, Z_n$ are CIE standard illuminant tristimulus values.

### Hue Angle and Chroma

$$H^\circ = \arctan\!\left(\frac{b^*}{a^*}\right) \quad \text{(in degrees)}$$

$$C^* = \sqrt{(a^*)^2 + (b^*)^2}$$

### Total Colour Difference (ΔE)

$$\Delta E = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2}$$

**Interpretation of ΔE:**
- $\Delta E < 1$: Not noticeable
- $1 \le \Delta E \le 3$: Barely noticeable
- $3 < \Delta E \le 6$: Noticeable
- $\Delta E > 6$: Strongly noticeable

### Browning Index (BI) — KTU Favourite

$$BI = \frac{100 \cdot (x - 0.31)}{0.172}$$

Where $x = \dfrac{a^* + 1.75 L^*}{6.645 L^* + a^* - 3.012 b^*}$

### Gloss

$$G = \frac{R_{specular}}{R_{total}} \times 100 \;\;(\%)$$

Measured at standard angles $\theta = 20^\circ, 60^\circ, 85^\circ$.

### Translucence / Opacity

$$\text{Opacity} = \frac{Y_{black}}{Y_{white}} \times 100 \;\;(\%)$$

## 2.7 Master Formula Table

| Concept | Formula / Constant | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Caramelization temp (sucrose) | $T \ge 160 \;^\circ C$ | °C | Acid lowers T |
| HMF indicator | HMF (mg/kg) | mg/kg | Quality marker |
| Extrusion SME | $E = T \omega / M$ | kJ/kg | Affects expansion |
| Maillard optimum a_w | $0.6 - 0.85$ | — | Peak rate |
| CO₂ critical point | $T_c = 31.1 \;^\circ C$, $P_c = 73.8 \;bar$ | °C, bar | SCF threshold |
| Aroma retention R | $R = C_{p}/C_{f} \times 100$ | % | |
| CIELAB L* range | $0 - 100$ | — | Black to white |
| ΔE threshold | $\Delta E = 1$ noticeable | — | Visual difference |
| Hue angle H° | $\arctan(b^*/a^*)$ | degrees | 0° = red, 90° = yellow |
| Gloss angles | $20^\circ, 60^\circ, 85^\circ$ | degrees | Standard |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Real-World Utility:** The ΔE and BI formulas are used industrially in **jam, bread, and fried snack colour QC**; the SCF parameters are used in **coffee decaffeination and spice oleoresin plants**; the Maillard kinetics are used in **bakery process design and acrylamide mitigation**.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: CIELAB L*a*b* from XYZ

We start from the **CIE 1931 tristimulus values** (X, Y, Z) and normalize them with respect to a reference white.

**Step 1: Normalize the tristimulus values.**

$$x_r = \frac{X}{X_n}, \quad y_r = \frac{Y}{Y_n}, \quad z_r = \frac{Z}{Z_n}$$

These are dimensionless ratios.

**Step 2: Apply the non-linear transfer function (cube root approximation).**

$$f(t) = \begin{cases} t^{1/3} & \text{if} \; t > \delta^3 \\[4pt] \dfrac{t}{3\delta^2} + \dfrac{4}{29} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Where the threshold $\delta = 6/29 = 0.2069$ (constant defined by CIE).

This function models the human eye's non-linear perception of brightness.

**Step 3: Compute L* (Lightness).**

The L* axis is based on the Y stimulus (luminance):

$$L^* = 116 \cdot f(y_r) - 16$$

A pure black has $Y = 0 \Rightarrow L^* = 0$, and a reference white has $y_r = 1 \Rightarrow L^* = 100$.

**Step 4: Compute a* (red-green axis).**

$$a^* = 500 \cdot [f(x_r) - f(y_r)]$$

Positive $a^*$ = reddish; negative $a^*$ = greenish.

**Step 5: Compute b* (yellow-blue axis).**

$$b^* = 200 \cdot [f(y_r) - f(z_r)]$$

Positive $b^*$ = yellowish; negative $b^*$ = bluish.

**Step 6: Compute Chroma and Hue Angle from L*a*b*.**

$$C^* = \sqrt{(a^*)^2 + (b^*)^2}$$

$$H^\circ = \arctan\!\left(\frac{b^*}{a^*}\right) \cdot \frac{180}{\pi}$$

## 3.2 Worked Example — Colour Difference Calculation

**Problem (KTU-style):** A freshly baked bread has $L^*=65, a^*=8, b^*=25$. After 7 days of storage, values are $L^*=55, a^*=10, b^*=18$. Calculate $\Delta E$ and Browning Index (BI).

**Step 1: Compute the differences.**

$$\Delta L^* = 55 - 65 = -10$$

$$\Delta a^* = 10 - 8 = 2$$

$$\Delta b^* = 18 - 25 = -7$$

**Step 2: Compute $\Delta E$.**

$$\Delta E = \sqrt{(-10)^2 + (2)^2 + (-7)^2} = \sqrt{100 + 4 + 49} = \sqrt{153}$$

$$\Delta E = 12.37$$

Since $\Delta E > 6$, the difference is **strongly noticeable** — a significant browning change during storage.

**Step 3: Compute Browning Index (Initial).**

$$x = \frac{a^* + 1.75 L^*}{6.645 L^* + a^* - 3.012 b^*} = \frac{8 + 1.75(65)}{6.645(65) + 8 - 3.012(25)}$$

$$x = \frac{8 + 113.75}{431.925 + 8 - 75.3} = \frac{121.75}{364.625} = 0.3339$$

$$BI = \frac{100 \cdot (0.3339 - 0.31)}{0.172} = \frac{100 \cdot 0.0239}{0.172} = 13.90$$

**Step 4: Repeat BI for Day-7 sample.**

$$x = \frac{10 + 1.75(55)}{6.645(55) + 10 - 3.012(18)} = \frac{10 + 96.25}{365.475 + 10 - 54.216}$$

$$x = \frac{106.25}{321.259} = 0.3308$$

$$BI = \frac{100 \cdot (0.3308 - 0.31)}{0.172} = 12.09$$

**Step 5: Interpretation.**
The BI decreased from 13.90 → 12.09 (≈ 13 % reduction), indicating that browning pigment intensity declined during storage. The ΔE of 12.37 confirms visible darkening.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Browning Reactions

| Parameter | Caramelization | Maillard Reaction |
|---|---|---|
| Reactants | Sugars only (no protein) | Reducing sugar + amino acid/protein |
| Temperature | High (> 160 °C) | Moderate (50–180 °C) |
| Catalyst | Heat + acid/base | Heat, no enzyme |
| Key intermediate | HMF (5-HMF) | Amadori / Strecker aldehydes |
| Colour product | Caramelins | Melanoidins |
| Aroma compounds | Diacetyl, furans | Pyrazines, furans, thiophenes |
| pH effect | Acid accelerates | Alkaline accelerates |
| Industrial control | Acid addition (jams, syrups) | Temperature/time control (bakery) |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Drying Methods & Aroma Retention

| Drying Method | Aroma Retention | Mechanism | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hot air drying | **Low (20–50 %)** | High T, surface evaporation | Onion flakes |
| Freeze drying | **High (80–95 %)** | Low T, sublimation locks aroma | Coffee, strawberry |
| Spray drying | **Moderate (50–70 %)** | Fast evaporation, encapsulation possible | Milk powder, flavours |
| Drum drying | **Low (30–50 %)** | Direct contact heating | Mashed potatoes |
| Microwave drying | **Moderate–High (60–80 %)** | Internal heat, low surface loss | Herbs, spices |
| Fluidized bed | **Moderate (55–75 %)** | Uniform drying | Granulated sugar |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — SCF vs Conventional Extraction

| Property | SCF-CO₂ | Steam Distillation | Solvent Extraction |
|---|---|---|---|
| Operating Temperature | 31–60 °C | 100 °C | 40–80 °C |
| Solvent Residue | None | None | Possible (toxic) |
| Selectivity | Tunable via P | Low | Moderate |
| Capital Cost | **High** | Low | Moderate |
| Energy Use | High (compression) | Moderate | Low |
| Aroma Quality | **Excellent** | Good (heat damaged) | Good |
| GRAS Status | Yes | Yes | No (often n-hexane) |

## 3.6 Ripening — Climacteric vs Non-Climacteric

| Property | Climacteric Fruits | Non-Climacteric Fruits |
|---|---|---|
| Respiration pattern | Peak during ripening | Steady decline |
| Ethylene production | High, autocatalytic | Low |
| Ripening post-harvest | **Yes** (banana, mango, apple, tomato) | **No** (citrus, grape, strawberry, pineapple) |
| Storage | Low-T (12–14 °C) | Low-T (0–4 °C) |
| Examples | Banana, mango, papaya, tomato | Orange, lemon, cherry, fig |

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between caramelization and Maillard browning. List the key intermediates of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Caramelization | Maillard Browning |
|---|---|---|
| Reactants | Sugars (no protein) | Reducing sugar + amino acid |
| Temperature | > 160 °C | 50–180 °C |
| Catalyst | Acid/alkali | Heat (no enzyme) |
| Key intermediate | HMF, diacetyl | Amadori compound, Schiff base |
| End products | Caramelins | Melanoidins |

**Marking split:** Difference table = 2 marks, Intermediates = 1 mark.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: What is supercritical fluid extraction? Why is CO₂ preferred as SCF solvent in food processing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**SCFE Definition (1 mark):** Supercritical fluid extraction is a separation process in which a fluid above its critical temperature and pressure is used to selectively extract desired compounds from a food matrix.

**CO₂ Preference (2 marks):**
1. **Low critical temperature (31.1 °C)** — gentle, retains heat-sensitive aromas.
2. **GRAS status** — non-toxic, leaves no residue in product.
3. **Tunable selectivity** — by varying pressure, polarity of CO₂ can be adjusted.
4. **Recyclable, cheap, non-flammable** — economically and environmentally favourable.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**Q: (a)** Explain the Maillard reaction in detail, including all three stages, the role of temperature, pH, and water activity. Discuss its significance in bakery and fried food products. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define extrusion cooking. Describe the process with a labelled diagram. Explain the role of Specific Mechanical Energy (SME) in determining the expansion and texture of extrudates. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q(a) — Maillard Reaction (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
The Maillard reaction is a non-enzymatic browning reaction between a reducing sugar's carbonyl group (C=O) and the free amino group (–NH₂) of amino acids, peptides, or proteins, producing brown-coloured high-molecular-weight nitrogenous polymers called **melanoidins**, along with characteristic aroma compounds.

**Three Stages (3 marks):**
