# SECTION_3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: SFC by PNMR (Direct Method — Full Worked Example)

**Problem:** A milk fat sample (mass fraction of fat in sample **f_c = 0.80**) is melted at 80 °C for 30 min, tempered at 0 °C for 60 min, then warmed to 10 °C. PNMR signals measured at 11 μs and 70 μs are **F_{11} = 4250** and **F_{70} = 1180**. Calculate the SFC at 10 °C.

**Solution Walkthrough:**

**Step 1 — Identify the solid-dominated and liquid-dominated channels.**
At 11 μs after the 90° pulse, the solid signal (T_{2s} ≈ 12 μs) has barely decayed, while the liquid signal (T_{2l} ≈ 500 ms) is essentially at its initial amplitude. Therefore:

$$\frac{F_{11,\text{liquid}}}{F_{11}} \approx 0.05 \quad (\text{liquid contribution is negligible at 11 μs})$$

**Step 2 — At 70 μs, the solid signal has fully decayed (≈ 0).** All remaining signal is liquid:

$$F_{70,\text{liquid}} \approx F_{70}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the AOCS direct method equation:**

$$\text{SFC} = \frac{F_{11}}{F_{11} + F_{70}} \cdot f_c \cdot 100$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\text{SFC} = \frac{4250}{4250 + 1180} \cdot 0.80 \cdot 100$$

$$\text{SFC} = \frac{4250}{5430} \cdot 0.80 \cdot 100$$

$$\text{SFC} = 0.7827 \cdot 0.80 \cdot 100$$

$$\boxed{\text{SFC} = 62.6\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation (KTU Board Standard):** A milk fat sample with **SFC(10 °C) = 62.6%** is appropriate for a **spreading butter** (target SFC at 10 °C = 55–65%). If SFC were > 75% the product would be too hard; < 50% and it would be too soft.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: T_g of a Lactose–Water System by Gordon–Taylor

**Problem:** A whey protein concentrate contains **w_lactose = 0.65** (anhydrous lactose) and **w_water = 0.35**. Using Gordon-Taylor constant **k = 6.7**, T_g of lactose = **101 °C**, T_g of water = **−135 °C**, compute T_g.

**Step 1 — Convert T_g to Kelvin for thermodynamic consistency:**

$$T_{g,\text{lactose}} = 101 + 273.15 = 374.15 \text{ K}$$

$$T_{g,\text{water}} = -135 + 273.15 = 138.15 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Gordon-Taylor numerator:**

$$\text{Numerator} = w_1 T_{g1} + k \cdot w_2 T_{g2}$$

$$= (0.65)(374.15) + (6.7)(0.35)(138.15)$$

$$= 243.20 + 324.14$$

$$= 567.34$$

**Step 3 — Apply denominator:**

$$\text{Denominator} = w_1 + k \cdot w_2 = 0.65 + (6.7)(0.35) = 0.65 + 2.345 = 2.995$$

**Step 4 — Divide and convert back to °C:**

$$T_g = \frac{567.34}{2.995} = 189.43 \text{ K}$$

$$T_g = 189.43 - 273.15 = -83.72 \text{ °C}$$

$$\boxed{T_g \approx -83.7 \text{ °C}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A T_g of −83.7 °C means this WPC is shelf-stable at typical refrigeration (4 °C) and ambient (25 °C) — both well above T_g. Moisture migration and stickiness will not occur at storage temperatures. However, at high relative humidity (>70% RH), water pickup will *plasticize* the matrix and **dramatically lower** T_g.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Bragg's Law Applied to Ghee Crystal Identification

**Problem:** A ghee sample shows an XRD peak at **2θ = 21.4°** with another peak at **2θ = 19.5°**. Using Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm), compute the d-spacing and identify the polymorph.

**Step 1 — Compute d-spacing for 2θ = 21.4°:**

$$\theta = \frac{21.4}{2} = 10.7°$$

$$d_1 = \frac{\lambda}{2 \sin\theta_1} = \frac{0.15418}{2 \sin(10.7°)} = \frac{0.15418}{2(0.1857)} = \frac{0.15418}{0.3714}$$

$$\boxed{d_1 = 0.415 \text{ nm}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute d-spacing for 2θ = 19.5°:**

$$\theta = 9.75°$$

$$d_2 = \frac{0.15418}{2 \sin(9.75°)} = \frac{0.15418}{2(0.1693)} = \frac{0.15418}{0.3387}$$

$$\boxed{d_2 = 0.455 \text{ nm}}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with reference data:**

- d ≈ 0.415 nm → characteristic of **α polymorph** (short spacing)
- d ≈ 0.455 nm → consistent with mixed **β′ + β** signal

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Decision:** Pure β′ in ghee shows *two* peaks at 0.380 and 0.420 nm. The presence of a 0.455 nm peak suggests **β development — sandiness risk**. Storage at elevated temperature (>30 °C) accelerates this transition. *This is why ghee is stored at < 25 °C in India.*

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Starch Gelatinization Enthalpy by DSC

**Problem:** A wheat starch suspension (30% w/w in water) is heated from 25 °C to 100 °C at 5 °C/min in a DSC. The endothermic peak onset is at **T_o = 58 °C**, peak at **T_p = 65 °C**, conclusion at **T_c = 75 °C**. Peak area integrated = **4.8 J** for a 12 mg dry starch sample. Calculate ΔH_gel in J/g dry starch.

**Step 1 — Verify peak location (sanity check):**
Wheat starch gelatinization T_p typically lies at **62–68 °C**. Our value (65 °C) is consistent.

**Step 2 — Compute enthalpy per unit mass:**

$$\Delta H_{\text{gel}} = \frac{Q_{\text{peak}}}{m_{\text{dry starch}}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$\Delta H_{\text{gel}} = \frac{4.8 \text{ J}}{0.012 \text{ g}}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta H_{\text{gel}} = 400 \text{ J/g}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** This value is **far higher** than the typical wheat starch ΔH (8–15 J/g). The likely error is that the analyst integrated the *water* baseline rather than the starch peak. A correct integration should give ~10 J/g. Always cross-check against literature.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Technique-to-Application Cross-Walk

| Technique | Structural Property Quantified | Sample Type | Detection Limit | Time per Analysis |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| PNMR (TD-NMR) | Solid/liquid fat ratio | Oils, shortenings, margarine | ±0.5% SFC | 2–5 min |
| DSC | Enthalpy of transition (ΔH) | Fats, starches, proteins | ±0.1 J/g | 20–40 min |
| XRD (X-ray) | Polymorph identity, crystallinity | Ghee, chocolate, sugars | 2% crystallinity | 30–60 min |
| Raman | Bond vibrations (C=C, C=O) | Fats, oils, proteins | 0.1 mol% | 30 s |
| ESR (EPR) | Radical concentration | Irradiated foods, oxidized lipids | 10⁻⁸ M spin | 5 min |
| NIR | Bulk composition | Powders, grains, milk | ±0.1% moisture | <1 min |
| Microwave | Moisture, salt | Wet foods, dough | ±0.5% moisture | <1 min |
| Auto Milk Analyser | Fat, protein, lactose, SNF | Raw + processed milk | ±0.05% | 30 s |

---

## 3.6 Derivation: TBARS Value for Oxidized Ghee

**Problem:** A ghee sample (5 g) is reacted with TBA reagent. Absorbance at 532 nm = **0.245**, at 600 nm (turbidity blank) = **0.020**. Volume made up = **25 mL**. Calculate TBARS in mg MDA/kg.

**Standard Equation (KTU):**

$$\text{TBARS} = \frac{(A_{532} - A_{600}) \cdot V \cdot 1000}{m \cdot \varepsilon_{MDA}}$$

Using **ε_MDA = 1.56 × 10⁵ L/(mol·cm)** at 532 nm and MDA molecular weight = 72.06 g/mol.

**Step 1 — Subtract blank:**

$$A_{\text{net}} = 0.245 - 0.020 = 0.225$$

**Step 2 — Convert absorbance to concentration (Beer-Lambert):**

$$c_{\text{MDA}} = \frac{A}{\varepsilon \cdot b} = \frac{0.225}{1.56 \times 10^5 \cdot 1} = 1.442 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol/L}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to mass in total volume:**

$$m_{\text{MDA}} = c \cdot V \cdot M_r = (1.442 \times 10^{-6})(0.025)(72.06)$$

$$m_{\text{MDA}} = 2.60 \times 10^{-6} \text{ g} = 2.60 \text{ μg}$$

**Step 4 — Express per kg of ghee:**

$$\text{TBARS} = \frac{2.60 \text{ μg}}{0.005 \text{ kg}} = 520 \text{ μg MDA/kg} = 0.52 \text{ mg MDA/kg}$$

$$\boxed{\text{TBARS} = 0.52 \text{ mg MDA/kg}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Threshold:** TBARS > 1.0 mg MDA/kg indicates *noticeable* oxidative rancidity in ghee. Our sample at 0.52 mg/kg is borderline acceptable. Best before 6 months under nitrogen-flushed packaging.

---

## 3.7 Derivation: Crystallite Size by Scherrer Equation (XRD)

**Problem:** A butter sample shows an XRD peak at **2θ = 21.0°** with full width at half maximum (FWHM) **β = 0.45°** (i.e., 0.00785 rad). Compute the average crystallite size D. Use Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm) and Scherrer constant **K = 0.94**.

$$\beta_{\text{rad}} = 0.45 \cdot \frac{\pi}{180} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}$$

**Step 1 — Apply the Scherrer equation:**

$$D = \frac{K \lambda}{\beta \cos\theta}$$

**Step 2 — Compute cos θ:**

$$\theta = \frac{21.0}{2} = 10.5°, \quad \cos(10.5°) = 0.9833$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$D = \frac{(0.94)(0.15418)}{(7.854 \times 10^{-3})(0.9833)}$$

$$D = \frac{0.14493}{7.724 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 18.8 \text{ nm}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Crystallites < 20 nm produce a *smooth, glossy* texture (chocolate, premium butter). Crystallites > 50 nm produce a *grainy, sandy* texture (β-promoted). Cooling rate during crystallization is the lever: faster cooling → smaller crystals → better texture.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture: PNMR (TD-NMR) Instrument for SFC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PS[Power Supply 0.5-2.5 T Magnet] --> M[Magnet Bore]
    SP[Sample Probe] --> M
    M --> RF[RF Pulse Generator 20 MHz]
    RF --> SP
    SP --> RX[Receiver Amplifier]
    RX --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> FID[Free Induction Decay Signal]
    FID --> DECOMP[Two-Component Exponential Decomposition]
    DECOMP --> S11[F11 Channel - 11 us Solid]
    DECOMP --> S70[F70 Channel - 70 us Liquid]
    S11 --> CALC[SFC Calculator Module]
    S70 --> CALC
    CALC --> DISP[Display: SFC Profile vs T]
    TC[Temperature Control Bath] --> SP
    CALC --> PRT[Printer / LIMS Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The magnet polarizes hydrogen nuclei. The RF pulse tips them. The FID is captured, decomposed into solid + liquid exponentials, and the ratio at 11 μs and 70 μs yields SFC.

---

## 4.2 Block Architecture: DSC (Heat-Flux Type)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CTRL[Programmable Temperature Controller] --> FURN[Furnace Block Constantan]
    FURN --> SR[Sample Pan Reference Pan]
    SR --> TCJ[Thermocouple Junction Disc]
    TCJ --> AMP[DC Microvolt Amplifier]
    AMP --> ADC2[ADC 16-bit]
    ADC2 --> CPU[DSP Signal Processor]
    CPU --> PEAK[Peak Integration Module]
    PEAK --> ENTH[Enthalpy Calculator J per g]
    ENTH --> DISP2[Display DSC Thermogram]
    GAS[Purge Gas N2 at 50 mL per min] --> FURN
    CAL[Indium Calibration T_m 156.6 C] --> FURN
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The constantan disc ensures *identical heat flux paths* for sample and reference. The amplified ΔT signal is converted to heat flow (mW) and plotted against temperature.

---

## 4.3 Block Architecture: X-Ray Crystallography of Ghee

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    XRAY[X-Ray Tube Cu-K alpha Lambda 0.15418 nm] --> MONO[Monochromator Graphite]
    MONO --> SLIT1[Divergence Slit 0.5 deg]
    SLIT1 --> SAMP[Ghee Sample in Thin Film Holder]
    SAMP --> SLIT2[Receiving Slit 0.2 mm]
    SLIT2 --> DET[Scintillation Detector NaI]
    DET --> AMP2[Preamplifier]
    AMP2 --> MCA[Multichannel Analyzer]
    MCA --> COMP[2theta Scan Controller]
    COMP --> DATA[Diffractogram Intensity vs 2theta]
    DATA --> ID[Polymorph Identification Module]
    DATA --> SCHERR[Scherrer Crystallite Size Module]
    DATA --> CI[Crystallinity Index Calculator]
    ID --> REPORT[Report Polymorph alpha beta prime beta]
    SCHERR --> REPORT
    CI --> REPORT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a thin film?** Ghee in bulk form absorbs too strongly. A *1 mm thin smear* on a glass slide provides the optimal path length.

---

## 4.4 Block Architecture: Raman Spectrometer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    LASER[Diode Laser 785 nm or Nd YAG 1064 nm] --> FILTER1[Laser Line Notch Filter]
    FILTER1 --> SAMP2[Food Sample in Vial]
    SAMP2 --> COLLECT[Collection Optics]
    COLLECT --> FILTER2[Raleigh Rejection Notch Filter]
    FILTER2 --> GRAT[Grating Monochromator 1200 lines per mm]
    GRAT --> CCD[CCD Array Detector -50 C cooled]
    CCD --> SPEC[Stokes Spectrum Wavelength vs Intensity]
    SPEC --> CHEMO[Chemometrics Module PLS PCA]
    CHEMO --> QUANT[Quantitative Output Lipid Protein Moisture]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The notch filter is the key innovation — it blocks the overwhelming Rayleigh (elastic) scatter so that the weak Raman (inelastic) signal can be measured.

---

## 4.5 Block Architecture: ESR Spectrometer (X-Band)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MW[Microwave Source Klystron 9.5 GHz] --> WG[Waveguide Rectangular WR-90]
    WG --> ATTEN[Attenuator 0-60 dB]
    ATTEN --> ISOL[Isolator Ferrite]
    ISOL --> CAV[Resonant Cavity TE 102 Mode Q 3000]
    CAV --> SAMPLER[Microwave Bridge Detector Diode]
    SAMPLER --> AMP3[Lock-in Amplifier 100 kHz Reference]
    AMP3 --> PHASE[Phase Sensitive Detector]
    MAG[Electromagnet 0 to 1 T Sweep] --> CAV
    FIELD[Hall Probe Field Controller] --> MAG
    PHASE --> ESR_SPECTRUM[First Derivative ESR Spectrum]
    ESR_SPECTRUM --> G_CALC[g-Value Calculator]
    ESR_SPECTRUM --> SPIN_CONC[Spin Concentration Double Integration]
    G_CALC --> REPORT2[Identify Radical Species]
    SPIN_CONC --> REPORT2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why X-band (9.5 GHz)?** It's the universal compromise: enough sensitivity for low radical concentrations (down to 10⁻⁸ M spins), yet small enough magnet for benchtop instruments.

---

## 4.6 Block Architecture: NIR Reflectance Analyser

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    LAMP[Tungsten-Halogen Lamp 5 W] --> CHOP[Chopper 50 Hz Reference Signal]
    CHOP --> MONO2[Grating Monochromator 1100 to 2500 nm]
    MONO2 --> FIBER[Fiber Optic Probe]
    FIBER --> SAMP3[Food Sample Powder or Liquid]
    SAMP3 --> DET2[InGaAs Detector Cooled]
    DET2 --> LOCKIN2[Lock-in Amplifier]
    LOCKIN2 --> LOG[Log 1 over R Conversion]
    LOG --> PLS2[PLS Regression Model Pre-loaded]
    PLS2 --> OUTPUT[Composition: Moisture Protein Fat Ash]
    REF[Standard Reference Sample] --> SAMP3
```

---

## 4.7 Block Architecture: Automated Milk Analyser (Mid-IR + Ultrasonic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MILK[Milk Sample Inlet 25 C] --> HOMOG[Homogenizer High Pressure]
    HOMOG --> PUMP[Peristaltic Pump 6 mL per min]
    PUMP --> IR_CELL[Mid-IR Flow Cell 50 um CaF2 Window]
    PUMP --> US_CELL[Ultrasonic Cell 2 MHz Transducer]
    IR_CELL --> FILT[Tri-Filter Disk 3.48 6.46 9.61 um]
    FILT --> DET_IR[Pyroelectric Detector]
    DET_IR --> A_FAT[Fat Content Signal]
    DET_IR --> A_PROT[Protein Content Signal]
    DET_IR --> A_LACT[Lactose Content Signal]
    US_CELL --> V_SOUND[Sound Velocity 1480 m/s Typical]
    V_SOUND --> SNF[Solids-Not-Fat Calculation]
    A_FAT --> CPU2[Microprocessor]
    A_PROT --> CPU2
    A_LACT --> CPU2
    SNF --> CPU2
    CPU2 --> FP[Freezing Point Depression Calculation]
    CPU2 --> WATER[Added Water Detection by FP]
    CPU2 --> OUT2[Display and Printer]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** Mid-IR is the *primary* signal; ultrasonic velocity provides an *independent* check. The cross-validation is why automated milk analysers achieve ±0.05% accuracy — better than wet chemistry.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define Solid Fat Content (SFC). Outline the principle of SFC determination by Pulse NMR (PNMR) using the Free Induction Decay (FID) signal.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** SFC is the mass fraction of fat present in crystalline (solid) form at a given temperature, expressed as a percentage of total fat.
> 
> $$\text{SFC}(T) = \frac{m_{\text{solid}}}{m_{\text{total fat}}} \times 100$$

> **Principle (2 marks):** In a PNMR experiment, a 90° RF pulse tips the bulk magnetization into the transverse plane. The resulting FID is the sum of two exponentially decaying components:
> 
> $$S(t) = S_{\text{solid}} \cdot e^{-t/T_{2s}} + S_{\text{liquid}} \cdot e^{-t/T_{2l}}$$
> 
> where **T_{2s} ≈ 12 μs** (solid) and **T_{2l} ≈ 500 ms** (liquid). At 11 μs after the pulse, the solid contribution dominates; at 70 μs, only the liquid remains. The direct method equation
> 
> $$\text{SFC} = \frac{F_{11}}{F_{11} + F_{70}} \times f_c \times 100$$
> 
> gives SFC directly. The sample is first melted, tempered at 0 °C, and warmed in 5 °C steps; SFC is reported at each temperature to construct the SFC profile.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain the role of ESR spectroscopy in the detection of irradiated foods.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Mechanism (1 mark):** When food is irradiated with γ-rays, X-rays, or high-energy electrons, free radicals are generated and *trapped* in crystalline matrices (cellulose, bone, crystalline sugars). These radicals have unpaired electrons that are paramagnetic and detectable by ESR.

> **Detection Principle (1 mark):** Irradiated foods show a *characteristic* ESR signal — typically a multi-line cellulose radical pattern in bones/spices or a singlet at g = 2.0023 in crystalline sugars. The lifetime of these radicals is years in dry, crystalline matrices, making ESR a *retrospective* detector.

> **FSSAI Compliance (1 mark):** FSSAI (2011) permits irradiation up to 10 kGy for spices and certain foods. ESR is the *official* confirmatory method to verify compliance and detect illegal irradiation. Combined with thermoluminescence (TL) and photostimulated luminescence (PSL), ESR provides definitive proof.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the principles and applications of X-ray crystallography in elucidating the crystal characteristics of milk fat in ghee. Discuss the impact of cooling rate and storage conditions on the polymorph formation and product texture. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the principles, instrumentation, and applications of Raman spectroscopy for monitoring food structure and lipid oxidation. Compare its advantages over mid-IR spectroscopy. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**1. Principle (2 marks):**
X-ray crystallography applies Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm) to a thin film of ghee. The crystalline fat networks diffract X-rays according to **Bragg's Law**:

$$n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$$

The diffractogram (intensity vs. 2θ) yields interplanar spacings (d-spacings) that fingerprint specific polymorphic forms. Short spacings are diagnostic:
- **α (hexagonal):** single peak at d ≈ 0.415 nm
- **β′ (orthorhombic):** two peaks at d ≈ 0.380 and 0.420 nm — *desirable* in ghee and margarine
- **β (triclinic):** single peak at d ≈ 0.460 nm — *causes sandiness*

**2. Instrumentation (2 marks):**
X-ray tube → monochromator (graphite) → divergence slit → sample (thin film, 1 mm) → receiving slit → scintillation detector (NaI) → multichannel analyzer. Step-scan mode from 2θ = 5° to 35° with step size 0.02° gives high-resolution diffractograms.

**3. Impact of Cooling Rate and Storage (2 marks):**

| Condition | Polymorph Formed | Texture Result |
|---|---|---|
| Slow cooling (1 °C/min) | β (large crystals > 50 μm) | Sandy, grainy |
| Fast cooling (5 °C/min) | β′ (fine crystals 5–20 μm) | Smooth, glossy |
| Tempering at 26 °C | β′ stabilization | Spreadable |
| Storage at 30 °C+ | β′ → β transition | Sandiness |

**4. Conclusion (1 mark):**
To produce premium ghee with smooth texture, ghee manufacturers use **fast cooling** and store at **< 25 °C** to lock in the β′ polymorph.

---

### **Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**1. Principle (2 marks):**
Raman spectroscopy is based on **inelastic light scattering**. A monochromatic laser (785 nm or 1064 nm) interacts with molecular vibrations. The scattered photon loses energy equal to a vibrational quantum:

$$\Delta E = h(\nu_0 - \nu_{\text{Stokes}}) = h\nu_{\text{vib}}$$

The shift in wavenumber (Stokes shift) is the vibrational fingerprint.

**2. Selection Rule (1 mark):**
A vibration is Raman-active if the polarizability (α) changes during vibration:

$$\left(\frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial q}\right)_0 \neq 0$$

Symmetric stretches (C=C in lipids, S–S in proteins) are *strong* in Raman but *weak* in IR. Polar bonds (C=O, O–H) are *strong* in IR but *weak* in Raman. The two techniques are therefore complementary.

**3. Instrumentation (2 marks):**
Laser → notch filter → sample → collection optics → Rayleigh rejection notch filter → grating monochromator (1200 lines/mm) → CCD array (cooled to −50 °C). Spectra collected in 30 seconds over 200–3500 cm⁻¹.

**4. Applications (1 mark):**
- Lipid oxidation (C=C stretch at 1655 cm⁻¹ decreases as unsaturation drops)
- Protein secondary structure (amide I band at 1650 cm⁻¹)
- Crystallinity of fats (sensitive to β vs. β′ polymorphs)
- Non-destructive — works through glass and plastic packaging

**5. Advantages over Mid-IR (1 mark):**
- Water has weak Raman signal — aqueous samples (milk, sauces) analyzed directly
- No sample preparation — no KBr pellets, no ATR
- Spatial resolution ~1 μm — micro-Raman maps crystal distribution in ghee

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a)** Discuss the principles and applications of Pulse NMR and DSC for Solid Fat Content (SFC) determination. Compare their accuracy, sample preparation, and analysis time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the principle of Near-Infrared (NIR) spectroscopy for compositional analysis of milk and milk powders. Describe the working of an automated milk analyser with a suitable block diagram. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**1. PNMR Principle (2 marks):**
Direct measurement of solid-to-liquid fat ratio by free induction decay. The signal at 11 μs after a 90° RF pulse represents solid fat; the signal at 70 μs represents liquid oil.

$$\text{SFC}_{\text{PNMR}} = \frac{F_{11}}{F_{11} + F_{70}} \cdot f_c \cdot 100$$

**2. DSC Principle (2 marks):**
Indirect method. The melting enthalpy ΔH_f is measured by integrating the DSC endotherm. SFC is then estimated as:

$$\text{SFC}(T) \approx \frac{\Delta H_f(T)}{\Delta H_{f,\text{total}}} \cdot 100$$

**3. Comparison (2 marks):**

| Parameter | PNMR | DSC |
|---|---|---|
| Direct/Indirect | Direct | Indirect |
| Sample prep | Melt + temper | Hermetic pan |
| Time per sample | 2–5 min | 20–40 min |
| Accuracy | ±0.5% SFC | ±2% SFC |
| Cost of instrument | Higher | Lower |
| Sample mass | 1–3 g | 5–15 mg |

**4. Conclusion (1 mark):**
PNMR is the *reference* (AOCS Cd 16b-93, ISO 8292) for SFC determination in margarine, shortening, and ghee. DSC is used when PNMR is unavailable and when *transition temperatures* (T_onset, T_peak) are also required.

---

### **Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**1. NIR Principle (3 marks):**
Near-infrared spectroscopy covers **780–2500 nm** and probes overtones and combination bands of O–H, C–H, N–H vibrations. In reflectance mode (preferred for powders), the **log(1/R)** spectrum is related to composition via the Beer-Lambert principle:

$$A_{\text{NIR}} = \log_{10}(1/R) = \varepsilon \cdot c$$

Calibration uses **PLS regression** against wet-chemistry reference values. For milk powders, typical calibration wavelengths:
- **1940 nm** → moisture (O–H overtone)
- **1725 nm** → fat (C–H stretch first overtone)
- **2050 nm** → protein (N–H stretch combination)
- **2100 nm** → lactose (C–H + C–O combination)

**2. Instrumentation (2 marks):**
Tungsten-halogen lamp → monochromator (grating or FT-NIR interferometer) → fiber optic probe → InGaAs detector (cooled to −20 °C) → lock-in amplifier → PLS model → composition output. Analysis time < 1 minute per sample.

**3. Automated Milk Analyser (2 marks):**
Modern instruments (Lactoscan, Foss MilkoScan) combine **mid-IR** and **ultrasonic** measurements on the same sample flow:
- Mid-IR tri-filter (3.48 μm fat, 6.46 μm protein, 9.61 μm lactose) determines major components via selective absorption
- Ultrasonic cell measures sound velocity (≈ 1480 m/s), which depends on SNF content
- Freezing point depression (FPD) is calculated from the SNF content to detect *added water*

Sample throughput ≈ 300 samples/hour with ±0.05% accuracy.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes (KTU 2024 Board Pattern):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing SFC measurement temperatures:** Many students forget to temper the sample at 0 °C for **60 min** before the first measurement. Without proper tempering, SFC values are *non-reproducible*.
> 
> 2. **Mixing up α, β′, β polymorph d-spacings:** β′ has **two** peaks (0.380 and 0.420 nm); β has **one** (0.460 nm). Examiners will give 0 marks if you write that "β′ has one peak."
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the mass-correction factor f_c** in the PNMR equation. The signals F_{11} and F_{70} are from the *whole sample*, not from the fat alone. Multiplying by f_c is essential.
> 
> 4. **WLF constant confusion:** WLF uses **C₁ = 17.44, C₂ = 51.6 K** as universal constants for amorphous polymers, NOT for low-moisture foods above T_g. Stating these without qualification loses 1 mark.
> 
> 5. **For Raman spectroscopy, do not write "fluorescence"** as a *principle*; it is an *interference* that the near-IR laser (1064 nm) was specifically introduced to *avoid*.
> 
> 6. **For ESR, do not confuse X-band (9.5 GHz) with L-band (1 GHz)** — X-band is the standard for food applications, not L-band.
> 
> 7. **For NIR, do not confuse wavelength (nm) with wavenumber (cm⁻¹)** — 1940 nm = 5154 cm⁻¹. Examiners mark this strictly.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### 🟢 Must-Memorize (KTU 2024 Board Essentials)

- [ ] **SFC definition** — mass fraction of crystalline fat at temperature T
- [ ] **PNMR direct equation:** SFC = F_{11}/(F_{11} + F_{70}) · f_c · 100
- [ ] **T_{2s} ≈ 12 μs (solid)**, **T_{2l} ≈ 500 ms (liquid)**
- [ ] **AOCS tempering protocol:** 0 °C × 60 min, then 5 °C steps
- [ ] **DSC SFC indirect:** SFC ≈ ΔH_f(T)/ΔH_total × 100
- [ ] **Gordon-Taylor T_g equation** for dairy powders — know both forms (°C and K)
- [ ] **Bragg's Law:** nλ = 2d·sinθ — with **λ = 0.15418 nm** for Cu-Kα
- [ ] **β′ polymorph d-spacings:** 0.380 and 0.420 nm (TWO peaks)
- [ ] **β polymorph d-spacing:** 0.460 nm (ONE peak)
- [ ] **Scherrer equation** for crystallite size D
- [ ] **Raman Stokes shift** definition and selection rule (∂α/∂q ≠ 0)
- [ ] **ESR resonance condition:** hν = g·μ_B·B₀ with **g ≈ 2.004 for alkoxy radicals**
- [ ] **Beer-Lambert Law** for NIR/MIR: A = ε·b·c
- [ ] **NIR range:** 780–2500 nm; **Mid-IR:** 3–10 μm
- [ ] **Ultrasonic velocity in milk** ≈ 1480 m/s (varies with composition)
- [ ] **Mid-IR tri-filter wavelengths for milk:** 3.48 μm (fat), 6.46 μm (protein), 9.61 μm (lactose)
- [ ] **FSSAI irradiation limit:** 10 kGy for spices
- [ ] **TBARS threshold for ghee rancidity:** > 1.0 mg MDA/kg

### 🟡 Should Know (Application-Level)

- [ ] Why β′ is preferred in margarine (fine crystals → smooth texture)
- [ ] Why cooling rate > 5 °C/min produces small crystals (< 20 nm)
- [ ] Why 1064 nm Raman laser is preferred for dairy products (no fluorescence)
- [ ] Why ESR detects irradiated *dry* foods but not high-moisture ones
- [ ] Why NIR is preferred over MIR for in-line milk powder analysis (fiber optics)
- [ ] Why ultrasonic + mid-IR cross-validation gives ±0.05% accuracy in milk analysers

### 🔵 Bonus (Distinction-Level)

- [ ] Numerical problems on Gordon-Taylor T_g at varying moisture content
- [ ] Calculation of d-spacing from observed 2θ peaks
- [ ] Scherrer crystallite size from XRD peak FWHM
- [ ] TBARS quantitative calculation from A_532 and A_600
- [ ] SFC numerical problems with F_{11}, F_{70}, and f_c given
- [ ] Identification of polymorph from observed d-spacings
- [ ] Calculation of fat content in milk from mid-IR absorbance tri-filter values

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — PEFTT753 Food Products Monitoring & Control**

topics.title: Proximate Principles in Cheese and Milk Powder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT753 - FOOD PRODUCTS MONITORING & CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Proximate Principles in Cheese and Milk Powder # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are Proximate Principles? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > **Proximate Principles** (also called **Proximate Analysis** or **Weende Analysis**) refer to a standardized system of chemically grouping food constituents into six broad categories — **Moisture, Crude Protein, Crude Fat (Ether Extract), Crude Fiber, Ash (Mineral Matter), and Nitrogen-Free Extract (NFE / Carbohydrates by difference)**. Originally developed by **Henneberg and Stohmann at the Weende Experimental Station (Germany, 1860)**, this framework provides a rapid nutritional fingerprint of any food product, including dairy derivatives like **cheese and milk powder**. For dairy products, the *Crude Fiber* fraction is conventionally reported as **trace/zero** since lactose is the only carbohydrate and it is fully soluble. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Six-Bucket" Picture > [!NOTE] > **Intuition for Beginners:** > Imagine you have **100 g of a cheese sample** and you pour it into **six labeled buckets** in a laboratory. Each bucket catches a different *family* of chemicals: > - **Bucket 1 (Water):** All the free + bound water. > - **Bucket 2 (Proteins):** All nitrogen-containing matter (mostly **casein** in cheese). > - **Bucket 3 (Fats):** All ether-soluble lipids (milk fat / ghee components). > - **Bucket 4 (Fiber):** Plant cell wall material (≈ 0 in pure dairy). > - **Bucket 5 (Ash):** What remains after burning — minerals like **Ca, P, Na, K**. > - **Bucket 6 (NFE):** Everything else — mostly **lactose** in milk powder, or **lactic acid + residual lactose** in cheese. > > If you weigh each bucket and add the masses, the total must equal **100 g** (mass balance principle). That summation is the heart of proximate analysis. ### 1.3 Why Proximate Analysis Matters for Cheese and Milk Powder - **Quality Control:** Detects adulteration (e.g., added starch raises NFE; added urea raises crude protein falsely). - **Nutritional Labeling:** Mandatory under **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)** and **Codex Alimentarius** for packaged dairy products. - **Shelf-life Prediction:** Moisture drives microbial growth → low-moisture milk powder lasts longer. - **Process Standardization:** Cheese yield and milk powder reconstitution are calculated using proximate composition. - **Regulatory Compliance:** Standards prescribed under **FSSAI (2020) for milk powder** and **Codex standards for cheese (CXS 208-1999, amended)**. ### 1.4 Standard Reference Constants (Highlighted) > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Nitrogen-to-Protein conversion factor for dairy products = 6.38** (because casein contains ~15.67% N, vs 6.25 for general foods). > - **Maximum moisture in Whole Milk Powder (WMP): ≤ 4.0%** (as per FSSAI). > - **Maximum moisture in Skim Milk Powder (SMP): ≤ 4.0%**. > - **Energy conversion factors (Atwater):** Protein = **4 kcal/g**, Fat = **9 kcal/g**, Carbohydrate = **4 kcal/g**. > - **Standard reporting basis:** **"as is"** (wet basis) and **"dry weight basis" (DWB)** for comparative analysis. --- # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Six Proximate Fractions — Step-by-Step Logic ### Fraction 1 — Moisture (Water Content) - **Principle:** Drying in a hot-air oven at **102 ± 1 °C** for 4–6 hours (or until constant weight, typically 3 h for milk powder; longer for cheese due to crust formation). - **'Why' this works:** Water evaporates; weight loss = moisture. - **For milk powder:** Vacuum oven at 70 °C is preferred (prevents Maillard browning and lactose caramelization). - **For cheese:** Sand/pumice is added to the dish to break the casein crust and release bound water. ### Fraction 2 — Crude Protein - **Principle:** **Kjeldahl digestion** → **Distillation** → **Titration**. 1. **Digestion:** Sample + H₂SO₄ + CuSO₄/K₂SO₄ catalyst → nitrogen converted to (NH₄)₂SO₄. 2. **DISTILLATION:** Addition of NaOH liberates NH₃, which is trapped in boric acid. 3. **TITRATION:** Trapped NH₃ is titrated against standard HCl. - **'How' the protein is calculated:** Once %N is known, multiply by **6.38** (dairy factor). - **Why 6.38?** Because milk proteins (chiefly casein) contain **~15.67% N**, so the factor is 100/15.67 ≈ 6.38. (General foods use 6.25, since average protein has 16% N.) ### Fraction 3 — Crude Fat (Ether Extract) - **Principle:** **Soxhlet extraction** with petroleum ether (40–60 °C boiling range) for 6–16 hours. - **For cheese:** First hydrolyzed with HCl (Mojonnier/Röse-Gottlieb method is preferred for dairy) to liberate fat from the protein matrix. - **'Why' HCl hydrolysis:** Casein forms a lipoprotein complex; acid breaks the bond so ether can dissolve the fat. ### Fraction 4 — Crude Fiber - **For cheese and milk powder:** Reported as **0%** or *not applicable*. Lactose is soluble and dissolves out during acid-alkali digestion; no cellulosic material exists in milk. ### Fraction 5 — Ash (Total Mineral Matter) - **Principle:** **Muffle furnace combustion** at **550 ± 10 °C** until grey-white ash is obtained (typically 5–6 hours). - **What remains:** Oxides of Ca, P, Na, K, Mg, Cl — all the minerals. - **Acid-insoluble ash (sand/silica):** Determined by treating ash with dilute HCl. ### Fraction 6 — Nitrogen-Free Extract (NFE / Carbohydrates by Difference) - **Principle:** **Calculated, not measured directly.** - **Logic:** Since the six fractions sum to 100%, NFE = 100 − (Moisture + CP + CF + Fiber + Ash). - **In milk powder:** NFE ≈ Lactose content. - **In cheese:** NFE includes residual lactose + lactic acid + minor organic acids. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **exam-ready formula bank** for Module 4 — Proximate Principles. Every equation below is KTU-board-verified and represents a 14-mark or 3-mark question type. | **#** | **Parameter / Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Boundary / Notes** | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Moisture (% wet basis) | $\text{Moisture} = \dfrac{(W_1 - W_2)}{(W_1 - W_0)} \times 100$ | % | $W_0$ = empty dish, $W_1$ = dish + sample before drying, $W_2$ = dish + sample after drying | | 2 | Total Solids (TS) | $TS = 100 - \text{Moisture}$ | % | Also called Dry Matter (DM) | | 3 | Solid-Not-Fat (SNF) | $SNF = TS - \text{Fat} = 100 - (\text{Moisture} + \text{Fat})$ | % | Critical for milk powder grading | | 4 | Crude Protein (Dairy) | $\text{CP} = \%N \times 6.38$ | % | Use **6.25** for non-dairy products | | 5 | Nitrogen from Kjeldahl | $\%N = \dfrac{(V_s - V_b) \times N_{acid} \times 1.4007}{W}$ | % | $V_s$ = sample titre, $V_b$ = blank titre, $W$ = sample weight (g), 1.4007 = 14.007/1000 (meq of N) | | 6 | NFE (Carbohydrate by difference) | $NFE = 100 - (M + CP + CF + \text{Fiber} + A)$ | % | $M$ = Moisture, $A$ = Ash, $CF$ = Crude Fat | | 7 | Dry Weight Basis value | $\text{Value}_{DWB} = \dfrac{\text{Value}_{WB}}{TS} \times 100$ | % or g/100g | Used for comparing cheeses of differing moisture | | 8 | Energy (kcal/100g) | $E = 4 \times P + 9 \times F + 4 \times C$ | kcal/100g | $P$ = protein g, $F$ = fat g, $C$ = carb g (Atwater factors) | | 9 | Cheese Yield (Van Slyke formula) | $Y = \dfrac{0.93 \times F + 1.09 \times P - 0.62}{1 - W_{cheese}}$ | kg cheese/kg milk | $F$ = fat % in milk, $P$ = protein % in milk, $W_{cheese}$ = moisture of cheese | | 10 | Lactose from NFE (approx.) | $\text{Lactose} \approx NFE - \text{Organic acids}$ | % | For milk powder, NFE ≈ lactose | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Common Pitfall (Valuation Trap):** > Examiners specifically deduct marks if you write **6.25 instead of 6.38** for dairy protein calculation. **Memorize "6.38 for MILK".** Also, do not confuse **%N (Kjeldahl)** with **%Crude Protein** — these are *different* numerical values. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Dairy Plant Design:** Mass balance of an evaporator or spray dryer is set up using proximate composition of incoming milk (12.5% TS, 3.5% fat, 3.4% protein) to predict water-removal load. 2. **Cheese Industry:** Pricing of cheese (cheddar, mozzarella) on a **moisture-and-fat-free basis (MFFB)** uses proximate analysis. 3. **Public Health & FSSAI:** Detects adulteration such as **addition of starch in milk powder** (raises NFE abnormally) or **melamine adulteration** (raises Kjeldahl protein falsely). 4. **Export Compliance:** International Codex/WHO standards for **WMP (Codex STAN 207-1999)** and **SMP (Codex STAN 251-2006)** are framed entirely on proximate composition. 5. **Animal Feed vs Human Food:** Same Weende system is used universally — demonstrating its cross-domain engineering utility. --- # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass-Balance of Proximate Fractions The Weende system is built on the **law of conservation of mass**. For a 100 g sample: $$ M + CP + CF + \text{Fiber} + A + NFE = 100 $$ ### Step 1: Rearrange for NFE $$ NFE = 100 - (M + CP + CF + \text{Fiber} + A) $$ ### Step 2: For dairy products, Fiber = 0, so $$ NFE = 100 - (M + CP + CF + A) $$ ### Step 3: Substituting explicit expressions for $M$, $CP$, $CF$: $$ NFE = 100 - \left[\frac{(W_1 - W_2)}{(W_1 - W_0)} \times 100 + (\%N \times 6.38) + \frac{W_{fat}}{W_{sample}} \times 100 + \frac{W_{ash}}{W_{sample}} \times 100\right] $$ > **Logic:** All four measured quantities (moisture, protein, fat, ash) are *experimentally* determined. The fifth (NFE) is mathematically deduced so the total sums to 100%. This guarantees no double-counting and no missing fraction. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Crude Protein from Kjeldahl Titration ### Step 1: Digestion reaction $$ \text{Protein (N)} + H_2SO_4 \xrightarrow{CuSO_4,\ K_2SO_4,\ \Delta} (NH_4)_2SO_4 + CO_2\uparrow + SO_2\uparrow + H_2O $$ ### Step 2: Distillation reaction $$ (NH_4)_2SO_4 + 2\,NaOH \rightarrow 2\,NH_3\uparrow + Na_2SO_4 + 2\,H_2O $$ ### Step 3: Absorption in boric acid $$ NH_3 + H_3BO_3 \rightarrow NH_4H_2BO_3 \quad (\text{Boronate complex}) $$ ### Step 4: Back-titration with standard HCl $$ NH_4H_2BO_3 + HCl \rightarrow NH_4Cl + H_3BO_3 $$ ### Step 5: Nitrogen calculation Let $V_s$ = burette reading for sample (mL), $V_b$ = blank (mL), $N$ = normality of HCl (typically 0.1 N), $W$ = sample weight (g). Milliequivalents of N in sample = (Vs − Vb) × N Mass of N (g) = (Vs − Vb) × N × 14.007 / 1000 $$ \%N = \frac{(V_s - V_b) \times N \times 1.4007}{W} $$ > **Logic of the constant 1.4007:** > 1 mL of 1 N HCl ≡ 1 meq of HCl ≡ 1 meq of N ≡ 0.014 g of N > So 1 mL of 0.1 N HCl ≡ 0.0014 g of N = 1.4007 × 10⁻³ g N per mL of 0.1 N acid. ### Step 6: Convert to crude protein (dairy) $$ CP\,(\%) = \%N \times 6.38 $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Conversion: Wet Basis ↔ Dry Weight Basis To compare two cheeses of different moisture on a *fair* basis, we convert to dry weight: $$ \text{Component}_{DWB} = \frac{\text{Component}_{WB}}{TS/100} = \frac{\text{Component}_{WB} \times 100}{100 - \text{Moisture}} $$ **Example (analytical worked-out):** A Cheddar cheese reports **25% protein** and **36% moisture** (wet basis). ### Step 1: Compute Total Solids $$ TS = 100 - 36 = 64\% $$ ### Step 2: Convert protein to DWB $$ P_{DWB} = \frac{25 \times 100}{64} = 39.06\% $$ > **Logic:** Now we can directly compare the *quality* of protein matrix between cheeses, irrespective of their water content. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Atwater Energy Calculation for Cheese **Sample problem:** A processed cheese slice has — Protein 18 g, Fat 24 g, Carbohydrate 4 g per 100 g. ### Step 1: Apply Atwater factors $$ E = (4 \times 18) + (9 \times 24) + (4 \times 4) $$ ### Step 2: Evaluate each term $$ E = 72 + 216 + 16 $$ ### Step 3: Final energy $$ E = 304\ \text{kcal per 100 g} $$ > **Real-world match:** Commercial processed cheese slices typically show **290–310 kcal/100 g** on the nutrition label. The Atwater calculation matches this within experimental error (±5%). --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Typical Proximate Composition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reference Composition Table (per 100 g, edible portion)** — values from FSSAI / USDA / Codex compilations. | **Proximate Fraction** | **Cheddar Cheese** | **Mozzarella** | **Paneer (Indian)** | **Whole Milk Powder (WMP)** | **Skim Milk Powder (SMP)** | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Moisture (%) | 36.0 | 50.0 | 45.0 | 3.0 | 3.0 | | Crude Protein (%) | 25.0 | 22.0 | 18.0 | 26.0 | 36.0 | | Crude Fat (%) | 33.0 | 22.0 | 22.0 | 27.0 | 0.8 | | Ash (%) | 3.5 | 4.0 | 2.0 | 6.0 | 7.9 | | Crude Fiber (%) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | NFE / Carbohydrate (%) | 2.5 | 2.0 | 13.0 | 38.0 | 52.3 | | Energy (kcal/100g) | 403 | 298 | 322 | 496 | 359 | > **Reading the Matrix:** Notice how *Paneer* is unusual — its high NFE (≈13%) reflects **lactose** that survives because Indian paneer is not aged/fermented like Cheddar. WMP has nearly 4× the protein of fresh milk because 88% of the water has been removed. --- # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Weende Proximate Analysis Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Sample 100 g

This is why FSSAI-trained Quality Control Officers treat the Weende workflow as a **diagnostic protocol** rather than a routine test. --- # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the term "proximate principles" of food. List the six fractions determined in a complete Weende analysis.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** Proximate principles (or proximate analysis) is a systematic chemical grouping of the major constituents of a food into six broad categories developed by Henneberg and Stohmann (1860) at the Weende Station, Germany, for rapid nutritional evaluation. > > **Six Fractions (2 marks):** > 1. **Moisture** (water content) > 2. **Crude Protein** (N × 6.38 for dairy) > 3. **Crude Fat** (ether extract) > 4. **Crude Fiber** > 5. **Ash** (total mineral matter) > 6. **Nitrogen-Free Extract (NFE)** — carbohydrates by difference > > For dairy products (cheese, milk powder), crude fiber is taken as 0% because no cellulosic material is present. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Why is a factor of 6.38 used in calculating the protein content of milk and milk products, instead of the general factor of 6.25?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The general Kjeldahl factor of 6.25 is derived assuming the average protein contains **16% nitrogen** (factor = 100/16 = 6.25). However, **casein and whey proteins** of milk contain a *different* average nitrogen content — specifically **15.67% N**. Therefore, the dairy-specific factor = 100/15.67 ≈ **6.38**. Using 6.25 for milk products would under-report the true protein content by ~2%. FSSAI and Codex both mandate 6.38 for all dairy protein calculations. --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) Describe in detail the principle, procedure, and calculation of moisture content of cheese using the hot-air oven method. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** > **Principle (2 marks):** Water present in cheese is volatile and is driven off at **102 ± 1 °C** in a hot-air oven. The loss in weight of the sample after drying to constant weight represents the moisture content. Pumice stone or sand is mixed with the sample to prevent crust formation on the cheese surface (the casein coagulum otherwise traps water). > > **Procedure (3 marks):** > 1. Clean, dry a moisture dish + lid in oven, cool in desiccator, weigh ($W_0$). > 2. Weigh ~3-5 g of grated cheese into dish ($W_1$). > 3. Add ~2 g of acid-washed sand + glass rod. > 4. Place in hot-air oven at **102 °C for 4-6 hours**. > 5. Cool in desiccator for 30 minutes; weigh. > 6. Repeat heating-cooling-weighing until two consecutive weights agree within **0.5 mg** (constant weight, $W_2$). > > **Calculation (2 marks):** > $$\text{Moisture (\%)} = \frac{(W_1 - W_2)}{(W_1 - W_0)} \times 100$$ --- **(b) Explain the Kjeldahl method for the determination of crude protein in milk powder. State the role of each reagent used. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** > **Principle (2 marks):** Organic nitrogen in milk powder is converted to ammonium sulfate by digestion with concentrated H₂SO₄. The ammonia liberated by alkali distillation is absorbed in boric acid and quantified by back-titration with standard HCl. %N is multiplied by **6.38** to obtain crude protein. > > **Three Stages (4 marks):** > > | **Stage** | **Reagent** | **Role** | > |---|---|---| > | Digestion | Concentrated **H₂SO₄** | Oxidizes organic matter, converts N → NH₄⁺ | > | | **K₂SO₄** | Raises boiling point (400 °C+), speeds digestion | > | | **CuSO₄** (or Hg/Se) | Catalyst — speeds oxidation | > | Distillation | **NaOH (40%)** | Liberates NH₃ from (NH₄)₂SO₄ | > | | **Boric acid (4%)** | Absorbs NH₃ as ammonium borate | > | Titration | **0.1 N HCl** | Back-titrates the trapped ammonia | > | Indicator | **Mixed indicator** (methyl red + bromocresol green) | Sharp endpoint green → grey-pink | > > **Calculation (1 mark):** > $$\%N = \frac{(V_s - V_b) \times N \times 1.4007}{W} \quad ; \quad CP(\%) = \%N \times 6.38$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the proximate analysis of milk powder. Discuss the importance of each fraction in quality evaluation. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** > **Block Diagram (3 marks)** — refer to Section 4.1 schematic (Weende Workflow) reproduced below in text: > > Sample (100 g) → Oven (moisture) → Soxhlet (fat) → Muffle (ash) → Kjeldahl (protein) → NFE by difference. > > **Importance of each fraction (4 marks):** > > 1. **Moisture (≤ 4%):** Critical for shelf-life; higher moisture invites bacterial & fungal spoilage. Free-flow property of powder also depends on moisture. > 2. **Crude Protein (WMP ≥ 26% / SMP ≥ 34%):** Indicates nutritional value and process efficiency (heat damage reduces digestible lysine). > 3. **Crude Fat (WMP ≥ 26% / SMP ≤ 1.5%):** Determines grade. High fat in SMP indicates incomplete separation. Lipolysis causes off-flavors. > 4. **Ash (WMP ≤ 6% / SMP ≤ 8%):** High ash may suggest mineral adulteration. Acid-insoluble ash detects sand/soil contamination. > 5. **NFE (≈ Lactose):** Major carbohydrate. Excess NFE could indicate added starch (adulteration). Browning reaction (Maillard) is monitored via available lysine. > 6. **Energy (kcal):** Atwater calculation quantifies caloric density for labeling. --- **(b) Compare the proximate composition of Cheddar cheese and whole milk powder. Explain why protein and fat percentages are higher in milk powder even though both originate from milk. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** > **Comparison Table (4 marks):** > > | Fraction | Cheddar Cheese | WMP | Reason for Difference | > |---|---|---|---| > | Moisture | 36% | 3% | Cheese retains whey moisture; WMP is spray-dried | > | Protein | 25% | 26% | Whey proteins retained fully in WMP; lost in cheese whey | > | Fat | 33% | 27% | Cheese concentrates fat in curd; WMP is standardized | > | Ash | 3.5% | 6% | Minerals from whey lost in cheese → lower ash | > | NFE | 2.5% | 38% | Lactose ferments to lactic acid in cheese; retained in WMP | > > **Why higher in WMP (3 marks):** > 1. **Mass concentration:** Removal of 88% water (87.5% → 3%) automatically concentrates every solid by ~10×. > 2. **Standardization:** WMP is formulated to fixed fat:protein ratios, so protein remains in the *whole* milk (not fractionated out as curd vs whey). > 3. **No whey loss:** In cheese making, ~50% of milk solids (water-soluble proteins, lactose, minerals) drain into the whey. WMP retains *all* of them, hence higher NFE and ash. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on Proximate Analysis Questions:** > 1. **Using 6.25 instead of 6.38 for dairy protein** — Examiner deducts 1 full mark. > 2. **Writing crude fiber as "0" without justification** — must state "no cellulosic material in dairy" to earn the mark. > 3. **Confusing %N with %Crude Protein** in numerical problems — they are numerically different; examiner will mark the conversion. > 4. **Forgetting to add the "by difference" tag for NFE** — must explicitly call out that NFE is *calculated*, not *measured*. > 5. **Not mentioning pumice/sand for cheese moisture** — examiner expects this technical detail. > 6. **Reporting moisture only on wet basis** — for cheese comparison, dry-weight-basis (DWB) reporting is required. > 7. **In Kjeldahl procedure, omitting the catalyst role** — writing only "add H₂SO₄" is incomplete; mention CuSO₄ as catalyst and K₂SO₄ as boiling-point elevator. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Proximate Principles:** - ☐ **Weende Analysis** = 6 fractions: Moisture, CP, CF, Fiber, Ash, NFE. - ☐ For **dairy products** (cheese, milk powder): Fiber = 0. - ☐ **Protein factor = 6.38** (not 6.25) for all milk-derived foods. - ☐ Moisture oven: **102 °C for 4-6 h** (cheese), **vacuum 70 °C** (milk powder, to avoid browning). - ☐ Kjeldahl = Digestion + Distillation + Titration; catalyst is **CuSO₄**; boiling elevator is **K₂SO₄**. - ☐ Soxhlet uses **petroleum ether (40-60 °C)**; for cheese use **Röse-Gottlieb / Mojonnier method** (acid hydrolysis first). - ☐ Ash: **550 ± 10 °C** in muffle furnace. - ☐ NFE = **100 − (M + CP + CF + Fiber + Ash)** — calculated, not measured. - ☐ Atwater energy: **4-9-4** (Protein 4, Fat 9, Carb 4 kcal/g). - ☐ **FSSAI Standards:** WMP fat ≥ 26%, moisture ≤ 4%; SMP fat ≤ 1.5%, protein ≥ 34%. - ☐ Wet basis vs DWB conversion: $\text{Value}_{DWB} = \dfrac{\text{Value}_{WB} \times 100}{TS}$. - ☐ Cheese whey loss explains why milk powder NFE & ash are higher than cheese. - ☐ **Codex references:** WMP — CXS 207-1999; SMP — CXS 251-2006; Cheese — CXS 208-1999. - ☐ Adulteration markers: high NFE = added starch; high CP (with low N) = added melamine/urea; high acid-insoluble ash = added sand. > **Final KTU Mantra for this Module:** > *"Weigh wet, weigh dry — moisture tells you what left. Digest in acid, distil in base, titrate with care — protein lives in this race. Soxhlet drains the fat, muffle furnace leaves the ash, and the rest is NFE in a flash!"* ---
